Existing chatbot-centric red-teaming benchmarks mainly standardize jailbreak and content-safety evaluation, but they do not systematically capture physically grounded functional failures or cover red-teaming methods that target physical-world VLMs. This raises the key challenge of comparing diverse attack methods under a unified protocol while targeting the same scenario-specific failures. We introduce REALM, to our knowledge the first unified red-teaming benchmark for physical-world VLMs. REALM integrates 12 red-teaming methods, 3 model-agnostic defenses, and 13 VLMs under a practical black-box threat model with shared datasets and metrics. To align adversarial objectives across attack families, REALM introduces an agentic target-generation pipeline that constructs shared, scenario-specific, and physically grounded attack objectives for each scene, enabling fair comparison of diverse red-teaming methods under aligned adversarial goals. Our evaluation shows that text and typographic injection attacks induce the most failures, multimodal co-optimization yields the strongest visual-perturbation transfer, single-pass attacks approach iterative methods at much lower cost, and model scale alone does not confer adversarial robustness. Code is available at https://github.com/UCF-ML-Research/REALM.

AI.tldr: REALM is the first unified red-teaming benchmark for physical-world VLMs, integrating 12 attack methods, 3 defenses, and 13 models under a standardized black-box threat model. It introduces agentic target-generation for fair comparison and finds text/typographic injection attacks most effective, multimodal co-optimization best for visual transfer, and that model scale alone doesn't ensure robustness.
AI.motivation: Existing red-teaming evaluations for VLMs are fragmented across datasets, metrics, and threat models, making direct comparison of attack methods impossible. Current benchmarks are chatbot-centric and fail to capture physically grounded functional failures in safety-critical embodied systems.
AI.method: REALM integrates 12 red-teaming methods, 3 model-agnostic defenses, and 13 VLMs under a unified black-box threat model. It introduces an agentic target-generation pipeline to construct shared, scenario-specific, physically grounded attack objectives across diverse attack families, enabling fair comparison.
AI.result: Text and typographic injection attacks induce the most failures; multimodal co-optimization yields the strongest visual-perturbation transfer; single-pass attacks approach iterative methods at much lower cost; model scale alone does not confer adversarial robustness.
AI.conclusion: REALM provides a standardized benchmark for systematically comparing diverse red-teaming methods on physical-world VLMs, revealing key findings about attack efficacy and model vulnerability that can inform future safety research.

title: Ensemble Feature Selection and Harris Hawks Optimization for Explainable Mental Health Risk Prediction in Female Sex Workers
comment: Accepted and presented at the 2026 8th IEEE Symposium on Computers & Informatics (ISCI 2026). To appear in IEEE conference proceedings
summary: One of the significant mental health issues affecting female sex workers (FSWs) is mental disorders, especially depression. Exposure to violence, stigma, and economic hardship further increases their psychological risk. Current machine learning (ML) models are typically ineffective at capturing the high-dimensional and complex risk patterns that exist in this marginalized group. This paper suggests a hybrid predictive model that merges an ensemble feature selection strategy using ANOVA and mutual information and Harris Hawks optimization-tuned logistic regression and represents a new application of swarm intelligence to predict mental health in vulnerable groups. The explainable AI (XAI) methods can be used to understand the factors of trauma associated with model predictions. When applied to a group of 3,005 FSWs, it can be seen that the proposed model is more effective than traditional classifiers, with an accuracy of 95.78%, an F1 score of 95.77%, and an AUC of 0.96, and identifying post-traumatic stress, client-related violence, and occupational factors as major contributors to depression. This work bridges the gaps between conventional and ML approaches to develop an XAI tool that enables vulnerable groups to receive early assistance, evidence-based targeted psychosocial care, and health planning.

AI.tldr: A hybrid ML model combining ANOVA-MI feature selection and Harris Hawks Optimization-tuned logistic regression achieves 95.78% accuracy in predicting depression among 3,005 female sex workers, with XAI identifying PTSD, client violence, and occupational factors as key contributors.
AI.motivation: Female sex workers face high depression risk due to violence, stigma, and economic hardship, but current ML models fail to capture complex risk patterns in this marginalized group.
AI.method: Hybrid model using ensemble feature selection (ANOVA + mutual information) + Harris Hawks Optimization-tuned logistic regression + explainable AI (XAI) for interpretability.
AI.result: Achieved 95.78% accuracy, 95.77% F1 score, 0.96 AUC on 3,005 FSWs, outperforming traditional classifiers. Key predictors: PTSD, client violence, occupational factors.
AI.conclusion: The XAI tool bridges conventional and ML approaches, enabling early assistance and targeted psychosocial care for vulnerable groups.

title: Grounding Generative Policies in Physics: Optimization-Guided Diffusion for Robot Control
summary:
Diffusion models sample effectively from high-dimensional, multimodal distributions, but their outputs may violate deployment constraints. For task-space robot policies, generated grasps, waypoints, or trajectories can be distributionally valid yet infeasible, violating reachability, collision-avoidance, or closed-loop executability requirements. This embodiment gap limits zero-shot deployment across robots, even when the task-space behavior itself is transferable.
  We propose an inference-time optimization framework that couples the behavior generation to physical feasibility by formulating diffusion guidance as a constrained optimization problem. Our key insight is to replace the sampling perturbation in the backward process with an optimized correction, allowing hard constraints or soft penalties to be imposed during sampling without the need to retrain the diffusion model, while keeping samples close to the learned prior.
  We evaluate the method on dexterous grasp synthesis with reachability and collision-avoidance constraints, and dynamic manipulation with controller-level trackability constraints. Across settings and robot embodiments, optimization-guided denoising matches the feasibility of projection- and gradient-guidance baselines while better preserving grasp quality, and improving controller-level executability and task success, with task success improving by up to 20pp. on dexterous grasping and 23pp. on visuomotor manipulation over the best baseline.

AI.tldr: This paper proposes an inference-time optimization framework that guides diffusion model sampling using constrained optimization to enforce physical feasibility (reachability, collision-avoidance, executability) for robot behavior generation, improving task success by up to 23pp without retraining.
AI.motivation: Diffusion models can generate diverse, high-quality robot behaviors in task space, but these outputs often violate embodiment-specific constraints (e.g., reachability, collision avoidance, closed-loop executability), creating an "embodiment gap" that prevents zero-shot deployment across different robots.
AI.method: The authors replace the standard sampling perturbation in the backward diffusion process with an optimized correction derived from a constrained optimization formulation. This allows hard constraints or soft penalties to be imposed during sampling while keeping samples close to the learned prior, all without retraining the diffusion model.
AI.result: The method improves task success by up to 20pp on dexterous grasping and 23pp on visuomotor manipulation over the best baseline. It matches projection- and gradient-guidance baselines in feasibility while better preserving grasp quality and improving executability.
AI.conclusion: Optimization-guided denoising effectively bridges the embodiment gap, enabling task-space diffusion policies to generalize across different robot embodiments with improved physical feasibility and task success, all at inference time without model retraining.

title: Integrated Sensing and Communications for Real-time Avatar Control in XR over 5G
summary: Extended Reality (XR) presents a challenging use case for 5G and 6G networks, requiring high data-rates and lowlatency communication to deliver a truly immersive experience. Moreover, in order to seamlessly translate physical actions to the virtual world, accurate gesture recognition and pose estimation are required. Current XR interaction solutions based on handheld controllers and cameras cannot easily capture full-body poses, inhibit the free use of hands, and require good visibility and a clear line of sight. In this work, we propose a multimodal sensing architecture for XR that combines 5G MillimeterWave (mmWave) Integrated sensing and communication (ISAC) and surface electromyography (sEMG) signals. 5G mmWave ISAC cannot only be used to deliver content wirelessly to the Head-mounted display (HMD), but also the same communication signals can be used to derive coarse body-level gestures and poses of the user, to support real-time avatar control. For fine-grained finger-level gestures, our architecture leverages lightweight sEMG sensors that capture forearm muscle activity. To illustrate the need of both modalities, we present evaluations of both sensing technologies. At the body level (5G), our architecture relies on power-per-beam-pair (PPBP), which can be computed from standard beam management or beam sweeping procedures of the 5G NR standard. PPBP-based sensing achieves 82.2$\pm$5.9% average accuracy when evaluated on users not seen during training. For fine-grained finger-level interactions, we show that surface electromyography (sEMG) carries strong discriminative information achieving consistent promising performance across different movement settings. Thus, combining the two modalities enables multi-scale gesture recognition, at the body level via existing 5G signals and finger level via lightweight sEMG sensors, forming a complete XR framework.

AI.tldr: This paper proposes a multimodal sensing architecture for XR that combines 5G mmWave ISAC for coarse body-level gesture recognition and sEMG sensors for fine-grained finger-level gestures, enabling multi-scale gesture recognition without handheld controllers or cameras.
AI.motivation: Current XR interaction solutions rely on handheld controllers and cameras that cannot capture full-body poses, inhibit free hand use, and require clear line-of-sight. There is a need for a seamless, controller-free multimodal sensing approach for immersive XR experiences.
AI.method: The architecture combines two modalities: (1) 5G mmWave ISAC signals using PPBP from standard 5G NR beam management for coarse body-level gestures, and (2) lightweight sEMG sensors on forearm for fine-grained finger-level gestures.
AI.result: PPBP-based body-level sensing achieves 82.2±5.9% accuracy on unseen users. sEMG shows strong discriminative information for fine-grained finger interactions across different movement settings.
AI.conclusion: Combining 5G mmWave ISAC and sEMG enables multi-scale gesture recognition (body-level via 5G signals, finger-level via sEMG), forming a complete controller-free XR interaction framework.

title: Reconstructing GRACE Terrestrial Water Storage with Spatio-Temporal Graph Neural Networks: An Application to South America
summary: Terrestrial water storage (TWS) integrates snow, soil moisture, surface water, and groundwater and is a key indicator of how climate variability and human activity reshape the global water cycle. The GRACE and GRACE-FO satellite missions provide the only direct, globally consistent observations of TWS change, but their record only begins in 2002 which is too short for many climate-scale analyses. We present a deep learning application that reconstructs monthly GRACE-like TWS anomalies (TWSA) back to 1940 by learning the relationship between daily ERA5 meteorological forcing (precipitation, evapotranspiration, runoff) and monthly GRACE observations. In contrast to prior reconstruction approaches based on grid-cell-wise regression, CNNs, or LSTMs, we adapt a multi-variate time series graph neural network (MTGNN) architecture, which was originally developed for mobility and traffic forecasting on urban sensor networks to this satellite-geodesy task. Spatial dependencies are encoded in a static, interpretable hybrid adjacency matrix that combines geodesic proximity with lagged correlations of climatic time series, capturing both local hydrological coupling and large-scale teleconnections. The reconstruction achieves a grid-cell Pearson correlation of 0.69, a basin-mean correlation of 0.94, and a near-zero bias, and it reproduces the spatial fingerprints of the 2015/16 El Niño and 2020/21 La Niña events. A systematic comparison with established reconstruction approaches (GTWS-MLrec, RM-REC, GRAiCE) shows that the graph-based model is statistically competitive at basin scale, reaching a correlation within 0.025 of the best baseline while using only roughly half to a tenth of the predictors the other models require and revealing characteristic weaknesses in arid regions in all models. The complete implementation is publicly available at github.com/hcu-cml/MTGNN-TWS-Reconstruction-GRACE

AI.tldr: This paper presents a deep learning approach using a multi-variate time series graph neural network (MTGNN) to reconstruct GRACE-like terrestrial water storage anomalies back to 1940 from ERA5 meteorological data, achieving strong performance with fewer predictors than existing methods.
AI.motivation: The GRACE and GRACE-FO satellite missions only provide TWS data from 2002 onward, which is too short for climate-scale analyses. The authors aim to extend the TWS record back to 1940 using a reconstruction approach.
AI.method: The authors adapt a multi-variate time series graph neural network (MTGNN) originally developed for traffic forecasting. They use a hybrid adjacency matrix combining geodesic proximity and lagged correlations of climatic time series to encode spatial dependencies, and train the model on daily ERA5 meteorological forcing (precipitation, evapotranspiration, runoff) to predict monthly GRACE TWS anomalies.
AI.result: The reconstruction achieves a grid-cell Pearson correlation of 0.69, basin-mean correlation of 0.94, near-zero bias, and reproduces spatial patterns of El Niño/La Niña events. It is statistically competitive with established methods (GTWS-MLrec, RM-REC, GRAiCE) while using only half to a tenth of the predictors.
AI.conclusion: The graph-based model provides an efficient and effective way to reconstruct long-term TWS records, with particular advantages in predictor efficiency. The implementation is publicly available on GitHub.

title: The Professor: Multi-Teacher Unsupervised Prompt Distillation for Vision-Language Models
summary: Prompt distillation compresses large vision-language models (VLMs) such as CLIP into lightweight student models by matching teacher predictions on unlabeled domain images. PromptKD (CVPR 2024) established this paradigm with a single PromptSRC-finetuned ViT-L/14 teacher and a ViT-B/16 student. We propose TheProfessor, a multi-teacher extension that distills from a fixed two-teacher ensemble: a domain-finetuned PromptSRC ViT-L/14 teacher and a zero-shot EVA-CLIP-L/14 teacher whose logits are pre-computed per dataset. We evaluate single-teacher PromptKD, equal-probability ensembling, and confidence-weighted ensembling on four base-to-novel datasets: Caltech-101, DTD, UCF101, and EuroSAT. In a 12-run single-seed sweep, confidence-weighted ensembling improves average HM from 87.52 to 89.28 (+1.77 points), while equal averaging improves average HM to 88.88 (+1.37 points). Gains are dataset dependent: they are negligible on Caltech-101 (+0.16 HM for confidence weighting), modest on UCF101 (+0.62), and largest on domain-shifted EuroSAT (+5.78). These results update our earlier Caltech-only analysis and show that multi-teacher prompt distillation is most useful when the second teacher contributes complementary supervision under domain shift.

AI.tldr: TheProfessor extends PromptKD by distilling from two teachers (domain-finetuned + zero-shot) instead of one, achieving +1.77 HM improvement on average across four datasets, with largest gains on domain-shifted EuroSAT (+5.78).
AI.motivation: Single-teacher prompt distillation (PromptKD) may lack complementary supervision. The authors propose using a second, zero-shot teacher to provide additional knowledge that improves student generalization, especially under domain shift.
AI.method: Multi-teacher distillation with a fixed two-teacher ensemble: (1) a domain-finetuned PromptSRC ViT-L/14 teacher, and (2) a zero-shot EVA-CLIP-L/14 teacher with pre-computed logits. Two ensembling strategies tested: equal-probability averaging and confidence-weighted averaging.
AI.result: Confidence-weighted ensembling improves average HM from 87.52 to 89.28 (+1.77). Gains vary: negligible on Caltech-101 (+0.16), modest on UCF101 (+0.62), largest on EuroSAT (+5.78). Equal averaging yields +1.37 HM average.
AI.conclusion: Multi-teacher prompt distillation is most beneficial when the second teacher provides complementary supervision under domain shift; gains are dataset-dependent.

title: Beyond Trajectory Imitation: Strategy-Guided Policy Optimization for LLM Reasoning
summary: Distilling reasoning capabilities from strong to weak language models typically involves imitating specific solution trajectories, effectively transferring what to answer rather than how to reason. This trajectory-level imitation encourages memorization of instance-specific steps rather than acquisition of transferable problem-solving skills, limiting generalization to novel problems. We propose Strategy-Guided Policy Optimization (SGPO), which replaces instance-level trajectory imitation with reusable strategy distillation. SGPO extracts structured strategy descriptions from strong-model responses and, for each problem, constructs both autonomous and strategy-guided trajectories to enable direct comparison of the model's behavior with and without strategic guidance. The framework then addresses two key questions. For how to distill, a token-level forward-KL objective selectively transfers the distributional shift induced by strategy conditioning into the unguided policy, with proximal constraints ensuring stability. For when to distill, adaptive instance-level weighting strengthens guidance when autonomous exploration falls short and reduces it as the model's own competence grows. Experiments on four mathematical benchmarks across two model families show that SGPO consistently outperforms SFT, on-policy RL, and hybrid-policy baselines, improving the average score by 2.2 points over the strongest baseline on Qwen2.5-7B-Instruct. Analysis reveals that the forward-KL objective provides an inherently selective distillation signal that outperforms direct trajectory imitation, and that strategy distillation exhibits complementary scaling with base model capability.

AI.tldr: SGPO replaces trajectory imitation with strategy distillation for reasoning transfer, using forward-KL objectives and adaptive weighting, outperforming SFT and RL baselines on math benchmarks.
AI.motivation: Existing reasoning distillation methods imitate solution trajectories (what to answer) rather than reasoning processes (how to reason), leading to memorization of instance-specific steps and poor generalization to novel problems.
AI.method: SGPO extracts structured strategy descriptions from strong models, constructs both autonomous and strategy-guided trajectories, uses a token-level forward-KL objective with proximal constraints for distillation, and applies adaptive instance-level weighting to balance guidance based on model competence.
AI.result: SGPO consistently outperforms SFT, on-policy RL, and hybrid-policy baselines across four math benchmarks and two model families, improving average score by 2.2 points on Qwen2.5-7B-Instruct. Forward-KL provides better selective distillation than direct imitation, and strategy distillation scales complementarily with base model capability.
AI.conclusion: Distilling how to reason via reusable strategies (rather than what to answer via trajectories) improves generalization and performance in weak-to-strong reasoning transfer.

title: Flood Mapping from RGB imagery using a Vision Foundation Model
summary: Timely, high-resolution maps of flood extent around settlements are essential for emergency response and damage assessment. We consider airborne RGB imagery for flood mapping as it can be collected rapidly at low cost. To produce flood maps, deep learning models for water segmentation are often used. CNN based and small vision transformer models are used. However, they need much data for adaptation to a change of scenery, i.e., another flooding event. Vision foundation models or large vision transformers are known to generalize across domains. Recently, foundation models for Earth observation became available. They are pretrained on satellite data, whose spatial resolution, viewing geometry, and radiometry differ from nadir RGB imagery. Thus, adaptation is required. We investigate how a satellite-pretrained Earth observation foundation model can be adapted to centimeter-scale floodwater mapping from RGB imagery. Specifically, we fine-tune a model we call Prithvi-2.0-UPN consisting of the Prithvi-EO-2.0-600M Vision Transformer combined with a UPerNet decoder for binary water segmentation on two RGB datasets (BlessemFlood21, NeuenahrFlood). In a first experiment we observe that Prithvi-2.0-UPN reaches state-of-the-art results on BlessemFlood21 and NeuenahrFlood, when trained on their datasets. In a second experiment we show that Prithvi-2.0-UPN performs better than state-of-the-art baseline models for transfer to a new flood event (trained on BlessemFlood21, tested on NeuenahrFlood) in a zero-shot setting. However, the performance indicates room for improvement. In this respect, we investigate in a third experiment how performance improves when further fine-tuning the models with small shares of NeuenahrFlood training data: Prithvi-2.0-UPN improves the fastest and reaches almost the performance level when fully trained on NeuenahrFlood, indicating transfer capabilities.

AI.tldr: This paper investigates adapting a satellite-pretrained Earth observation foundation model (Prithvi-EO-2.0-600M Vision Transformer with UPerNet decoder) for centimeter-scale floodwater mapping from RGB aerial imagery. The model achieves state-of-the-art results on two flood datasets, shows better zero-shot transfer to new flood events than baselines, and demonstrates efficient fine-tuning with limited data.
AI.motivation: Timely, high-resolution flood maps around settlements are essential for emergency response, but deep learning models for water segmentation require substantial data for adaptation to new flood events. Vision foundation models for Earth observation exist but are pretrained on satellite data, which differs significantly from nadir RGB imagery in resolution, viewing geometry, and radiometry.
AI.method: The authors fine-tune Prithvi-2.0-UPN (Prithvi-EO-2.0-600M Vision Transformer + UPerNet decoder) for binary water segmentation on two RGB flood datasets (BlessemFlood21, NeuenahrFlood). Three experiments are conducted: (1) full training on both datasets, (2) zero-shot transfer from BlessemFlood21 to NeuenahrFlood, (3) fine-tuning with small portions of NeuenahrFlood training data.
AI.result: Prithvi-2.0-UPN achieves state-of-the-art results on both datasets when fully trained. It outperforms baseline models in zero-shot transfer to a new flood event. With small shares of target data, it improves fastest and nearly matches fully-trained performance.
AI.conclusion: A satellite-pretrained Earth observation foundation model can be effectively adapted to centimeter-scale flood mapping from RGB imagery, showing strong transfer capabilities and data efficiency. However, zero-shot performance still has room for improvement.

title: Exact Schur-Sylvester Dimensionality Reductions for Non-Smooth Stochastic Complexity and Manifold Sampling
summary: The exact computation of the Normalized Maximum Likelihood (NML) codelength for regular non-smooth estimators (e.g., Lasso) has been historically limited by the cubic scaling walls of manifold-constrained projection and volume integration. At each step of the geometric Propose-and-Project Metropolis--Hastings (PPMH) sampler, evaluating the projection operator requires inverting an $(N+k) \times (N+k)$ generalized KKT matrix, while calculating the volume factor requires the determinant of an $(N-k) \times (N-k)$ Gram matrix. This paper presents an exact, mathematically equivalent formulation that bypasses both bottlenecks by utilizing the block Schur complement and Sylvester's determinant identity. We prove that the computational complexity of both operations collapses from $\mathcal{O}(N^3)$ to $\mathcal{O}(k^3 + N^2 k)$ per step. We generalize this reduction to Sparse Support Vector Machines (SVMs), Elastic Net, and Group Lasso. Finally, we provide a rigorous numerical stability analysis and evaluate the sampler's efficiency using the Effective Sample Size (ESS) per second. Our empirical benchmarks on high-dimensional datasets confirm a constant speedup exceeding $14{,}100\times$ while maintaining double-precision numerical equivalence, rendering exact non-smooth NML estimation highly tractable for large-scale statistical inference.

AI.tldr: This paper introduces a mathematically equivalent reformulation of the PPMH sampler for exact NML codelength computation that reduces per-step complexity from O(N³) to O(k³ + N²k) using Schur complement and Sylvester's determinant identity, achieving >14,000× speedup while maintaining numerical precision for Lasso, Sparse SVM, Elastic Net, and Group Lasso.
AI.motivation: Exact computation of NML codelength for regular non-smooth estimators like Lasso has been computationally prohibitive due to O(N³) bottlenecks from manifold-constrained projection (requiring inversion of (N+k)×(N+k) KKT matrices) and volume integration (requiring determinants of (N-k)×(N-k) Gram matrices).
AI.method: The authors reformulate the PPMH sampler using block Schur complement to avoid full KKT matrix inversion and Sylvester's determinant identity to bypass direct Gram matrix determinant calculation, reducing complexity from O(N³) to O(k³ + N²k) per step. The method is generalized to Sparse SVM, Elastic Net, and Group Lasso, with rigorous numerical stability analysis.
AI.result: The approach achieves a constant speedup exceeding 14,100× on high-dimensional datasets while maintaining double-precision numerical equivalence. Efficiency is measured via Effective Sample Size (ESS) per second.
AI.conclusion: Exact non-smooth NML estimation is rendered highly tractable for large-scale statistical inference, breaking the historical cubic scaling limitations.

title: RoBoSR: Structured Scene Representations for Embodied Robotic Reasoning
summary:
Despite rapid progress, embodied reasoning under real-world variability remains challenging. Existing approaches rely on demonstration-driven sequential biases, limiting flexibility in open-ended and long-horizon tasks that require structured reasoning over evolving states.
  We introduce RoBoSR, an intermediate structural representation that formulates manipulation as step-wise state transitions over semantically grounded, object-centric scene graphs. By modeling object states and their spatial relations at the perception-action interface, RoBoSR disentangles high-level task reasoning from raw inputs and enables structured reasoning over preconditions, effects, and goal states. This representation endows the agent with causal reasoning capability, enforcing subtask dependencies and supporting coherent long-horizon task planning.
  To learn such structure-aware reasoning, we construct Manip-Cognition-1.6M, an open-world dataset that jointly supervises scene understanding, instruction interpretation, and subtask planning across diverse tasks.
  Across several benchmarks and real-world demonstrations, our method consistently outperforms prompting-based methods and classical TAMP baselines in zero-shot generalization and long-horizon tasks. The results underscore structured intermediate representations as a critical inductive bias for scalable embodied reasoning.

AI.tldr: RoBoSR introduces an object-centric scene graph representation for embodied reasoning, paired with a large dataset (Manip-Cognition-1.6M), enabling structured, causal long-horizon task planning that outperforms prompting-based and TAMP methods.
AI.motivation: Existing embodied reasoning methods rely on demonstration-driven sequential biases, which limit flexibility in open-ended and long-horizon tasks requiring structured reasoning over evolving states.
AI.method: RoBoSR uses intermediate structural representation (object-centric scene graphs) to model state transitions, disentangling high-level reasoning from raw inputs. A large dataset Manip-Cognition-1.6M is constructed to jointly supervise scene understanding, instruction interpretation, and subtask planning.
AI.result: Outperforms prompting-based methods and classical TAMP baselines in zero-shot generalization and long-horizon tasks across benchmarks and real-world demonstrations.
AI.conclusion: Structured intermediate representations serve as a critical inductive bias for scalable embodied reasoning in open-world environments.

title: Generative Modeling for Physiological Signals
summary: Physiological signals support clinical diagnosis, health monitoring, rehabilitation, wearable sensing, and human--machine interaction. However, their applications are often constrained by limited labeled data, class imbalance, noisy or incomplete recordings, heterogeneous acquisition settings, and privacy restrictions. Generative modeling has therefore attracted increasing attention as a means of addressing some of these barriers. Recent studies have used generative models to augment scarce datasets, restore degraded recordings, translate between modalities, and synthesize conditional physiological waveforms. This review summarizes recent work on generative modeling for cardiovascular, neural, muscular, peripheral, and specialized physiological signals. Major model families are covered, including generative adversarial networks (GANs), autoencoders and variational autoencoders (AEs/VAEs), diffusion models, autoregressive sequence models, and hybrid architectures. In addition, it organizes existing evaluation practices into a hierarchical framework spanning signal-level similarity, dataset-level distribution, physiological validity, task-oriented utility, and assessments of generalization and robustness. By linking signal-specific constraints, generative roles, model families, and evaluation evidence, this review provides structured guidance for the future use and evaluation of generative models in physiological-signal research.

AI.tldr: This review comprehensively summarizes generative modeling approaches (GANs, VAEs, diffusion models, autoregressive models, hybrids) applied to physiological signals (cardiovascular, neural, muscular, peripheral, specialized), addressing challenges like limited labeled data, class imbalance, noise, and privacy. It also proposes a hierarchical evaluation framework covering signal-level similarity, dataset distribution, physiological validity, task utility, and generalization/robustness.
AI.motivation: Physiological signal applications face significant barriers including limited labeled data, class imbalance, noisy/incomplete recordings, heterogeneous acquisition settings, and privacy restrictions. Generative modeling offers potential solutions to these challenges, motivating the need for a structured review of existing approaches.
AI.method: The paper conducts a literature review organized by: (1) signal types (cardiovascular, neural, muscular, peripheral, specialized), (2) model families (GANs, AEs/VAEs, diffusion models, autoregressive models, hybrids), and (3) evaluation practices structured into a hierarchical framework with five levels (signal similarity, dataset distribution, physiological validity, task-oriented utility, generalization/robustness).
AI.result: Identifies that generative models are used for data augmentation, signal restoration, modality translation, and conditional waveform synthesis across physiological signal domains. Proposes a hierarchical evaluation framework to standardize assessment practices in this field.
AI.conclusion: By linking signal-specific constraints, generative roles, model families, and evaluation evidence, this review provides structured guidance for future use and evaluation of generative models in physiological-signal research.

title: The Degeneracy Distillery
comment: 30 pages, 10 figures. Supporting code found at https://github.com/tlmakinen/degeneracy_distillery
summary: When two or more parameters or labels produce similar data, they are degenerate, or hard to distinguish. Degeneracies render both label prediction and inverse problems difficult, since both machine learning algorithms and probabilistic samplers rely on the distinguishability of data and its gradients with respect to parameters. However, identifying degeneracies in physical models or real-world datasets can be elucidating about the choice of model or the underlying process that produces the data. We present the degeneracy distillery, a method that (1) detects and (2) resolves degenerate parameter combinations (a) automatically and (b) symbolically, from parameter-data (or parameter-simulation) pairs alone, through estimation and flattening of the Fisher information matrix. By exploring the information geometry of the likelihood, we characterize degeneracies as an intrinsic property of the physical model, requiring no realised data observation. We demonstrate our approach on a range of synthetic and real-world problems, discovering symbolic coordinate transformations that identify the combinations of parameters of a model which yield independent effects on the data. The resulting coordinates flatten the Fisher information in expectation globally, in contrast to posterior-based methods that flatten only at a single point, and substantially reduce the simulation budget required for downstream neural posterior estimation. In test cases we require up to $10\times$ fewer simulations for posterior estimation at matched validation calibration whilst simultaneously gaining physical insight on the system.

AI.tldr: A method (degeneracy distillery) that automatically detects and resolves degenerate parameter combinations in models by analyzing the Fisher information matrix, requiring no actual data - just parameter-simulation pairs. It discovers symbolic coordinate transformations that flatten the Fisher information globally, reducing simulation needs by up to 10x for neural posterior estimation.
AI.motivation: Degeneracies (parameters/labels producing similar data) make label prediction and inverse problems difficult. The authors aim to automatically detect and resolve these degeneracies to improve model understanding and reduce computational costs in posterior estimation.
AI.method: The degeneracy distillery works by (1) estimating the Fisher information matrix from parameter-data/parameter-simulation pairs, (2) flattening it through symbolic coordinate transformations, and (3) exploring the information geometry of the likelihood. This is done automatically and symbolically without requiring realized data observations.
AI.result: Demonstrated on synthetic and real-world problems, the method discovers symbolic coordinate transformations that identify parameter combinations yielding independent effects. It flattens Fisher information globally (unlike posterior-based methods that flatten at a single point), and reduces simulation budget by up to 10x for neural posterior estimation at matched validation calibration.
AI.conclusion: Degeneracies are intrinsic properties of physical models, and the degeneracy distillery effectively identifies and resolves them via Fisher information analysis and symbolic coordinate transformations, providing both computational efficiency gains and physical insight into the system.

title: Trustworthy Image Authentication using Forensic Knowledge Graphs
summary: Advances in generative AI have made image falsification highly realistic, demanding trustworthy authentication systems. Existing forensic detectors can target certain forgery types but lack interpretability, while vision-language models (VLMs) provide explanations but cannot exploit forensic traces for reliable detection. We propose Forensic Knowledge Graphs (FKGs), a unified framework that integrates forensic evidence extraction, structured reasoning, and human-interpretable explanation. Our FKG structure encodes forensic traces along with their causal dependencies and links to scene content. To generate accurate FKGs, we introduce a novel forensic authentication network and an Iterative Context Refinement strategy that guides VLMs to produce faithful, grounded explanations. We also present FKG-50K, a dataset of 50,000 realistic forgeries with ground-truth FKGs. Experiments demonstrate that FKG outperforms both forensic detectors and VLMs in detection, forgery identification and localization, and forensic justification.

AI.tldr: The paper proposes Forensic Knowledge Graphs (FKGs) that unify forensic evidence extraction, structured reasoning, and interpretable explanations for image forgery detection, outperforming both traditional detectors and vision-language models.
AI.motivation: Existing forensic detectors lack interpretability, and VLMs provide explanations but cannot exploit forensic traces for reliable detection. A unified framework combining both capabilities is needed.
AI.method: The authors introduce Forensic Knowledge Graphs (FKGs) that encode forensic traces, causal dependencies, and scene content. They develop a forensic authentication network and an Iterative Context Refinement strategy for faithful explanation generation, along with the FKG-50K dataset.
AI.result: FKG outperforms both forensic detectors and VLMs in detection accuracy, forgery identification, localization, and forensic justification.
AI.conclusion: FKGs provide a unified, interpretable framework for trustworthy image authentication, bridging the gap between forensic detection and explainable AI.

title: Exploring Academic Influence of Algorithms by Co-occurrence Network Based on Full-text of Academic Papers
summary: Algorithms have become central to scientific research in the era of artificial intelligence (AI). Although algorithm mentions in papers are often used to indicate popularity and influence, existing studies usually evaluate individual algorithms in isolation and pay limited attention to the collective influence formed through their interconnections. This study constructs large-scale algorithm co-occurrence networks in natural language processing (NLP) based on the full text of academic papers and investigates algorithm influence from a network perspective. Using deep learning models, we extract algorithm entities and build overall, cumulative, and annual co-occurrence networks. We analyze their structural characteristics and apply multiple centrality measures to assess the group influence of algorithms across the whole field and over time. The results show that algorithm networks display typical features of complex networks, with increasingly dense connections developing over approximately two decades. Classic, high-performing algorithms and those located at the intersections of different research periods tend to have high popularity, control, centrality, and balanced influence. When the influence of an algorithm declines, it usually loses its core network position first, followed by weaker associations with other algorithms. This study is the first large-scale analysis of algorithm co-occurrence networks. Covering more than four decades of academic publications, it provides a temporal and structural view of algorithm influence and offers a foundation for future research on networks linking algorithms, scholars, and tasks.

AI.tldr: This study constructs large-scale algorithm co-occurrence networks in NLP using full-text academic papers, analyzing network structures and centrality measures to assess collective algorithm influence over four decades, revealing that algorithm networks exhibit complex network features with classic and bridging algorithms holding high influence.
AI.motivation: Existing studies evaluate individual algorithms in isolation and pay limited attention to the collective influence formed through their interconnections in the era of AI-driven research.
AI.method: Using deep learning models to extract algorithm entities from full-text academic papers, constructing overall, cumulative, and annual co-occurrence networks, and applying multiple centrality measures to analyze structural characteristics.
AI.result: Algorithm networks display typical complex network features with increasingly dense connections over ~20 years. Classic, high-performing algorithms and those at intersections of research periods have high popularity and balanced influence. When influence declines, algorithms first lose core network position, then weaker associations.
AI.conclusion: This is the first large-scale analysis of algorithm co-occurrence networks, providing temporal and structural views of algorithm influence across four decades, and offering a foundation for future research on networks linking algorithms, scholars, and tasks.

title: High-Fidelity Synthetic Transmission Electron Microscopy Image Generation Using Diffusion Probabilistic Models for Data-Limited Semiconductor Metrology
comment: To be presented at the 2026 International Symposium ELMAR, published by IEEE in the conference proceedings
summary: Advanced semiconductor nodes drastically increased demand for Transmission Electron Microscopy (TEM), yet destructive sample preparation, slow imaging and high costs severely limit the availability of diverse datasets needed for downstream machine learning (ML). Synthetic data generation is becoming essential, but current generative models often miss TEM-specific noise, structural detail, and stochastic variability crucial for evaluation. We present a Denoising Diffusion Probabilistic Model (DDPM) framework for synthetic TEM image generation under extreme data scarcity. A progressive patch-based training strategy scales from low-resolution patches to full images, enabling from-scratch training with only 15 samples. We integrate a custom TrivialAugment adaptation, cross-process domain transfer, classifier guidance, and RePaint-style inpainting, culminating in full-image generation that preserves global structural and spatial relationships in compliance with FAB metrology requirements. Beyond synthesis, we repurpose DDPM feature representations for segmentation, partitioning encoder feature maps to obtain coherent region masks. Our synthetic images achieve up to MS-SSIM > 0.98 and qualitative expert assessment consistent with structural similarity results, facilitating downstream ML training for defect detection, segmentation, and metrology while preserving statistical and physical realism.

AI.tldr: A DDPM framework generates realistic synthetic TEM images from only 15 real samples, using progressive patch-based training and custom augmentations, achieving >0.98 MS-SSIM and enabling downstream ML for defect detection and segmentation.
AI.motivation: Advanced semiconductor nodes require TEM imaging, but destructive sample preparation, slow imaging, and high costs severely limit the availability of diverse datasets needed for machine learning applications.
AI.method: A Denoising Diffusion Probabilistic Model (DDPM) with progressive patch-based training (low-res to full images), TrivialAugment adaptation, cross-process domain transfer, classifier guidance, and RePaint-style inpainting, plus repurposing DDPM features for segmentation.
AI.result: Achieves MS-SSIM > 0.98 on synthetic images, with qualitative expert assessment consistent with structural similarity results, enabling downstream ML training for defect detection, segmentation, and metrology.
AI.conclusion: The DDPM framework successfully generates physically realistic TEM images under extreme data scarcity (only 15 samples), preserving statistical and physical realism for downstream semiconductor metrology tasks.

title: SlipSense: Multimodal Sensing for Online Slip Detection in Legged Robots
summary: Legged robots rely on accurate ground interaction awareness to traverse variable terrains, such as slippery surfaces. Existing slip detection methods often rely on kinematics and proprioception, which lack the sensitivity to detect early-stage slips that occur prior to catastrophic instability. Thus, this paper presents SlipSense, a novel framework for online force-based slip detection using a custom lightweight sensorized foot for quadrupeds to detect slip. The framework integrates a multimodal sensor design with a LSTM-based model to infer ground reaction forces and detect slip-indicative anomalies during locomotion. The proposed framework is deployed on a Unitree Go1 quadruped to demonstrate blind online slip detection over a slippery terrain. Our method detects early-stage slips down to an average displacement of 24.1 +/-6.4mm with an overall accuracy of 85.9%. This represents a 3.3-fold finer detection resolution and a 24% relative accuracy improvement over a standard kinematic baseline that uses foot velocity inferred through state estimation. The work in this paper serves as a foundation for force-aware gait adaptation in legged robotic locomotion, allowing future controllers to estimate terrain friction and adjust constraints, thus improving the overall stability of the system.

AI.tldr: SlipSense is a force-based slip detection framework for legged robots that uses a custom sensorized foot and LSTM model to detect early-stage slips on slippery terrain, achieving 85.9% accuracy and detecting slips as small as 24.1mm—3.3x finer resolution than kinematic baselines.
AI.motivation: Existing slip detection methods rely on kinematics and proprioception, which lack sensitivity to detect early-stage slips before catastrophic instability occurs on variable terrains like slippery surfaces.
AI.method: A custom lightweight sensorized foot is designed for quadruped robots, integrating multimodal sensors with an LSTM-based model to infer ground reaction forces and detect slip-indicative anomalies during locomotion, deployed on a Unitree Go1 quadruped.
AI.result: Detects early-stage slips down to 24.1±6.4mm average displacement with 85.9% overall accuracy, achieving 3.3x finer detection resolution and 24% relative accuracy improvement over kinematic baseline using foot velocity from state estimation.
AI.conclusion: This work provides a foundation for force-aware gait adaptation in legged locomotion, enabling future controllers to estimate terrain friction and adjust constraints for improved system stability.

title: Unlocking Realism and Interpretability in Wireless Channel Synthesis: A Physics-Guided Generative Approach
comment: arXiv admin note: text overlap with arXiv:2506.00374
summary: In recent years, machine learning (ML) methods have become increasingly popular for wireless communication systems. These require large amounts of data reflecting the behavior of realistic channels with high fidelity. However, sampling over-the-air (OTA) channel data is an extremely resource-intensive process which cannot accurately represent the variety of real world channels. This results in the need for realistic training data for ML systems. To this end, generative models have been proposed to synthesize channel data. However,(i) the outputs produced by such methods may not correspond to physically viable channels, (ii) the outputs may not provide insights into the associated environment, and (iii) training the generative model may need labeled data, requiring resource intensive data annotation. Through this work, we address these issues by integrating a parametric, physics-based geometric channel (PPGC) modeling framework derived from planar wave propagation equations, with generative methods to produce realistic channel matrices with interpretable representations in the parameter domain. To overcome the limitations of the resulting non-convex optimization landscape, we propose a linearized reformulation of the PPGC model to ensure smooth gradient flow during training, while also providing insights into the underlying physical environment. We incorporate a tensor decomposition framework into the linearized reformulation to allow for flexibility in the number of wireless channel parameters. We also show the compatibility of this reformulation with parameter extraction tasks. We evaluate our model against prior baselines by comparing generated, scenario-specific samples to true channels in terms of their similarity and through their utility in downstream compression tasks.

AI.tldr: This paper proposes integrating a physics-based geometric channel model with generative methods to produce realistic, physically-viable wireless channel data. It addresses three key issues: outputs may not correspond to physically valid channels, lack environmental insights, and need for labeled data. The authors introduce a linearized reformulation of the PPGC model with tensor decomposition for smooth training and parameter flexibility.
AI.motivation: ML methods for wireless communication need large amounts of realistic channel data, but OTA sampling is resource-intensive and cannot fully represent real-world channels. Existing generative models produce outputs that may not be physically viable, lack environmental insights, and often require labeled data for training.
AI.method: (1) Integration of parametric physics-based geometric channel (PPGC) modeling with generative methods. (2) Linearized reformulation of the PPGC model to overcome non-convex optimization issues. (3) Tensor decomposition framework for flexible channel parameters. (4) Compatibility demonstration with parameter extraction tasks.
AI.result: The model is evaluated against prior baselines by comparing generated scenario-specific samples to true channels in terms of similarity and utility in downstream compression tasks.
AI.conclusion: The proposed approach produces physically viable, interpretable channel data with smooth training dynamics and flexible parameter handling, offering advantages over prior generative methods for wireless channel synthesis.

title: Machine Learning Modeling for Real-Time Melt Pool Monitoring in Laser Powder Bed Fusion Additive Manufacturing: A Hybrid Approach
summary: This work investigates the implementation of artificial intelligence and machine learning (AI/ML) for real-time monitoring in laser powder bed fusion (LPBF) additive manufacturing. We developed a binary image classification framework for distinguishing normal and abnormal melt pool images using a balanced dataset of 1,200 images collected from Nickel superalloy 625 on the NIST AMMT platform. The study evaluates accuracy and inference time based on control requirements and hardware limitations of open-architecture LPBF machines. We benchmark three transfer learning architectures (ResNet50, EfficientNetB0, and MobileNetV2) against two Random Forest approaches: one trained on EfficientNetB0 feature embeddings (hybrid) and one trained on raw pixel features (baseline). Images are stratified into 80/20 train-test splits, with a further 90/10 validation split on the training set, and undergo standardized resizing, normalization, and label-preserving data augmentation to emulate realistic process variability. Each model is evaluated using accuracy, precision, recall, F1 score, and area under the receiver operating characteristic curve (AUC), along with training time, inference latency, and CPU & GPU usage to capture deployability constraints relevant to factory-floor monitoring. The hybrid EfficientNetB0-plus-Random Forest approach achieves the best performance on the held-out test set, with an F1 score of 0.9451, accuracy of 0.9458, and AUC of 0.9904, while maintaining sub-millisecond per-image inference (1.15 ms). In contrast, purely deep learning models exhibit significantly higher inference times with lower accuracy. These results demonstrate that combining pre-trained convolutional features with classical ensemble methods provides a robust, computationally efficient route to real-time melt pool anomaly detection in data-limited additive manufacturing environments.

AI.tldr: This paper develops a binary image classification framework using AI/ML for real-time melt pool anomaly detection in laser powder bed fusion additive manufacturing. A hybrid approach combining EfficientNetB0 feature embeddings with Random Forest achieves the best performance (F1=0.9451, accuracy=0.9458, AUC=0.9904) with sub-millisecond inference (1.15 ms), outperforming pure deep learning models in both accuracy and computational efficiency.
AI.motivation: To enable real-time monitoring and anomaly detection in LPBF additive manufacturing by developing a computationally efficient AI/ML framework that balances accuracy with hardware constraints of open-architecture machines, addressing data-limited environments in factory-floor settings.
AI.method: A binary image classification framework using a balanced dataset of 1,200 melt pool images (Nickel superalloy 625). Benchmarks three transfer learning architectures (ResNet50, EfficientNetB0, MobileNetV2) against two Random Forest approaches (hybrid using EfficientNetB0 embeddings, baseline using raw pixels). Models evaluated on accuracy, precision, recall, F1, AUC, training time, inference latency, and CPU/GPU usage.
AI.result: The hybrid EfficientNetB0-plus-Random Forest approach achieves the best performance: F1=0.9451, accuracy=0.9458, AUC=0.9904 with sub-millisecond inference (1.15 ms). Pure deep learning models show higher inference times with lower accuracy.
AI.conclusion: Combining pre-trained convolutional features with classical ensemble methods offers a robust, computationally efficient solution for real-time melt pool anomaly detection in data-limited additive manufacturing environments.

title: DivRL: Disentangled Self-Similarity Rewards for Diverse Subject-Driven Generation
comment: Accepted to ECCV 2026. Project page: https://qianwangx.github.io/DivRL/
summary: Subject-driven image generation faces an "Identity-Diversity Paradox", where strong identity preservation often leads to rigid and low-diversity outputs. We propose a post-training framework called DivRL that jointly optimizes identity consistency and structural diversity simultaneously by leveraging disentangled visual features from a robust similarity model. Specifically, we introduce a Negative Self-Similarity Measure (nSSM) to quantify structural diversity, and Visual Semantic Matching (VSM) to evaluate identity consistency. We propose an "Explore-and-Suppress" strategy that treats VSM as a gated constraint: the model freely explores structurally diverse configurations, and only samples that violate the identity threshold are penalized via a quadratic hinge loss. This converts identity preservation from a competing objective into a feasibility constraint, allowing nSSM and VSM to improve jointly. Experiments demonstrate that our method effectively pushes the model to generate both consistent and diverse images and improves structural diversity while maintaining comparable identity consistency through a gated optimization formulation.

AI.tldr: DivRL is a post-training framework that resolves the Identity-Diversity Paradox in subject-driven image generation by using disentangled visual features, a Negative Self-Similarity Measure (nSSM) for diversity, Visual Semantic Matching (VSM) for identity, and an Explore-and-Suppress strategy that treats identity as a gated constraint rather than a competing objective.
AI.motivation: Subject-driven image generation suffers from an "Identity-Diversity Paradox"—strong identity preservation leads to rigid, low-diversity outputs. The authors aim to jointly optimize both identity consistency and structural diversity.
AI.method: The framework (DivRL) introduces: (1) Negative Self-Similarity Measure (nSSM) to quantify structural diversity, (2) Visual Semantic Matching (VSM) for identity consistency, and (3) an "Explore-and-Suppress" strategy where VSM acts as a gated constraint—only samples violating identity threshold are penalized via quadratic hinge loss, converting identity preservation from competing objective to feasibility constraint.
AI.result: The method effectively generates both identity-consistent and structurally diverse images. It improves structural diversity while maintaining comparable identity consistency through the gated optimization formulation.
AI.conclusion: The Explore-and-Suppress strategy successfully resolves the Identity-Diversity Paradox by reformulating identity preservation as a feasibility constraint, enabling joint improvement of nSSM and VSM.

title: ReMMD: Realistic Multilingual Multi-Image Agentic Verification for Multimodal Misinformation Detection
comment: The project is available at https://dang-ai.github.io/ReMMD
summary: Multimodal misinformation detection is increasingly important because viral posts now combine long multilingual narratives, several images, mixed provenance, and subtle text--image framing errors. Existing benchmarks and methods remain poorly matched to this setting: they usually isolate short captions, single images, binary labels, or one manipulation source, while agentic verification remains costly under realistic evidence search. We present ReMMD, a realistic multilingual multi-image agentic verification framework for multimodal misinformation detection. ReMMD includes ReMMDBench, a real-world multimodal misinformation detection benchmark with 500 samples, 2,756 images, five monolingual languages, two cross-lingual settings, three text-length tiers, multi-image posts, five-way veracity labels, eight distortion labels, evidence provenance, and rationales. It also includes ReMMD-Agent, a persistent-memory verifier that decomposes posts into atomic points, builds a reusable evidence set, and predicts structured L1/L2/L3 outputs. Across proprietary systems, open LVLMs, MMD-Agent, and T2-Agent, ReMMD-Agent obtains the best five-way veracity performance, with 41.80% accuracy and 39.12% macro-F1 using GPT-5.2, while reducing cost by 17.5% relative to MMD-Agent and 79.9% relative to T2-Agent. The project is available at https://dang-ai.github.io/ReMMD.

AI.tldr: ReMMD presents a realistic multilingual, multi-image agentic verification framework for multimodal misinformation detection, featuring a new benchmark (ReMMDBench) with 500 samples, 2,756 images, and diverse linguistic/veracity settings, along with a persistent-memory agent (ReMMD-Agent) that achieves best five-way veracity performance (41.80% accuracy) while reducing cost by 17.5-79.9%.
AI.motivation: Existing multimodal misinformation detection benchmarks and methods poorly match real-world viral posts, which combine long multilingual narratives, multiple images, mixed provenance, and subtle text-image framing errors. Current approaches isolate short captions, single images, binary labels, or one manipulation source, and agentic verification remains costly under realistic evidence search.
AI.method: ReMMD includes (1) ReMMDBench — a benchmark with 500 samples, 2,756 images, five monolingual languages, two cross-lingual settings, three text-length tiers, multi-image posts, five-way veracity labels, eight distortion labels, evidence provenance, and rationales; (2) ReMMD-Agent — a persistent-memory verifier that decomposes posts into atomic points, builds a reusable evidence set, and predicts structured L1/L2/L3 outputs.
AI.result: ReMMD-Agent achieved the best five-way veracity performance with 41.80% accuracy and 39.12% macro-F1 using GPT-5.2, while reducing cost by 17.5% relative to MMD-Agent and 79.9% relative to T2-Agent.
AI.conclusion: ReMMD demonstrates that realistic evaluation settings (multi-image, multilingual, multi-granularity labels) are feasible and important for advancing multimodal misinformation detection, and that persistent-memory agent architectures can improve both accuracy and cost efficiency.

title: PDS Joint: A Parametric Double-Spiral Joint Tailored for Dexterous Hands
summary: Compliant joints can embed safety and adaptability into dexterous hands, but achieving large-stroke anthropomorphic motion while maintaining joint-specific, directiondependent stiffness and reliable proprioception remains challenging. This paper presents the PDS joint, a parametric doublespiral (PDS) compliant joint that enables systematic shaping of directional stiffness across multiple deformation modes, including flexion/extension, abduction/adduction, and pronation/supination. We instantiate the joint using Archimedean and logarithmic spiral templates for different hand joints and introduce an asymmetry ratio to tailor stiffness distributions for both grasp stability and hyperextension resistance. To make the joint practically usable under large deformation, we co-design embedded inductive proprioception and propose a learningbased calibration pipeline that maps raw inductive signals to joint states using ArUco-marker tracking. Experiments characterize the stiffness landscapes across geometric parameters and demonstrate a non-monotonic dependence of lateral support on asymmetry, indicating the importance of principled parameter tuning. For joint-state estimation in the most challenging abduction/adduction motion, a learned multilayer-perceptron (MLP) mapping reduces the error compared with conventional curve fitting by 41.6%. Finally, we integrate the proposed joints into an open-source dexterous hand as a demonstration platform, on which the hand grasps a set of nine everyday objects and performs safe, contact-rich human-involved interactions.

AI.tldr: This paper presents the PDS (parametric double-spiral) compliant joint for dexterous hands, enabling directional stiffness shaping across multiple deformation modes. It introduces Archimedean/logarithmic spiral templates with an asymmetry ratio for stiffness tuning, a learning-based calibration pipeline for proprioception using inductive sensing and MLP mapping, and demonstrates integration into an open-source hand for safe grasping and human interaction.
AI.motivation: Compliant joints offer safety and adaptability for dexterous hands, but existing designs struggle to achieve large-stroke anthropomorphic motion while maintaining joint-specific, direction-dependent stiffness and reliable proprioception.
AI.method: The paper proposes a parametric double-spiral (PDS) compliant joint with Archimedean and logarithmic spiral templates, an asymmetry ratio for stiffness tailoring, embedded inductive proprioception, and a learning-based calibration pipeline using ArUco-marker tracking and MLP mapping for joint-state estimation.
AI.result: Experiments revealed non-monotonic dependence of lateral support on asymmetry. The MLP-based joint-state estimation reduced error by 41.6% compared to conventional curve fitting for abduction/adduction. The joint was successfully integrated into an open-source dexterous hand that grasped nine everyday objects and performed safe human-involved interactions.
AI.conclusion: The PDS joint provides a principled approach to achieving direction-dependent stiffness and reliable proprioception for compliant dexterous hands, with demonstrated effectiveness in large-stroke motion and contact-rich tasks.

title: A Kalman Filter-Based Tracking Loop Design for Real-Time Aerospace GNSS Applications with Minimum Pull-Out Probability
comment: This is a revised version of the manuscript. V1 was previously posted on TechRxiv with DOI: 10.36227/techrxiv.176344172.21345819/v1 This work has been submitted to the IEEE for possible publication
summary: Kalman filter-based (KF-based) tracking loops are a powerful alternative to traditional phase-locked loops (PLLs) for Global Navigation Satellite Systems (GNSS) signal tracking. The primary advantage of the KF is its ability to incorporate high-fidelity models for receiver dynamics and clock errors, allowing the loop to adapt optimally to signal conditions. However, this theoretical optimality is often compromised in practice by the processing delays inherent in real-time systems with hardware correlators, which existing KF formulations typically neglect. This paper introduces a Modified Kalman filter (mKF) that overcomes this limitation specifically for hardware-based architectures. By reformulating the measurement update to be consistent with the processing delays, the proposed mKF maintains optimality in a practical implementation. We further present a systematic method for tuning both the process noise covariance matrix and the correlation time, based on an analytical expression for the pull-out probability (POP), which is validated through Monte Carlo simulation. The mKF is then validated with a GNSS signal simulator, both by post-processing baseband samples and on a real-time GPS receiver with hardware correlators. A direct equivalence between the mKF and a one-delay Digital PLL (DPLL) is established entirely in the digital domain. At equal noise bandwidth, the mKF matches the DPLL's phase error variance while achieving lower error in the higher-order states. Moreover, the mKF sustains lock at bandwidths inaccessible to the optimal one-delay DPLL under the same dynamic stress, positioning the proposed architecture as a robust and noise-efficient solution for high-dynamic aerospace GNSS applications.

AI.tldr: The paper introduces a Modified Kalman filter (mKF) for GNSS tracking that accounts for processing delays in hardware-based architectures, achieving better performance than traditional KF and one-delay DPLL under high dynamics.
AI.motivation: Existing KF-based tracking loops neglect processing delays inherent in real-time systems with hardware correlators, which compromises their theoretical optimality in practical implementations.
AI.method: The authors reformulate the measurement update to be consistent with processing delays, propose a systematic tuning method for process noise covariance and correlation time based on pull-out probability (POP), and establish a direct equivalence between the mKF and a one-delay DPLL in the digital domain.
AI.result: At equal noise bandwidth, the mKF matches DPLL's phase error variance while achieving lower error in higher-order states. The mKF sustains lock at bandwidths inaccessible to the optimal one-delay DPLL under the same dynamic stress.
AI.conclusion: The mKF is a robust and noise-efficient solution for high-dynamic aerospace GNSS applications, validated via Monte Carlo simulation, post-processing, and real-time GPS receiver testing.

title: Sesame: Structure-Aware Molecular Generation via Spatial Density-Map Conditioning
summary: Generative molecular models for drug design are a promising direction with much active research. In the next phase of computational drug design, such models will need to understand small molecule structure and protein-ligand interactions, and they will need to possess the machinery to generate molecules \textit{de novo}. Incorporating each feature poses a critical challenge. Equally important, yet often treated as secondary, is the ability to grow a molecule from a partial starting point -- a scaffold or fragment supplied by a chemist -- which is the central operation of lead optimization. We present Sesame (Spatial Evoformer for a Structure-Aware Molecular Engine), a diffusion-based molecular generation model that leverages a novel spatial pairformer module to condition on partial molecular structure and the surrounding protein pocket, both expressed as continuous spatial density maps. This single conditioning mechanism supports both \textit{de novo} generation and fragment-conditioned lead optimization, letting a medicinal chemist prune a hit to a scaffold and have Sesame grow it in productive ways. In addition to this module, we also introduce a diffusion framework for joint denoising of atom types, bond types, and positions, along with a trajectory finetuning scheme that trains on the model's own sampling rollouts to improve generation quality. Sesame is trained on a large corpus of ligand-only and protein-ligand datasets.

AI.tldr: Sesame is a diffusion-based molecular generation model that uses a spatial pairformer module to condition on both partial molecular structures and protein pockets via continuous spatial density maps, enabling both de novo generation and fragment-conditioned lead optimization, with joint denoising of atom types, bond types, and positions plus trajectory finetuning.
AI.motivation: Existing generative molecular models often lack the ability to incorporate partial molecular starting points (scaffolds/fragments) for lead optimization, treating this as secondary to de novo generation, whereas in practice medicinal chemists need to grow molecules from existing scaffolds conditioned on protein pockets.
AI.method: Sesame introduces: (1) a spatial pairformer module that conditions on partial molecular structure and protein pockets via continuous spatial density maps; (2) a diffusion framework for joint denoising of atom types, bond types, and positions; (3) a trajectory finetuning scheme that trains on the model's own sampling rollouts to improve generation quality.
AI.result: The model is trained on a large corpus of both ligand-only and protein-ligand datasets, supporting both de novo generation and fragment-conditioned lead optimization from scaffolds supplied by chemists.
AI.conclusion: Sesame demonstrates a unified approach to molecular generation that equally prioritizes both de novo design and scaffold-based lead optimization through a single conditioning mechanism, addressing a critical gap in computational drug design.

title: Token-to-Token Alignment of Text Embeddings for Semantic Blending
summary: In modern generative models, images are specified and controlled through text prompts. In practice, images are generated from sequences of tokens derived from these prompts. However, the space of token sequences lacks a consistent accessible structure: semantically similar images may correspond to sequences that differ in wording, ordering, and placement of concepts, while similar token sequences may encode very different semantics. This apparent lack of structure makes it difficult to perform smooth transitions in this space, hindering applications such as image blending and continuous control of edits. We argue that this limitation stems not from the absence of semantic structure, but from misalignment between representations. To address this misalignment, we introduce Token-to-Token alignment, a framework that establishes explicit semantic correspondence between tokens across prompts. Our approach transforms prompts into a structured representation in which semantically corresponding concepts are mapped to consistent positions across prompts, and then aligns their token embeddings based on semantic similarity. Concretely, the method consists of two stages: a structural alignment that rephrases prompts into a shared structured form, followed by an embedding-level alignment that matches token representations across prompts. With this alignment in place, simple linear interpolation becomes a meaningful operation, producing smooth and coherent semantic transitions and enabling applications such as blending and continuous editing. Our results show that text embedding spaces in text-to-image models implicitly encode a continuous semantic structure that becomes accessible once representations are properly aligned, suggesting that semantic control can be achieved by organizing existing representations rather than modifying the generative model.

AI.tldr: Introduces Token-to-Token alignment, a method that rephrases prompts into a structured format and aligns token embeddings semantically, enabling smooth linear interpolation for image blending and continuous editing in text-to-image models.
AI.motivation: Token sequences lack a consistent structure—semantically similar images can come from very different token sequences, while similar sequences can yield different semantics. This makes smooth transitions (blending, editing) difficult.
AI.method: Two-stage alignment: (1) Structural alignment rephrases prompts into a shared structured form; (2) Embedding-level alignment matches token representations across prompts based on semantic similarity.
AI.result: After alignment, simple linear interpolation produces smooth, coherent semantic transitions, enabling effective image blending and continuous editing without modifying the generative model.
AI.conclusion: Text embedding spaces in text-to-image models already encode continuous semantic structure, and proper alignment makes it accessible—semantic control can be achieved by organizing existing representations rather than modifying the model.

title: VeryTrace: Verifying Reasoning Traces through Compilable Formalism and Structured Verification
summary:
Multi-step reasoning with Chain-of-Thought (CoT) prompting remains fragile: logical errors or hallucinations in early steps silently propagate, producing confident but incorrect conclusions. This paper presents VeryTrace, a zero-shot verification-and-repair framework that formalizes natural-language reasoning traces into a structured, compilable representation. VeryTrace introduces a Domain-Specific Language (DSL) that (i) makes step dependencies explicit, (ii) mechanizes quantitative content as executable expressions, and (iii) structures semantic inferences via deduction schemas. Our hybrid verifier combines deterministic checks for computational correctness, dependency resolution, and constraint satisfaction with targeted LLM audits for non-mechanizable semantic judgments, enabling step-level error localization and repair.
  Across three diverse domains-competition mathematics (AIME 2025), robotics planning (LLM-BabyBench), and kinship reasoning (CLUTRR), VeryTrace improves accuracy over zero-shot baselines on state-of-the-art LLMs without requiring domain-specific training or in-context examples, demonstrating that formalized trace verification achieves both precision and generalization.

AI.tldr: VeryTrace is a zero-shot framework that converts natural language reasoning traces into a structured DSL for hybrid verification (deterministic + LLM audits), enabling step-level error localization and repair. It improves accuracy across math, robotics planning, and kinship reasoning without domain-specific training.
AI.motivation: Chain-of-Thought reasoning is fragile because early errors silently propagate, leading to confident but incorrect conclusions. Existing methods lack mechanisms to formally verify and repair reasoning traces.
AI.method: VeryTrace introduces a DSL that makes step dependencies explicit, mechanizes quantitative content as executable expressions, and structures inferences via deduction schemas. A hybrid verifier uses deterministic checks (computational correctness, dependency resolution, constraints) plus targeted LLM audits for non-mechanizable semantic judgments to localize and repair errors at the step level.
AI.result: Across AIME 2025 (math), LLM-BabyBench (robotics), and CLUTRR (kinship), VeryTrace improves accuracy over zero-shot baselines on state-of-the-art LLMs without domain-specific training or in-context examples.
AI.conclusion: Formalized trace verification achieves both precision (via deterministic checks) and generalization (across diverse domains) for multi-step reasoning.

title: RE4: Transformation-aware Imitation of Object Interactions Using Manipulation Modes
summary: Object interaction tasks have been a focus of advances in imitation learning. End-to-end methods, dominated by diffusion and flow-based variants have shown leaps in performance while sacrificing interpretability. Object-centric and pose-informed variants have had a role in learning from demonstration in manipulation tasks. In this paper, we revisit a few modern imitation learning benchmarks for object interactions, with the aim of composing a framework that repurposes principled theories of manipulation, preserving both performance and interpretability. For image observations, lightweight training is proposed for model-free pose estimation of the target object, using self-supervision over the demonstration data available for imitation learning. This information is then used to inform a manipulation mode-aware retrieval of a demonstration, a mode-aware transformation, a replan step that connects to the retrieval point while preserving mode constraints, and finally rolling out the transformed demonstration. These compose four key steps of the proposed RE4 framework, evaluated over state-based and image-based benchmarks in Push-T and Robomimic. An adversarial benchmark that evaluates sparse data regions of image-based Push-T showcases the robustness, further bolstered by indications from low-data regime experiments. The current work shows promise in using simple interpretable building blocks to learn manipulation skills.

AI.tldr: The paper proposes RE4, a framework that combines lightweight self-supervised pose estimation with mode-aware demonstration retrieval, transformation, and replanning to achieve interpretable and robust imitation learning for object interaction tasks, outperforming on Push-T and Robomimic benchmarks.
AI.motivation: To address the trade-off between performance and interpretability in imitation learning for object interactions, where end-to-end methods (diffusion/flow-based) achieve high performance but sacrifice interpretability, while object-centric methods preserve interpretability but lag in performance.
AI.method: RE4 framework with four steps: (1) lightweight self-supervised pose estimation from image observations using demonstration data, (2) manipulation mode-aware retrieval of demonstrations, (3) mode-aware transformation, and (4) replanning to connect to retrieval points while preserving mode constraints, followed by rolling out transformed demonstrations.
AI.result: Evaluated on Push-T and Robomimic benchmarks (both state-based and image-based). Adversarial benchmark testing sparse data regions in image-based Push-T demonstrated robustness. Low-data regime experiments showed promising performance.
AI.conclusion: Simple interpretable building blocks can effectively learn manipulation skills while preserving both performance and interpretability, showing promise over complex end-to-end methods.
