title: Low-rank Updates in Slowly Time-varying Graphs for Spatial-Temporal Signal Interpolation
summary: A crucial assumption in graph signal processing (GSP) is the existence of an underlying graph that captures the pairwise similarities between nodes, allowing filters to be designed based on this graph for tasks such as denoising. For spatial-temporal data in which node-to-node similarities evolve over time, a static spatial graph is insufficient. In this paper, to represent slowly time-varying pairwise relationships, we model the graph changes in two consecutive adjacency matrices $P = W^{(2)} - W^{(1)}$ across time as a low-rank matrix. % Specifically, given an initial adjacency matrix $W^{(1)}$ at time $t=1$, we jointly interpolate a signal $x_2$ and estimate $W^{(2)}$ at $t=2$ using both a graph signal smoothness prior for $x_2$ and a low-rank prior on $¶$. We alternate optimization steps. With $W^{(2)}$ fixed, $x_2$ is interpolated by solving a linear system. Alternatively, holding $x_2$ fixed, $W^{(2)}$ is updated via proximal gradient descent (PGD). The proximal mapping of the rank term $Gamma(W^{(2)} - W^{(1)})$ is approximated in linear time using a fast orthogonal matching pursuit (OMP) algorithm that selects a sparse combination of atoms from a dictionary $cR$ formed by the outer products of $W^{(1)}$'s eigenvectors. We unroll iterations of our algorithm into layers to build a lightweight neural network for limited data-driven parameter tuning. Experiments show that our joint optimization achieves better signal interpolation compared to existing time-varying graph models.

AI.tldr: This paper proposes a joint optimization framework for interpolating signals and estimating time-varying graph adjacency matrices by modeling the change between consecutive graphs as low-rank, solved via alternating optimization with OMP-based proximal mapping and unrolled into a lightweight neural network.
AI.motivation: Static spatial graphs are insufficient for spatial-temporal data where node-to-node similarities evolve over time. The authors aim to model slowly time-varying pairwise relationships in graphs.
AI.method: The change between two consecutive adjacency matrices is modeled as a low-rank matrix. Alternating optimization is used: fixing W(2) to interpolate x2 via linear system, and fixing x2 to update W(2) via proximal gradient descent, where the proximal mapping of the rank term is approximated using fast OMP with a dictionary from W(1)'s eigenvectors. Iterations are unrolled into a lightweight neural network.
AI.result: The joint optimization achieves better signal interpolation compared to existing time-varying graph models.
AI.conclusion: The proposed approach effectively combines graph signal smoothness and low-rank priors for time-varying graphs, and the unrolled network enables limited data-driven parameter tuning.

title: Are Safety Guarantees in Neural Networks Safe? How to Compute Trustworthy Robustness Certifications
summary: A primary challenge in AI safety is the existence of adversarial examples -- slightly distorted inputs that cause a neural network (NN) to misclassify. To mitigate this problem, recent research focuses on the computation of robustness certifications, which, for a given input, determine the largest distortion the input may receive without breaking the network's prediction. Robustness certifications can be interpreted as an axis-aligned hyper-rectangle (multi-dimensional intervals). Most existing approaches focus on maximizing the certification's volume, but recent intractability results prohibit the computation of volume-optimal certifications in reasonable time. We introduce the apothem measure and show how to compute apothem-optimal certifications in a linear number of calls to a NN verifier (oracle) w.r.t. the input domain's diameter. Moreover, we prove that we cannot have a volume-optimal, oracle-based algorithm, even if we discard the oracle costs. Also, we introduce dual certifications -- an interval including all instances of a class -- thus providing apothem-minimum upper bounds to a robustness certification. Further, we present the ParallelepipedoNN system, which we evaluate on the standard MNIST and Fashion MNIST benchmarks. A preliminary comparison with existing work on the same datasets reveals at least two-fold improvement w.r.t. the minimum edge length.

AI.tldr: The paper introduces the apothem measure for computing optimal robustness certifications for neural networks, proving it can be computed in linear time (vs. intractable volume-optimal approaches), and presents the ParallelepipedoNN system achieving two-fold improvement on MNIST/Fashion MNIST.
AI.motivation: Adversarial examples are a major AI safety challenge. Existing robustness certifications focus on volume-optimal hyper-rectangles, but recent results show computing volume-optimal certifications is intractable in reasonable time.
AI.method: Introduces the apothem measure for robustness certifications. Proposes an oracle-based algorithm that computes apothem-optimal certifications with linear complexity (w.r.t. input domain diameter). Introduces dual certifications for apothem-minimum upper bounds. Implements the ParallelepipedoNN system.
AI.result: The apothem-optimal certification can be computed in a linear number of NN verifier calls. Proves no volume-optimal oracle-based algorithm exists (even ignoring oracle costs). ParallelepipedoNN achieves at least two-fold improvement in minimum edge length on MNIST and Fashion MNIST.
AI.conclusion: Apothem-optimality is a practical alternative to volume-optimality for NN robustness certifications, with provable computational efficiency and strong empirical performance.

title: DriveStack-VLA: Render-Teacher Alignment for BEV-Based DeepStack Vision-Language-Action Model
summary: Vision-Language-Action driving models convert a pretrained Vision-Language Model into a driving policy, allowing them to use world knowledge and follow language guidances. However, existing VLA driving models still lack driving-oriented spatial intelligence: their policies are mainly grounded on perspective image tokens and language priors, while precise motion planning requires metric geometry, top-down scene structure, and attention to safety-critical perceptual cues. This limitation makes current models vulnerable to weak visual geometry modeling and perceptual coverage in expert demonstrations. In this paper, we present DriveStack-VLA, a framework built upon a large VLM backbone. To strengthen the spatial grounding of VLA driving, we develop dual visual modeling components. We inject a Bird-Eye-View representation into the Large Language Model decoder through a DeepStack-style connection, and propose Render-Teacher Alignment to align the perceptual focus of real images with that of rasterized images. Furthermore, to bridge the gap in multimodal trajectory selection, we introduce a head-based self-critique module that ranks sampled trajectories and conditionally refines the best one. DriveStack-VLA achieves 91.6 PDMS on NAVSIMv1, 91.0 EPDMS on NAVSIMv2 (with the human penalty filter enabled), and a driving score of 79.49 with a success rate of 56.36\% on the closed-loop Bench2Drive. More visualizations are available on our project page: https://anonymous.4open.science/w/drivestack-vla/.

AI.tldr: DriveStack-VLA enhances VLA driving models with dual visual modeling (BEV + Render-Teacher Alignment) and a self-critique module for trajectory refinement, achieving state-of-the-art results on NAVSIM and Bench2Drive benchmarks.
AI.motivation: Existing VLA driving models lack driving-oriented spatial intelligence, relying on perspective image tokens and language priors while ignoring metric geometry, top-down scene structure, and safety-critical perceptual cues, making them vulnerable to weak geometry modeling.
AI.method: (1) Dual visual modeling: BEV representation injected into LLM decoder via DeepStack-style connection, plus Render-Teacher Alignment to align perceptual focus of real and rasterized images. (2) Head-based self-critique module for multimodal trajectory selection that ranks sampled trajectories and conditionally refines the best one.
AI.result: 91.6 PDMS on NAVSIMv1, 91.0 EPDMS on NAVSIMv2, and driving score 79.49 with 56.36% success rate on closed-loop Bench2Drive.
AI.conclusion: Strengthening spatial grounding in VLA driving models through BEV injection and perceptual alignment significantly improves driving performance and safety.

title: OmniPath: A Multi-Modal Agentic Framework for Auditing Wheelchair Accessibility
comment: 10 pages, 13 figures. Submitted to IEEE COMPSAC 2026. OmniPath: A Multi-Modal Agentic Framework for Auditing Wheelchair Accessibility
summary: For a wheelchair user, a standard blue line on a map is often a broken promise. While platforms like OpenStreetMap (OSM) successfully capture where a path is, they frequently fail to convey how it physically feels to travel on it. This information barrier is problematic for wheelchair users. To solve this issue, we present OmniPath, a system that moves from passive mapping to proactive environmental auditing. Our framework fuses the network topology of OSM with the submeter precision of high-density aerial LiDAR (USGS 3DEP) to create a high-fidelity 3D model of the pedestrian environment. Rather than simply routing a user, our agent virtually traverses the network, analyzing the surface in 0.5 meter increments. It rigorously quantifies physical friction points specifically running slope, cross slope, and vertical discontinuities against ADA compliance standards, calculating a weighted severity score to categorize hazards from ``Mild'' to ``Critical.'' To ensure real world reliability, we validated the system against 200 physical ground truth field surveys across the National Mall using stratified random sampling. The framework demonstrated strong diagnostic reliability for high-severity hazards, achieving F1-scores of 0.60 for Severe and 0.58 for critical categories. By automating this micro-scale inspection, OmniPath identifies the ``invisible'' barriers that standard maps miss, effectively transforming a static dataset into accessibility data source that anticipates accessibility challenges before the user ever leaves home.

AI.tldr: OmniPath is a system that fuses OpenStreetMap data with high-density aerial LiDAR to create a 3D pedestrian model, then virtually traverses paths in 0.5m increments to quantify physical friction points (slope, cross slope, vertical discontinuities) against ADA standards, validated against 200 ground truth surveys on the National Mall.
AI.motivation: Standard maps (even OSM) fail to convey how a path physically feels to wheelchair users; the "blue line on a map is often a broken promise" because it captures location but not surface conditions, creating problematic information barriers for wheelchair users.
AI.method: The framework fuses OSM network topology with USGS 3DEP aerial LiDAR data (submeter precision) to create a high-fidelity 3D model. An agent virtually traverses the network in 0.5m increments, quantifying running slope, cross slope, and vertical discontinuities against ADA compliance standards, and computes a weighted severity score (Mild to Critical). Validation uses 200 stratified random ground truth surveys on the National Mall.
AI.result: Strong diagnostic reliability for high-severity hazards: F1-scores of 0.60 (Severe) and 0.58 (Critical) categories based on 200 field surveys.
AI.conclusion: OmniPath automates micro-scale inspection to identify "invisible" barriers that standard maps miss, transforming static datasets into proactive accessibility data that anticipates challenges before the user leaves home.

title: Legible and Intuitive Multi-modal Robot State and Intent Communication Validated in Online and Real-world Studies
comment: Accepted for publication at the 35th IEEE International Conference on Robot and Human Interactive Communication (RO-MAN 2026)
summary: Effective robot-to-human communication can increase transparency and trust, reduce uncertainty, and contribute to safer collaboration in shared workspaces. Designing and validating an effective robot communication strategy is challenging due to the varying and often limited communication modalities across robots, differences in how diverse recipients interpret messages, and the underexplored virtual-to-real gap in studies of communication legibility. We present a systematic, large-scale comparative validation of existing communication strategies for a mobile non-humanoid robot across message types and settings (online and in-person). Based on the prescribed message types in the existing standards for industrial robots, we realize and compare a low-expressive, unimodal LED-based strategy with a highly expressive, multimodal one that leverages robotic gaze, gestures, and voice. For each strategy, we analyze the communication of a turning intention, an attention request, error status, whether the robot is stuck, and whether it is functioning normally. We evaluate these strategies in replicated online and in-person experiments. We find strong evidence that highly expressive multimodal communication is perceived as more legible and intuitive than unimodal LED-based communication. Comparing the online and real-world study findings, we observe a notable decrease in overall legibility, particularly for signaling with LEDs. Similarly, confidence in message interpretation decreases during the real-world evaluation.

AI.tldr: This paper systematically compares unimodal LED-based vs. highly expressive multimodal (gaze, gestures, voice) robot-to-human communication strategies across message types and settings (online vs. in-person), finding multimodal communication more legible and intuitive, and revealing a significant virtual-to-real gap where legibility and confidence decrease in real-world settings.
AI.motivation: Effective robot-to-human communication is crucial for transparency, trust, and safety, but designing such strategies is challenging due to varying robot modalities, diverse recipient interpretations, and the underexplored virtual-to-real gap.
AI.method: Systematic large-scale comparative validation of existing communication strategies (unimodal LED vs. multimodal gaze/gestures/voice) for a mobile non-humanoid robot, evaluating communication of five message types (turning intention, attention request, error status, stuck, functioning normally) in replicated online and in-person experiments.
AI.result: Highly expressive multimodal communication was perceived as more legible and intuitive than unimodal LED-based communication. Real-world legibility and confidence decreased notably compared to online settings, especially for LED signaling.
AI.conclusion: Multimodal strategies outperform unimodal ones for robot-to-human communication, but researchers should account for significant virtual-to-real gaps, as results from online studies may overestimate real-world legibility and user confidence.

title: A Conditional Timing Protection Level: Holdover-Limited Undetected Time Error Under GNSS Spoofing
comment: 8 pages, 5 figures, 2 tables. Calibrated on the public JammerTest 2024 dataset (Zenodo 10.5281/zenodo.15911589). TPL primitives, CUSUM detector, and calibration example open source in the Kshana simulator (AGPL-3.0)
summary: A GNSS timing receiver under spoofing has no nominal-geometry fault for position-domain RAIM to bound: the threat is a slow, common-mode pull of served clock time that the receiver's own time-accuracy flag need not reveal. We make three graded contributions. First, a field measurement: solving the receiver clock trajectory from raw L1 pseudoranges and broadcast ephemeris, we show a recorded over-the-air spoof from the public JammerTest 2024 campaign pulled a u-blox ZED-F9P by about 1.01 ms of served time while it reported at most 51 ns, a gap near 20,000x. Second, an impossibility: against an adversary free to choose the ramp rate, no finite unconditional bound on undetected time error exists under a single self-referential clock-aided monitor, because a ramp slow enough to keep the disciplined reference in lock-step is never alarmed while the error grows without limit, so any finite guarantee is conditional. Third, the conditional bound: the Timing Protection Level (TPL), a model-free monitor's static detectability floor plus the oscillator's coast over the detection latency, holds given detection by an independent cross-satellite consistency check a coherent spoofer does not drive in lock-step. Each term is a closed form over a primitive verified in the open Kshana simulator, so the sum is reproducible by hand. Calibrated on the recorded attack, the budget is 114 ns at one-second recovery and 458 ns at a 60-second coast, thousands of times below the 1.01 ms accepted; a clock-aided sequential test alone gives essentially no protection on this slow ramp (it alarms only near the ~1 ms capture), while the model-free monitor alarms during the ramp. We are explicit: the bound is calibrated, not field-validated; carries no integrity-risk budget; and is reported as a band at long coast. The simulator, bound, and calibration example are open source under AGPL-3.0.

AI.tldr: This paper addresses GNSS timing receiver vulnerabilities under spoofing attacks. Through field measurements, it shows a 20,000x gap between actual time error (~1.01 ms) and reported error (51 ns). It proves no finite unconditional bound exists for undetected time error under a single self-referential clock monitor. It proposes a Timing Protection Level (TPL) — a conditional bound combining a model-free monitor's detectability floor and oscillator coast — that provides protection orders of magnitude better than clock-aided sequential tests alone.
AI.motivation: GNSS timing receivers under spoofing lack nominal-geometry faults that position-domain RAIM can bound. The threat is a slow, common-mode clock time pull that the receiver's own time-accuracy flag may not reveal. The authors aim to characterize this vulnerability and propose a method to bound undetected time errors.
AI.method: The paper makes three contributions: (1) Field measurement using raw L1 pseudoranges and broadcast ephemeris from a recorded over-the-air spoofing attack (JammerTest 2024 campaign) on a u-blox ZED-F9P; (2) Impossibility proof showing no finite unconditional bound exists under a single self-referential clock-aided monitor; (3) Proposal of the Timing Protection Level (TPL), a conditional bound combining a model-free monitor's static detectability floor plus oscillator coast over detection latency, verified in the open Kshana simulator.
AI.result: The recorded spoofing attack pulled the receiver's served time by ~1.01 ms while it reported at most 51 ns — a ~20,000x gap. The TPL provides 114 ns at one-second recovery and 458 ns at 60-second coast, thousands of times below the 1.01 ms attack. A clock-aided sequential test alone alarms only near ~1 ms capture, while the model-free monitor alarms during the ramp.
AI.conclusion: The bound is calibrated, not field-validated; carries no integrity-risk budget; and is reported as a band at long coast. The simulator, bound, and calibration example are open source under AGPL-3.0. The work explicitly acknowledges limitations while providing practical protection against slow-ramp spoofing attacks.

title: EPEdit: Redefining Image Editing with Generative AI and User-Centric Design
summary: The demand for image manipulation has seen a significant increase recently. Traditional tools like Photoshop and Capture One, while powerful, require considerable expertise to use effectively. Generative AI has introduced alternative platforms, such as Luminar Neo, Pixlr X, and Canva. However, many of these solutions, including resource-heavy models like Stable Diffusion, often require substantial retraining and fine-tuning, leading to high costs for users. To address these challenges, we introduce Efficient Photo Editor (EPEdit), an application that integrates a robust backend framework with a user-friendly front-end interface. EPEdit supports a wide range of creative image editing tasks, including image generation, object replacement, object removal, background modification, changes in object pose or perspective, region-specific editing, and thematic collection design, all guided by masks and prompts. Users can interact with the system through simple text commands or by marking areas for precise adjustments, making it accessible even to those without technical expertise. At its core, EPEdit leverages zero-shot image editing algorithms based on Stable Diffusion model, removing the need for additional fine-tuning. This approach enables efficient image manipulation and thematic collection creation. User evaluations for tasks of image editing, thematic design, and overall system performance demonstrate that EPEdit outperforms existing solutions, offering a user-friendly, cost-effective solution for comprehensive image editing.

AI.tldr: EPEdit is a user-friendly photo editor that combines zero-shot Stable Diffusion-based algorithms with a simple interface, enabling efficient image manipulation without costly fine-tuning.
AI.motivation: Existing image editing tools (Photoshop, Capture One) require expertise, while generative AI solutions like Stable Diffusion need costly retraining. The goal is to provide a cost-effective, accessible alternative for non-experts.
AI.method: EPEdit integrates a backend framework with zero-shot image editing algorithms based on Stable Diffusion, eliminating fine-tuning. Users interact via text commands or masks for precise region-specific edits.
AI.result: User evaluations for image editing, thematic design, and overall system performance show EPEdit outperforms existing solutions in usability and cost-effectiveness.
AI.conclusion: EPEdit offers a user-friendly, cost-effective, and efficient solution for comprehensive image editing, accessible to non-technical users.

title: T2D-Bench: Evidence-Gated Evaluation of LLM Outputs for Type 2 Diabetes Using a Multi-Layer Clinical-Lifestyle Knowledge Graph
comment: 7 pages, 2 figures, 2 tables. Accepted as a poster at AMIA 2026 Annual Symposium
summary: Large language models (LLMs) can produce clinically fluent recommendations for type 2 diabetes while failing to satisfy guideline constraints or explicitly justify lifestyle-related glycemic claims. We present T2D-Bench, a reproducible benchmark and evidence-gated evaluation framework for testing whether LLM outputs satisfy explicit, graph-checkable evidence requirements. T2D-Bench is built on a multi-layer clinical-lifestyle knowledge graph that combines a biomedical spine (UMLS, DrugBank, SIDER), computable ADA Standards of Care rules, and lifestyle knowledge connected through a mechanistic bridge to glycemic laboratory effects. Across 100 structured vignettes spanning diagnosis, medication safety, and adversarial lifestyle conflicts, baseline outputs failed benchmark-defined evidence-path checks in 35% of cases for GPT-4o-mini and 33% for GPT-4o. The evidence gate detects unsupported omissions and uses constrained revision to bring outputs into verifier-level compliance with benchmark-defined evidence requirements. These results show that computable evidence constraints can make unsupported clinical omissions explicit, measurable, and correctable in diabetes-focused LLM outputs.

AI.tldr: T2D-Bench is a benchmark and evaluation framework that tests whether LLM outputs satisfy evidence requirements for type 2 diabetes, using a multi-layer knowledge graph. Baseline LLMs failed evidence-path checks in 33-35% of cases, and the framework's evidence gate detects and corrects unsupported omissions.
AI.motivation: LLMs can generate clinically fluent but guideline-noncompliant recommendations for type 2 diabetes, making it necessary to systematically detect and measure unsupported clinical omissions.
AI.method: T2D-Bench combines a multi-layer clinical-lifestyle knowledge graph (UMLS, DrugBank, SIDER, ADA Standards of Care rules, lifestyle-laboratory mechanistic bridges) with an evidence-gated evaluation framework that detects unsupported omissions and applies constrained revision for compliance.
AI.result: GPT-4o-mini failed evidence-path checks in 35% of cases and GPT-4o in 33% across 100 vignettes spanning diagnosis, medication safety, and adversarial lifestyle conflicts.
AI.conclusion: Computable evidence constraints can make unsupported clinical omissions explicit, measurable, and correctable in diabetes-focused LLM outputs.

title: Supervise What Survives: Geometry-Guided VLA Adaptation from Synthetic Robot Videos
summary: Vision-Language-Action (VLA) models require large-scale video-action pairs, yet real teleoperation remains scarce. While generated robot videos offer a scalable alternative, existing methods treat them as real robot data by recovering pseudo-actions from synthesized pixels. We argue that deriving low-level control from generated visuals is a mismatched abstraction. A video captures only \emph{geometry}: the spatial trajectory representing the \emph{where} of a task. A real demonstration captures \emph{control}: the exact motor commands representing the \emph{how}. Human-to-robot video generation preserves these unequally: the visible geometry survives the generation process, while the underlying control signals are lost. This \textbf{Asymmetric Preservation Principle} dictates a clean rule: this surviving geometry should solely supervise visual perception, leaving control to real demonstrations. Following this principle, we propose \textbf{GRA} (\textbf{G}eometry-guided \textbf{R}epresentation \textbf{A}lignment), which extracts the geometric content as future 2D end-effector waypoints, computed from the source human video through pose estimation, retargeting, simulation, and calibrated projection, and routes them to the VLA vision backbone via an auxiliary 2D head. The action head is trained on real demonstrations only. During fine-tuning, the waypoint loss persists as a \textbf{spatial representation anchor} that prevents the backbone from losing its geometric grounding. On real-robot tasks, GRA outperforms pseudo-action baselines under matched data budgets and narrows the gap to policies trained with substantially more real demonstrations, suggesting that correctly routed geometry bridges generated videos to robot policies more reliably than recovered actions.

AI.tldr: This paper proposes GRA (Geometry-guided Representation Alignment), a method that uses generated robot videos to supervise only visual perception (via 2D waypoints) rather than recovering pseudo-actions, preserving the geometric content while leaving control to real demonstrations. It outperforms pseudo-action baselines and narrows the gap to policies trained with more real data.
AI.motivation: VLA models need large-scale video-action pairs but real teleoperation data is scarce. Generated robot videos are a scalable alternative, but existing methods incorrectly try to recover low-level control signals (actions) from generated visuals, creating a mismatch between what video generation preserves (geometry) and what they try to extract (control).
AI.method: GRA extracts geometric content as future 2D end-effector waypoints from human videos via pose estimation, retargeting, simulation, and calibrated projection. These waypoints are routed to the VLA vision backbone through an auxiliary 2D head, while the action head is trained only on real demonstrations. During fine-tuning, the waypoint loss acts as a spatial representation anchor to maintain geometric grounding.
AI.result: GRA outperforms pseudo-action baselines under matched data budgets and narrows the gap to policies trained with substantially more real demonstrations on real-robot tasks.
AI.conclusion: Correctly routed geometry bridges generated videos to robot policies more reliably than recovered actions, validating the Asymmetric Preservation Principle that generated videos should supervise perception (geometry) while real data should supervise control (actions).

title: PROTECT-90: A Fault Dataset for Power System Protection
comment: 6 pages, 3 figures, 3 tables. Accepted for publication at IEEE PES ISGT Europe 2026. Author accepted manuscript. Final published version will be available via IEEE Xplore
summary: The increasing interest in data-driven methods for power system protection is accompanied by a lack of standardized, publicly available high-voltage waveform datasets that enable transparent and reproducible evaluation. To address this gap, this paper introduces the PROTECT-90 dataset, an open electromagnetic transient (EMT)-simulated reference benchmark for high-voltage fault studies with consistent digital-fault-recorder-like measurements, publicly released with this work. The dataset comprises 9,022 physically consistent short-circuit simulation episodes generated on a standardized 90 kV double-line topology with systematically documented domain randomization of grid operating points, line parameters, and fault conditions. For each episode, synchronized three-phase voltage and current waveforms are recorded at eight measurement locations and released together with structured, machine-readable metadata describing fault type, fault location, inception time, and operating conditions. All modeling assumptions, parameter ranges, and data-generation procedures are explicitly documented to ensure transparency and cross-study comparability. By combining physically grounded EMT simulation, balanced scenario coverage, and open accessibility, PROTECT-90 establishes a standardized foundation for reproducible benchmarking of protection-oriented signal processing and learning-based methods.

AI.tldr: The PROTECT-90 dataset provides 9,022 open EMT-simulated fault waveforms on a standardized 90 kV double-line topology with systematic documentation, enabling reproducible benchmarking for power system protection methods.
AI.motivation: Current data-driven approaches for power system protection lack standardized, publicly available high-voltage waveform datasets for transparent and reproducible evaluation.
AI.method: Creates an open EMT-simulated reference benchmark (PROTECT-90) with 9,022 physically consistent short-circuit episodes on a 90 kV double-line topology, using systematic domain randomization of operating points, line parameters, and fault conditions, with synchronized three-phase measurements at eight locations.
AI.result: A publicly available dataset of 9,022 fault episodes with structured metadata, documented modeling assumptions, and balanced scenario coverage for reproducible benchmarking.
AI.conclusion: PROTECT-90 establishes a standardized, open foundation for reproducible comparison of protection-oriented signal processing and learning-based methods in power systems.

title: Federated Survival Analysis in Healthcare: A Multi-Model Evaluation on Cross-Institutional Heterogeneous Breast Cancer Data
summary: Survival analysis is central to clinical decision-making, yet reliable time-to-event models require large, diverse cohorts that are rarely available at a single institution, while privacy regulations restrict the centralization of patient data. Federated learning (FL) offers a privacy-preserving alternative by training shared models without exchanging raw data, but its effectiveness for survival modeling under realistic, heterogeneous conditions remains insufficiently understood. This paper presents a systematic, multi-model evaluation of federated survival analysis on a cross-institutional breast cancer cohort with naturally heterogeneous distributed clients. Three representative survival models, the Cox Proportional Hazards model, DeepSurv, and Random Survival Forest (RSF), are compared across centralized, local, and federated training, and three federated optimization strategies (FedAvg, FedProx, and FedAdam) are assessed for the gradient-based models. Results show that FL consistently outperforms local training and approaches, and occasionally exceeds, centralized performance, while RSF offers the best overall balance of discrimination, calibration, and robustness across heterogeneous clients. We further find that performance depends on the diversity of client distributions, and that FedAvg and FedProx are stronger and more stable than FedAdam. Based on these findings, we derive practical, decision-oriented guidelines mapping data, privacy, interpretability, and resource constraints to recommended model and training-paradigm choices for federated survival modeling in healthcare.

AI.tldr: A systematic evaluation of federated survival analysis on a cross-institutional breast cancer cohort, comparing three models (Cox, DeepSurv, RSF) and three FL strategies (FedAvg, FedProx, FedAdam). FL outperforms local training, RSF performs best overall, and FedAvg/FedProx are more stable than FedAdam. Practical guidelines for model selection are provided.
AI.motivation: Time-to-event models require large, diverse datasets rarely available at single institutions, but privacy regulations prevent centralizing patient data. FL offers a privacy-preserving alternative, but its effectiveness for survival modeling under realistic heterogeneous conditions was insufficiently understood.
AI.method: Evaluated three survival models (Cox PH, DeepSurv, RSF) under centralized, local, and federated training on a naturally heterogeneous cross-institutional breast cancer cohort. Compared three FL optimization strategies (FedAvg, FedProx, FedAdam) for gradient-based models.
AI.result: FL consistently outperforms local training and approaches or exceeds centralized performance. RSF offers the best discrimination, calibration, and robustness. FedAvg and FedProx are stronger and more stable than FedAdam. Performance depends on client distribution diversity.
AI.conclusion: Derived practical guidelines mapping data, privacy, interpretability, and resource constraints to recommended model and training-paradigm choices for federated survival modeling in healthcare.

title: VisChronos: Revolutionizing Image Captioning Through Real-Life Events
summary: This paper aims to bridge the semantic gap between visual content and natural language understanding by leveraging historical events in the real world as a source of knowledge for caption generation. We propose VisChronos, a novel framework that utilizes large language models and dense captioning models to identify and describe real-life events from a single input image. Our framework can automatically generate detailed and context-aware event descriptions, enhancing the descriptive quality and contextual relevance of generated captions to address the limitations of traditional methods in capturing contextual narratives. Furthermore, we introduce a new dataset, EventCap (https://zenodo.org/records/14004909), specifically constructed using the proposed framework, designed to enhance the model's ability to identify and understand complex events. The user study demonstrates the efficacy of our solution in generating accurate, coherent, and event-focused descriptions, paving the way for future research in event-centric image understanding.

AI.tldr: VisChronos bridges the semantic gap between images and language by using LLMs and dense captioning models to generate event-aware captions based on real-world historical knowledge, supported by a new EventCap dataset.
AI.motivation: Traditional image captioning methods fail to capture contextual narratives and semantic meaning behind visual content; there is a semantic gap between what is seen and what is understood in context.
AI.method: VisChronos framework combines large language models (LLMs) with dense captioning models to identify and describe real-life events from a single input image, leveraging historical real-world knowledge for caption generation. A new dataset, EventCap, is constructed using this framework.
AI.result: The framework generates detailed, context-aware, and event-focused descriptions. User study confirms accuracy, coherence, and relevance of generated captions.
AI.conclusion: VisChronos effectively enhances event-centric image understanding and sets a foundation for future research in context-rich, narrative-driven captioning.

title: The Geometry Behind Diffusion and Flow Matching: Gradient Flows and Geodesics in Wasserstein Space
summary: The space $\mathcal{P}_2(\mathbb{R}^d$) of probability measures with finite second moment carries a natural geometry: the quadratic Wasserstein distance W_2 makes it a complete metric space and, following Otto, a (formal) Riemannian manifold whose geodesics are the optimal-transport interpolations. On this manifold, the gradient flow of the free energy F(rho) = KL(rho || π) is exactly the Fokker-Planck equation, and its implicit-Euler discretization is the JKO scheme. This is the geometry underlying diffusion models: the forward process descends the free energy, and each denoising step realizes one JKO step, which recovers DDPM, DDIM, NCSN/SMLD, and Energy Matching; this is one scheme, not separate theories. The same manifold supports a second variational principle. Its geodesics - the minimum-action curves of the Benamou-Brenier formula - are precisely the optimal-transport paths that Flow Matching learns. Fixing both endpoints and following the geodesic, generation becomes a deterministic ODE along a straight line, hence far fewer sampling steps. Placing both families of models on one manifold makes their relationship exact: diffusion follows a free-energy gradient flow, an initial-value problem; optimal-transport Flow Matching follows a Wasserstein geodesic, a boundary-value problem. The two reach the same endpoints along different paths.

AI.tldr: This paper unifies diffusion models (DDPM, DDIM, NCSN/SMLD, Energy Matching) and Flow Matching on the Wasserstein manifold: diffusion models solve free-energy gradient flows (Fokker-Planck via JKO scheme), while Flow Matching solves optimal-transport geodesics (Benamou-Brenier). Both reach the same target distribution via different paths—initial-value vs. boundary-value problems.
AI.motivation: To provide a unified geometric framework explaining the relationship between diffusion models and Flow Matching, showing they are not separate theories but different variational principles on the same Wasserstein manifold.
AI.method: The paper identifies $\mathcal{P}_2(\mathbb{R}^d)$ with the quadratic Wasserstein distance as a Riemannian manifold, then shows: (1) diffusion models correspond to gradient flow of KL divergence (Fokker-Planck equation, JKO scheme), and (2) Flow Matching corresponds to geodesic/optimal-transport paths (Benamou-Brenier formula).
AI.result: DDPM, DDIM, NCSN/SMLD, and Energy Matching are all unified as one JKO scheme. Flow Matching learns deterministic ODEs along Wasserstein geodesics, requiring fewer sampling steps.
AI.conclusion: Diffusion and Flow Matching are two different variational principles on the same Wasserstein geometry: an initial-value problem (gradient flow) vs. a boundary-value problem (geodesic), both reaching the same target distribution.

title: NoContactNoWorries: Estimating Contact through Vision and Proprioception for In-Hand Dexterous Manipulation
comment: Accepted to IEEE/RSJ International Conference on Intelligent Robots and Systems(IROS) 2026
summary: Perceiving physical contact is fundamental to dexterous manipulation. While robots often rely on dedicated hardware tactile sensors, humans exhibit a remarkable ability to infer contact by integrating visual information with an innate sense of their body's pose and movement. Inspired by this embodied perceptual skill, we investigate whether a robot can learn to infer contact from vision, an approach that also offers a scalable alternative to tactile hardware specifically for binary contact estimation, which faces practical challenges in cost, fragility, and integration. We present NoContactNoWorries, a transformer-based multimodal framework that fuses RGB-D vision with the robot's proprioception to infer binary contact states as a pseudo-tactile signal for hand-object interactions. We validate by training a single contact prediction model on multiple objects and show that the inferred contact signal supports downstream reinforcement learning agents for in-hand object reorientation, generalizing to novel objects. Experiments in both simulation and on a real-world robot validate our approach, highlighting the feasibility of inferring contact from vision and proprioception. Project Page: https://soham2560.github.io/no-contact-no-worries/

AI.tldr: NoContactNoWorries is a transformer-based framework that infers binary hand-object contact states from RGB-D vision and robot proprioception, eliminating the need for dedicated tactile sensors. It supports downstream reinforcement learning for in-hand object reorientation and generalizes to novel objects in both simulation and real-world settings.
AI.motivation: Tactile sensors face practical challenges in cost, fragility, and integration. Inspired by humans' ability to infer contact from vision and proprioception, the authors aim to develop a scalable, hardware-free alternative for binary contact estimation in dexterous manipulation.
AI.method: A transformer-based multimodal framework (NoContactNoWorries) that fuses RGB-D vision with robot proprioception to predict binary contact states as pseudo-tactile signals. A single model is trained on multiple objects and used to provide contact feedback to downstream RL agents for in-hand reorientation tasks.
AI.result: The approach successfully predicts contact states from vision and proprioception, supports downstream RL for in-hand object reorientation, and generalizes to novel objects. Validation conducted in both simulation and on a real-world robot.
AI.conclusion: Inferring contact from vision and proprioception is a feasible and scalable alternative to dedicated tactile hardware for binary contact estimation in dexterous manipulation tasks.

title: Explainable AI for Next-Generation Wireless Physical Layer: Basics, State-of-the-Art, and Open Challenges
summary: Next-generation wireless systems are expected to be ``AI-native," with neural networks (NNs) embedded throughout the physical (PHY) layer protocol stack to improve spectral efficiency, latency, and network autonomy. However, the opacity of deep learning (DL) models raises increasing concerns about system reliability, safety, and privacy, especially under complex and time-varying network environments. This survey studies explainable AI (XAI) in wireless PHY layers from the explainability perspective. We first formalize a series of responsibility-oriented goals for wireless XAI. Then, we develop a systematic taxonomy of explainability approaches and distill practical criteria for deploying explanations in communication scenarios. We provide a comprehensive review of where and how XAI can be applied throughout the PHY layer, connecting representative learning paradigms to appropriate explanation techniques, evaluation metrics, and deployment considerations. Open challenges and future directions are discussed, including explainability-performance tradeoffs, explainability-aware data processing, customized XAI for communication-specific structures, cross-layer explanation consistency, and emerging needs for explaining LLM- and Agentic-AI-driven PHY layers.

AI.tldr: This survey explores explainable AI (XAI) for next-generation wireless PHY layers, proposing a responsibility-oriented goal framework, a taxonomy of explainability methods, and practical deployment criteria, while identifying key challenges such as explainability-performance tradeoffs, cross-layer consistency, and the need for XAI in LLM- and agentic-AI-driven systems.
AI.motivation: As wireless systems become "AI-native" with NNs embedded throughout the PHY layer, deep learning models' opacity raises concerns about reliability, safety, and privacy in complex, time-varying environments, motivating the need for explainable AI to ensure responsible deployment.
AI.method: The paper formalizes responsibility-oriented goals for wireless XAI, develops a systematic taxonomy of explainability approaches, distills practical deployment criteria, and provides a comprehensive review connecting learning paradigms to explanation techniques, evaluation metrics, and deployment considerations across the PHY layer.
AI.result: A structured taxonomy and mapping of XAI techniques to PHY-layer communication scenarios, along with practical criteria for deploying explanations in wireless systems.
AI.conclusion: Open challenges remain, including explainability-performance tradeoffs, explainability-aware data processing, customized XAI for communication-specific structures, cross-layer explanation consistency, and emerging needs for explaining LLM- and Agentic-AI-driven PHY layers.

title: MGI: Member vs Generated Inference
summary: As generative models increasingly produce samples that are indistinguishable from human-created content, it becomes difficult to determine whether a given data point was part of a model's natural training set or was generated by the model itself, especially when models memorize and reproduce training data. We formalize this challenge as Member vs Generated Inference (MGI): given a sample and a target generative model, infer whether the sample is a true training member or a generated output of that model. Focusing on image generation, we show that existing membership inference methods systematically misclassify generated samples as training members, while attribution-based methods often misclassify true members as generated. This failure arises because both approaches rely on likelihood-related signals that are similarly elevated for training examples and for the model's own outputs. To address MGI, we propose Data Circuit Breaker (DCB), a three-stage method that combines complementary signals from a generative model's autoencoder and latent generator to distinguish training members from generated samples. Across multiple generative models, including image autoregressive and diffusion models, DCB consistently addresses the shortcomings of membership inference and attribution methods, remains effective even when models reproduce near-duplicates of training samples, and generalizes to challenging model derivative settings in which new models are trained on generated data.

AI.tldr: This paper formalizes the Member vs Generated Inference (MGI) problem and proposes Data Circuit Breaker (DCB), a three-stage method that combines signals from a model's autoencoder and latent generator to distinguish training members from generated samples, outperforming existing membership inference and attribution methods.
AI.motivation: Existing membership inference methods systematically misclassify generated samples as training members, while attribution methods misclassify true members as generated, because both rely on likelihood signals that are elevated for both training examples and model outputs.
AI.method: DCB (Data Circuit Breaker) is a three-stage method that combines complementary signals from a generative model's autoencoder and latent generator to distinguish training members from generated samples.
AI.result: DCB consistently outperforms existing methods across image autoregressive and diffusion models, remains effective even with near-duplicate outputs, and generalizes to model derivative settings where new models are trained on generated data.
AI.conclusion: The proposed DCB method effectively addresses the MGI challenge by leveraging combined signals, overcoming the fundamental limitations of likelihood-based approaches used in prior work.

title: Ingredient-Level Food Image Segmentation for Nutrition Awareness
summary: Food images often contain several visible ingredients, so assigning one dish label to an entire image hides important visual structure. This work studies ingredient-level semantic segmentation on FoodSeg103, where the model predicts an ingredient class for each pixel. Two SegFormer variants were fine-tuned and evaluated under a controlled setup: SegFormer-B0 as the smaller baseline model and SegFormer-B1 as the larger final model. Both models use ImageNet-pretrained MiT backbones with newly initialized 104-class output layers. On the held-out FoodSeg103 test split of 2,135 images, B0 achieved 0.7709 pixel accuracy and 0.2521 mean IoU, while B1 achieved 0.7929 pixel accuracy and 0.3204 mean IoU. B1 improved every saved test metric, including a +0.0683 absolute gain in mean IoU. The system also converts predicted masks into visible ingredient-area percentages, giving a simple visual composition summary of the predicted meal. This summary can serve as a first-pass nutrition-awareness cue by providing a visual alternative to detailed food tracking similar to plate-based meal guidance, but it is not a direct estimate of calories, macronutrients, food mass, volume, density, or true portion size.

AI.tldr: This paper studies ingredient-level semantic segmentation on FoodSeg103, fine-tuning SegFormer-B0 and SegFormer-B1 models. B1 achieved 0.7929 pixel accuracy and 0.3204 mean IoU, outperforming B0. The system converts predictions into ingredient-area percentages for visual nutrition-awareness cues.
AI.motivation: Food images contain multiple visible ingredients, but assigning a single dish label loses important visual structure. The authors aim to capture ingredient-level details through pixel-wise semantic segmentation.
AI.method: Two SegFormer variants (B0 and B1) with ImageNet-pretrained MiT backbones and newly initialized 104-class output layers were fine-tuned and evaluated on FoodSeg103 test split (2,135 images). Predicted masks are converted into ingredient-area percentages.
AI.result: B0: 0.7709 pixel accuracy, 0.2521 mean IoU. B1: 0.7929 pixel accuracy, 0.3204 mean IoU. B1 showed a +0.0683 absolute gain in mean IoU over B0 across all metrics.
AI.conclusion: The system provides a visual composition summary for nutrition-awareness cues as an alternative to detailed food tracking, but is not a direct estimate of calories, macronutrients, or portion size.

title: An Introduction to Causal Reinforcement Learning
summary: Causal inference provides a set of principles and tools that allow one to combine data and knowledge about an environment to reason with questions of counterfactual nature, i.e., what would have happened had reality been different, even when no data of this unrealized reality is currently available. Reinforcement learning provides methods to learn a policy that optimizes a specific measure (e.g., reward, regret) when the agent is deployed in an environment and pursues an exploratory, trial-and-error approach. These two disciplines have evolved independently and with virtually no interaction between them. We note that they operate over different aspects of the same building block, counterfactual relations, which makes them umbilically connected. Based on these observations, novel learning opportunities arise when this connection is explicitly acknowledged and mathematized. To realize this potential, we note that any environment where the RL agent is deployed can be decomposed as a collection of autonomous mechanisms with different causal invariances, parsimoniously modeled as a structural causal model; any standard RL setting implicitly encodes such a model. This formalization allows us to put under a unifying treatment different modes of learning, including online, off-policy, and causal calculus learning, which appear unrelated in the literature. However, these modalities are not exhaustive: we introduce several natural and pervasive classes of learning settings that entail novel dimensions of analysis. Specifically, we introduce and discuss through causal lenses generalized policy learning, where to intervene, imitation learning, and counterfactual learning. These tasks lead to a broader view of counterfactual learning and suggest great potential for studying causal inference and reinforcement learning side by side, which we call causal reinforcement learning (CRL).

AI.tldr: This paper bridges causal inference and reinforcement learning by framing RL environments as structural causal models, introducing a unified treatment of online, off-policy, and causal learning, and proposing novel CRL settings like generalized policy learning, imitation learning, and counterfactual learning.
AI.motivation: Causal inference and reinforcement learning have evolved independently despite both operating on counterfactual relations. The authors identify this gap and argue that explicitly acknowledging their connection unlocks novel learning opportunities.
AI.method: The paper decomposes any RL environment as a collection of autonomous mechanisms modeled as a structural causal model (SCM). This formalization unifies online, off-policy, and causal calculus learning, and introduces new learning modalities through a causal lens.
AI.result: Identifies that standard RL implicitly encodes an SCM. Introduces generalized policy learning, imitation learning under interventions, and counterfactual learning as new pervasive classes of learning settings.
AI.conclusion: Proposes causal reinforcement learning (CRL) as a broader framework that studies causal inference and RL side by side, opening new dimensions for analysis and learning beyond existing paradigms.

title: Varying Bundle Size Reactive Multi-Task Assignment using Selective Cost Estimation for Multi-Agent Systems
comment: Accepted for publication at the 23rd World Congress of the International Federation of Automatic Control (IFAC 2026)
summary: This paper presents a scalable framework for multi-robot task allocation in complex environments where estimating task execution costs is computationally expensive. While combinatorial auction-based approaches offer reliable solutions, the exponential complexity of bundle generation typically renders them intractable for real-time reactive applications, particularly when accurate path planning is required for cost validation. We address this through a distributed, two-stage multi-fidelity bundle generation approach. Agents utilize a local search tree guided by a low-fidelity heuristic (such as euclidean distance) to rapidly explore the bundle space, applying high-fidelity path planning only to the most promising candidates in a best-first manner. These refined bids are then submitted to a central coordinator that solves a set packing problem to ensure global feasibility and maximize the overall utility. Simulation results in multiple environments demonstrate that the framework is able to improve the performance of reactive auction-based task allocation. Overall, the presented framework is shown to enable reactive task allocation with dynamic bundle sizes in multiple settings without exposing the agents' state and internal cost estimation models.

AI.tldr: This paper proposes a scalable two-stage multi-fidelity bundle generation framework for multi-robot task allocation, using low-fidelity heuristics for rapid bundle exploration and high-fidelity path planning only for promising candidates, enabling reactive auction-based allocation with dynamic bundle sizes.
AI.motivation: Combinatorial auction-based multi-robot task allocation suffers from exponential complexity in bundle generation when accurate path planning is needed for cost estimation, making it intractable for real-time reactive applications.
AI.method: A distributed two-stage approach: (1) agents use a local search tree with low-fidelity heuristics (e.g., Euclidean distance) to explore bundle space rapidly, then apply high-fidelity path planning only to the most promising candidates in a best-first manner; (2) refined bids are submitted to a central coordinator solving a set packing problem for global feasibility and utility maximization.
AI.result: Simulation results across multiple environments show the framework improves the performance of reactive auction-based task allocation.
AI.conclusion: The framework enables reactive task allocation with dynamic bundle sizes without exposing agents' internal states or cost estimation models.

title: WiWorld-RealData: A Real-World Multi-Modal Dataset for 6G Wireless World Models
summary: Wireless world models aim to represent, predict, and reason about wireless propagation by jointly understanding physical environments and channel responses. Realizing such models in sixth-generation (6G) digital twin channels requires datasets that capture measured wireless responses and environment states under real-world propagation conditions. This paper presents WiWorld-RealData, a real-world outdoor multi-band channel and multi-modal sensing dataset collected along campus mobile routes. WiWorld-RealData provides measured channel impulse responses (CIRs) at 3.7 GHz and 6.775 GHz, together with multi-view images, panoramic images, light detection and ranging (LiDAR) point clouds, millimeter-wave (mmWave) radar records, and global navigation satellite system (GNSS) trajectories. Through unified file organization and metadata manifests, the dataset establishes sample-level correspondences among channel responses, environment observations, timestamps, route information, antenna configurations, and quality flags. The overall measurement campaign has produced 10 TB-level multi-modal field data. The current public release provides one representative dual-band route at 3.7 GHz and 6.775 GHz with complete channel-environment alignment, while the acquisition framework supports extension to more frequency bands and scenarios. A case study on environment-assisted path-loss prediction achieves a mean absolute error (MAE) of 2.02 dB and a root mean squared error (RMSE) of 2.69 dB, indicating that the aligned environment observations contain predictive information for channel variations. The dataset is available at https://scc.bupt.edu.cn/dataset-manage/datasets/44, and a ScienceDB mirror will be provided upon release.

AI.tldr: WiWorld-RealData is a large-scale, real-world outdoor multi-band channel and multi-modal sensing dataset (3.7 GHz & 6.775 GHz) with aligned CIRs, images, LiDAR, mmWave radar, and GNSS data for 6G digital twin channel research, achieving 2.02 dB MAE in environment-assisted path-loss prediction.
AI.motivation: To enable wireless world models that jointly understand physical environments and channel responses, there is a need for real-world datasets that capture both measured wireless responses and corresponding environment states under realistic propagation conditions.
AI.method: The dataset was collected along campus mobile routes, providing channel impulse responses at 3.7 GHz and 6.775 GHz, paired with multi-view images, panoramic images, LiDAR point clouds, mmWave radar records, and GNSS trajectories. Unified file organization and metadata manifests establish sample-level correspondences among all modalities.
AI.result: 10 TB-level multi-modal field data collected; public release provides one representative dual-band route with complete channel-environment alignment. A case study on environment-assisted path-loss prediction achieves MAE of 2.02 dB and RMSE of 2.69 dB.
AI.conclusion: The dataset demonstrates that aligned environment observations contain predictive information for channel variations. The acquisition framework supports extension to more frequency bands and scenarios, supporting 6G digital twin channel research.

title: GRACE: Gated Refinement for Accurate Causal Edge Discovery in High-Dimensional Time Series
summary: From climate teleconnections to gene regulation, modern time-series datasets encompass tens or hundreds of interacting variables, making causal discovery increasingly challenging. Constraint-based methods offer statistical rigor but their nonlinear CI tests are infeasible at scale, while score-based alternatives avoid CI testing but require arbitrary thresholds to binarize continuous edge scores. We propose GRACE ($\textbf{G}$ated $\textbf{R}$efinement for $\textbf{A}$ccurate $\textbf{C}$ausal $\textbf{E}$dge discovery), which refines constraint-based discovery using Hard Concrete gates with $L_0$ regularization: each candidate edge has an independent gate whose values concentrate near 0 or 1, yielding a clean bimodal separation that makes the binary decision robust, unlike the narrow, overlapping score distributions produced by $L_1$ and attention-based methods. A fast linear CI skeleton provides high-recall candidates; a single gated model then prunes false positives by learning which edges genuinely improve prediction, with automatic regularization adapted to problem dimensions and skeleton density. Systematic experiments on synthetic benchmarks, spanning diverse graph topologies (scale-free, Erdős-R'enyi, small-world) and dimensionalities up to $d=100$, show that GRACE substantially improves F1 over its base CI method while maintaining high precision, and outperforms attention-based and score-based alternatives. GRACE matches or exceeds expensive nonlinear CI tests at a fraction of the cost ($75\times$ faster). On a real-world river flow dataset, where rainfall confounders, variable propagation lags, and distributional shifts violate standard assumptions, a temporal bootstrap variant of GRACE recovers 9 of 11 causal edges along the Elbe River with only 1 false positive ($F_1 = 0.86$, AUROC${} = 0.99$), reducing the skeleton's 106 false positives by 99%.

AI.tldr: GRACE refines constraint-based causal discovery using Hard Concrete gates with L0 regularization, producing clean bimodal edge scores that outperform L1/attention methods. It achieves 75x speedup over nonlinear CI tests and recovers 9/11 edges on a real river dataset with 99% fewer false positives.
AI.motivation: Modern high-dimensional time-series datasets (tens/hundreds of variables) make causal discovery difficult: constraint-based methods with nonlinear CI tests are infeasible at scale, while score-based methods require arbitrary thresholds on continuous scores that often have narrow, overlapping distributions.
AI.method: GRACE uses Hard Concrete gates with L0 regularization — each candidate edge has an independent gate that concentrates near 0 or 1, producing clean bimodal separation. A fast linear CI skeleton provides high-recall candidates, then a single gated model prunes false positives by learning which edges improve prediction, with automatic regularization adapted to problem dimensions.
AI.result: On synthetic benchmarks (scale-free, Erdős-Rényi, small-world, d≤100), GRACE substantially improves F1 over its base CI method while maintaining high precision, outperforms attention-based and score-based alternatives, and matches expensive nonlinear CI tests at 75× lower cost. On a real Elbe River dataset, it recovers 9/11 edges with F1=0.86, AUROC=0.99, reducing skeleton's 106 false positives by 99%.
AI.conclusion: GRACE provides an efficient and robust bridge between constraint-based and score-based causal discovery, achieving high accuracy with dramatically lower computational cost, especially valuable for real-world settings where standard assumptions (e.g., no confounding, stationarity) are violated.

title: ObsGraph: Hierarchical Observation Representation for Embodied Reasoning and Exploration
summary: Embodied reasoning and exploration are increasingly considered crucial abilities for robots operating in complex and unfamiliar environments. To accomplish tasks in such settings, an agent must identify and acquire the information necessary for the task through exploration. We propose ObsGraph, an observation-centric hierarchical scene graph that unifies scene representation, retrieval, and exploration. It retains visual evidence and organizes it into room-view-object layers: rooms provide coarse semantic anchors, views preserve contextual object covisibility, and objects store fine-grained details. On top of this representation, we perform coarse-to-fine hierarchical retrieval under a bounded budget, and crucially use retrieval outcomes to structure the exploration candidate space--activating room-level exploration, view refinement, or frontier exploration--thereby tightly coupling representation, retrieval, and adaptive multi-scale exploration. Experiments across embodied reasoning and exploration benchmarks demonstrate improved success and efficiency, highlighting the benefits of structured scene representation and more targeted information gathering driven by identified evidence gaps.

AI.tldr: ObsGraph is a hierarchical scene graph with room-view-object layers that unifies scene representation, retrieval, and exploration for embodied agents, using coarse-to-fine retrieval under bounded budgets to structure multi-scale exploration, achieving improved success and efficiency on embodied reasoning benchmarks.
AI.motivation: Robots operating in complex, unfamiliar environments need to identify and acquire necessary information through exploration, but existing methods lack a unified framework that tightly couples scene representation, evidence retrieval, and adaptive exploration strategies.
AI.method: ObsGraph organizes visual evidence into a room-view-object hierarchical scene graph: rooms provide semantic anchors, views preserve object covisibility contexts, and objects store fine-grained details. It performs coarse-to-fine hierarchical retrieval under bounded budgets and uses retrieval outcomes to adaptively activate room-level exploration, view refinement, or frontier exploration.
AI.result: Experiments on embodied reasoning and exploration benchmarks show improved success rates and efficiency, demonstrating the benefits of structured scene representation and targeted information gathering driven by identified evidence gaps.
AI.conclusion: Tightly coupling scene representation, retrieval, and adaptive multi-scale exploration through a hierarchical structure like ObsGraph significantly enhances robotic performance in complex environments by enabling more targeted information gathering.

title: Data Scale, Not Latency, Shapes Cross-Lingual Encoder Transfer in Streaming ASR
summary: Adapting a streaming speech recognition model to a new language requires choosing between two plausible warm starts: a multilingual (ML) encoder or an English-only (EN) encoder. The common intuition is that the multilingual encoder should help most at low data, but it is unclear how long that advantage persists, whether tight streaming latency amplifies it, and whether it survives deployment quantization. We answer these questions with a controlled sweep of a 0.6 B-parameter cache-aware FastConformer transducer across eight European languages, up to five target-language data scales (100 h to 2500 h), three streaming tiers plus offline decoding, and up to four public test sets. The main result is that multilingual initialization is a data-limited advantage, not a latency-limited one. On FLEURS at 160 ms, the mean EN-ML word error rate (WER) gap falls from +4.21 percentage points (pp) at 100 h to +0.20 pp at 2500 h; a power-law fit summarizes this decay, with each doubling of target-language data roughly halving the remaining advantage. Across the three streaming tiers, the across-language mean EN-ML gap is approximately stable at each scale from 100 to 1000 h, and is near zero by 2500 h. Finally, 4-bit weight-only encoder quantization at the matched 560 ms streaming tier reduces the encoder footprint by about 3x, with an average FLEURS WER increase of about 0.5 pp. The resulting guideline is simple: use multilingual initialization in low-data regimes, treat the choice as effectively irrelevant at large data, and make latency and quantization decisions independently.

AI.tldr: A controlled study of multilingual vs. English-only encoder warm starts for streaming speech recognition across 8 languages, 5 data scales, and 3 latency tiers shows multilingual initialization helps at low data (4.21 pp WER gap at 100h), but the advantage decays to near zero by 2500h, is stable across latency tiers, and quantization decisions are independent of the initialization choice.
AI.motivation: To resolve the unclear trade-off between multilingual and English-only encoder initialization for adapting streaming speech recognition to new languages, specifically examining how data scale, streaming latency, and quantization affect this choice.
AI.method: Controlled experiments with a 0.6B-parameter FastConformer transducer across 8 European languages, varying target-language data from 100h to 2500h, testing 3 streaming latency tiers plus offline decoding, and evaluating on up to 4 public test sets.
AI.result: Multilingual initialization's WER advantage decays sharply with data scale (4.21pp → 0.20pp), follows a power-law (advantage halves per data doubling), is stable across latency tiers, and 4-bit quantization adds ~0.5pp WER increase regardless of initialization choice.
AI.conclusion: Use multilingual initialization for low-data regimes; the choice becomes irrelevant at large data scales; latency and quantization decisions can be made independently of the warm-start strategy.

title: FT-WBC: Learning Fault-Tolerant Whole-Body Control for Legged Loco-Manipulation
summary: Legged manipulators combine the mobility of legged platforms with the manipulation capability of robotic arms. However, arm-induced Center-of-Mass shifts and dynamic disturbances make the system more prone to instability under actuator failures, potentially leading to falls, task failures, or safety risks. Existing fault-tolerant control methods mainly focus on locomotion alone, leaving the coupled problem of whole-body stability and arm reachability in fault-tolerant loco-manipulation largely unaddressed. To bridge this gap, we propose FT-WBC, a fault-tolerant loco-manipulation framework for robust whole-body control of legged manipulators under actuator failures. FT-WBC adopts a decoupled upper- and lower-body policy architecture and introduces two key modules: a Fault Estimator (FE) and a Posture Adaptation Module (PAM). The FE predicts faulty joints from lower-body proprioceptive histories, while the PAM uses this fault information to adapt the base posture plan generated by the arm policy, converting potentially unstable posture requests into safe and executable base posture commands. Through this fault-aware posture adaptation mechanism, FT-WBC synthesizes compensatory gaits under actuator failures and preserves as much arm workspace as possible while maintaining whole-body stability. Simulation and real-world experiments show that FT-WBC significantly improves survival rate and workspace under weakening or locked failures, and transfers zero-shot to a real legged manipulator in the real world.

AI.tldr: FT-WBC is a fault-tolerant loco-manipulation framework for legged manipulators that uses a Fault Estimator and Posture Adaptation Module to handle actuator failures, significantly improving survival rate and workspace under weakening or locked joint failures.
AI.motivation: Existing fault-tolerant control methods focus on locomotion alone, leaving the coupled problem of whole-body stability and arm reachability in fault-tolerant loco-manipulation largely unaddressed.
AI.method: FT-WBC uses a decoupled upper- and lower-body policy architecture with two key modules: (1) Fault Estimator (FE) that predicts faulty joints from lower-body proprioceptive histories, and (2) Posture Adaptation Module (PAM) that adapts base posture plans to convert unstable requests into safe commands.
AI.result: Simulation and real-world experiments show FT-WBC significantly improves survival rate and workspace under weakening or locked failures, and transfers zero-shot to a real legged manipulator.
AI.conclusion: The fault-aware posture adaptation mechanism enables compensatory gaits under actuator failures while preserving arm workspace and maintaining whole-body stability.

title: Adaptive Machine Learning Framework for UAV Trajectory Optimization in O-RAN
comment: 16 pages, 12 figures, IEEE Transactions on Vehicular Technology
summary: The deployment of unmanned aerial vehicles (UAV) as open radio units (O-RUs) in 6G cellular systems presents a promising opportunity to achieve scalable and adaptive network coverage. However, optimizing UAV trajectories in dynamic and unfamiliar environments remains a critical challenge, particularly due to the need for extensive retraining in each new scenario. In this paper, we introduce a novel UAV trajectory optimization framework that integrates enhanced continual transfer learning within the O-RAN architecture. The proposed system maintains a library of pre-trained models and employs a model selection mechanism to identify and transfer knowledge from the most relevant environments, minimizing adaptation time and improving efficiency. When no sufficiently similar model is available, a fallback model empowered by continuous refinements ensures baseline performance. The framework leverages real-world city maps and ray tracing techniques to enhance learning reliability and improve trajectory planning. Simulation results demonstrate that the proposed model selection-based transfer learning approach reduces convergence time by 44% to 56% compared to retraining from scratch, and up to 40% compared to traditional transfer learning without model selection.

AI.tldr: This paper proposes a UAV trajectory optimization framework for 6G systems that uses continual transfer learning within O-RAN architecture, leveraging a library of pre-trained models and a selection mechanism to reduce adaptation time. Simulations show 44-56% faster convergence than retraining from scratch.
AI.motivation: Deploying UAVs as O-RUs in 6G systems offers scalable network coverage, but optimizing their trajectories in dynamic, unfamiliar environments is challenging due to extensive retraining requirements in each new scenario.
AI.method: The framework integrates enhanced continual transfer learning within O-RAN architecture, maintaining a library of pre-trained models with a selection mechanism to transfer knowledge from relevant environments. A fallback model with continuous refinements handles cases with no sufficiently similar model. Real-world city maps and ray tracing techniques are used for training.
AI.result: The model selection-based transfer learning approach reduces convergence time by 44% to 56% compared to retraining from scratch, and up to 40% compared to traditional transfer learning without model selection.
AI.conclusion: The proposed framework significantly improves UAV trajectory optimization efficiency in 6G O-RAN systems by reducing adaptation time through intelligent knowledge transfer, enabling scalable and adaptive network coverage.

title: ARIA: Adaptive Region-Based Importance Allocation for Conditional Diffusion Distillation
summary: Distilling conditional diffusion models aims to transfer the behavior of a large teacher to a smaller student while preserving alignment across conditioning inputs. Unlike recognition tasks, knowledge distillation in conditional diffusion often struggles to transfer knowledge beyond the training distribution, since the predicted noise strongly depends on the conditioning signal. As a result, effective distillation requires exploring a large conditioning space. In practical settings, this creates a major bottleneck. Paired image-condition data may be limited, and generating synthetic images for every available condition is often computationally infeasible, while the pool of conditions, such as text prompts, can be extremely large. Recent work addresses this issue by switching conditions during training, exposing the student to a broader conditioning space without changing the distillation objective. Yet this raises a complementary question: once a large conditioning corpus is available, how should the training effort be allocated? In this work, we introduce ARIA, a framework that adaptively allocates training effort across coarse regions of the conditioning space. By maintaining online estimates of teacher-student discrepancy at the region level, ARIA focuses updates where misalignment persists while preserving the original distillation objective. Empirically, ARIA improves over RC across most architectures and settings, with the clearest gains observed in unseen and underrepresented regimes. We also provide a theoretical analysis showing that the proposed tracking mechanism follows the evolving discrepancy during training under bounded variance and drift assumptions.

AI.tldr: ARIA is an adaptive framework for distilling conditional diffusion models that allocates training effort across regions of the conditioning space based on online estimates of teacher-student discrepancy, improving performance especially in unseen and underrepresented regimes.
AI.motivation: Effective distillation of conditional diffusion models requires exploring a large conditioning space, but paired image-condition data may be limited and generating synthetic images for all conditions is computationally infeasible. Existing methods (RC) address this by switching conditions during training but don't address how training effort should be allocated across conditions.
AI.method: ARIA adaptively allocates training effort across coarse regions of the conditioning space by maintaining online estimates of teacher-student discrepancy at the region level, focusing updates where misalignment persists while preserving the original distillation objective.
AI.result: ARIA improves over RC across most architectures and settings, with clearest gains in unseen and underrepresented regimes. Theoretical analysis shows the tracking mechanism follows evolving discrepancy under bounded variance and drift assumptions.
AI.conclusion: Adaptive allocation of training effort across conditioning regions is an effective strategy for knowledge distillation in conditional diffusion models, particularly beneficial for addressing distributional gaps in unseen or underrepresented conditions.

title: Fabric Image Demoiréing Benchmark from Synthesis to Restoration
summary: Fabric moiré is a sampling-induced aliasing artifact caused by the interaction between fine textile patterns and camera sensor grids, producing structured interference that severely degrades image quality. Unlike screen-induced moiré, which stems from strictly periodic display lattices, fabric moiré is intrinsically more challenging due to the broadband and semi-periodic nature of textile weaves. The heavy spectral overlap between intrinsic texture and aliasing components renders fabric demoiréing substantially more ill-posed. Consequently, existing models trained on screen moiré datasets generalize poorly to these complex textile patterns. Despite its practical importance, fabric image demoiréing remains underexplored and lacks standardized benchmarks. We present the first comprehensive benchmark for fabric image demoiréing. To address the difficulty of acquiring pixel-aligned real-world pairs, we develop a physically motivated synthesis framework and construct a large-scale dataset comprising 16,050 paired multi-resolution fabric images with controllable aliasing severity. Furthermore, we customize a baseline model, which establishes promising performance on the proposed benchmark dataset with strong generalization ability. Our benchmark provides a standardized platform for advancing research in fabric image demoiréing.
