AI.tldr: This paper introduces the first comprehensive benchmark for fabric image demoiréing, addressing the underexplored problem of aliasing artifacts caused by textile-camera interaction. The authors propose a physically motivated synthesis framework, create a large-scale dataset (16,050 paired images), and provide a baseline model with strong generalization performance.
AI.motivation: Fabric moiré is fundamentally different from screen moiré due to the broadband, semi-periodic nature of textiles, making demoiréing more ill-posed. Existing models trained on screen moiré datasets fail to generalize, yet fabric demoiréing lacks standardized benchmarks and datasets.
AI.method: A physically motivated synthesis framework is developed to generate pixel-aligned real-world-like paired images. A large-scale dataset of 16,050 multi-resolution fabric images with controllable aliasing severity is constructed, and a customized baseline model is designed for fabric demoiréing.
AI.result: The proposed benchmark dataset and baseline model demonstrate promising demoiréing performance with strong generalization ability on fabric images.
AI.conclusion: This work provides the first standardized benchmark for fabric image demoiréing, filling a critical gap in the field and offering a foundation for future research.

title: Navigating User Behavior toward Personalized Multimodal Generation
comment: 16 pages, 15 figures, 5 tables. Code is available at https://github.com/iLearn-Lab/NaviGen
summary: Modern AIGC pipelines deliver high-fidelity images and videos but presuppose a well-formed creation instruction, while end users rarely articulate visual details, leaving generators misaligned with user demand. We study personalized content generation, which turns a user's interaction history into an executable instruction for downstream synthesis, and identify two obstacles: behavior must be encoded in a form legible to language reasoning, and the model must acquire instruction-writing skill absent from both pretraining and behavior data. We propose NaviGen, which represents each item with a dual identifier coupling a collaborative code and a textual code as a behavioral substrate and a semantic bridge in one token stream. On this representation, a two-stage SFT+RL pipeline first distills preference reasoning and instruction writing from evolutionarily searched supervision, then aligns generation with user intent through hierarchical and self-consistent rewards. Experiments across product, game, and short-video domains show that NaviGen improves personalized image and video generation, strengthens next-item prediction, and yields more specific, relevant, and visually generatable instructions. Our code is anonymously released at: https://github.com/iLearn-Lab/NaviGen.

AI.tldr: NaviGen turns user interaction history into executable instructions for AIGC by coupling collaborative and textual codes via dual identifiers, trained with two-stage SFT+RL pipeline, improving personalized image/video generation across product, game, and short-video domains.
AI.motivation: Users rarely articulate visual details, causing misalignment between AIGC output and user demand. The paper aims to enable personalized content generation by converting interaction history into executable instructions.
AI.method: NaviGen represents each item with a dual identifier coupling collaborative code (behavioral substrate) and textual code (semantic bridge). A two-stage SFT+RL pipeline first distills preference reasoning and instruction writing from evolutionarily searched supervision, then aligns generation with user intent via hierarchical and self-consistent rewards.
AI.result: Improved personalized image and video generation, strengthened next-item prediction, and yielded more specific, relevant, and visually generatable instructions across product, game, and short-video domains.
AI.conclusion: Encoding user behavior into a language-legible form and acquiring instruction-writing skill through SFT+RL effectively bridges the gap between user history and AIGC synthesis, enabling better personalization.

title: G$^3$VLA: Geometric inductive bias for Vision-Language-Action Models
summary: Vision-language-action (VLA) models have made rapid progress in generalist robot manipulation by harnessing semantic knowledge from pretrained vision-language backbones, but their visual tokens remain grounded in 2D image coordinates rather than the calibrated geometry of the robot's cameras -- a mismatch especially pronounced in multi-camera setups, where views are coupled by known intrinsics and extrinsics yet processed as independent images. We propose G$^3$VLA, a camera-aware geometric module that injects calibrated structure into the visual-token stream of a pretrained VLA without altering its action space or imitation objective, combining intrinsic-conditioned ray embeddings, projective positional encoding (PRoPE), and bidirectional cross-view fusion. Geometric supervision is provided either from ground-truth point maps when available, or from confidence-gated $π^3$X teacher predictions, requiring no depth sensors or manual annotations. Instantiated on $π_0$, G$^3$VLA yields consistent gains across the LIBERO suites, RoboCasa24, RoboTwin2.0, and real-robot settings, with the largest improvements on spatially and object-sensitive tasks. We further validate on $π_{0.5}$ and GR00T 1.5, with results suggesting that geometric transfer is most effective when geometry-aware tokens have direct access to the action generation pathway. Our project page is at https://sites.google.com/view/g3vla

AI.tldr: G^3VLA injects camera-aware geometric information (ray embeddings, projective positional encoding, cross-view fusion) into pretrained VLAs, improving robot manipulation performance across multiple benchmarks and real-robot settings without needing depth sensors or manual annotations.
AI.motivation: Existing VLA models use 2D image tokens without calibrated camera geometry, which is problematic for multi-camera setups where views are related via known intrinsics/extrinsics but processed independently, limiting spatial understanding.
AI.method: Three key components: (1) intrinsic-conditioned ray embeddings, (2) projective positional encoding (PRoPE), and (3) bidirectional cross-view fusion. Geometric supervision comes from ground-truth point maps or confidence-gated π^3X teacher predictions, requiring no depth sensors. Instantiated on π_0, π_{0.5}, and GR00T 1.5.
AI.result: Consistent gains across LIBERO suites, RoboCasa24, RoboTwin2.0, and real-robot settings. Largest improvements on spatially and object-sensitive tasks. Geometric transfer most effective when geometry-aware tokens directly access the action generation pathway.
AI.conclusion: Injecting calibrated geometric structure into VLA visual tokens improves manipulation performance, especially for spatial tasks, and works best when geometry-aware tokens connect directly to action generation.

title: Degeneracy-Aware Resilient Resource Allocation in Cell-Free Cache-Aided MU-MIMO Networks
comment: 13 pages, 19 figures, IEEE Transactions on Communications (TCOM)
summary: Cell-free cache-aided multi-user multiple-input-multiple-output (MIMO) (CF-CA-MU-MIMO) networks improve spectral efficiency through coded multicast delivery and distributed spatial multiplexing, but their distributed architecture introduces vulnerabilities to jamming, cache-aware eavesdropping, Byzantine corruption, and pilot-contamination attacks. This paper develops a degeneracy-aware resilient framework based on four vulnerability-mode partitions (subfile, edge node, multicast stream, and user) and three attack-aware structural metrics: Degeneracy-Weighted Path Robustness (DWPR$^{\mathrm{att}}$), trust-aware Functional Substitution Score (FSS$^{\mathrm{trust}}$), and a robust degeneracy index ($D_k^{\mathrm{rob}}$). These metrics are incorporated into a fully decentralized consensus-based agent framework (DC-ABM) using trust-weighted trimmed-mean aggregation and adaptive trust evolution. Five theoretical results are established: (i) a tight top-mass concentration lemma, (ii) matching memory--rate--resilience achievability and converse bounds, (iii) a robust-degeneracy bound with outage characterization, (iv) a secrecy--cache coupling theorem, and (v) a Byzantine-robust mean-square convergence result with an explicit breakdown threshold $f_{\max}$. Simulations validate the analytical bounds and demonstrate $1.8\times$ to $3\times$ faster convergence than distributed alternating direction method of multipliers (ADMM), multi-agent reinforcement learning (MARL)/graph neural network (GNN)-based control, and Su--Vaidya consensus while maintaining throughput up to the predicted threshold $f_{\max}\approx0.19$.

AI.tldr: This paper develops a degeneracy-aware resilient framework for cell-free cache-aided MIMO networks, introducing three attack-aware metrics (DWPR^{att}, FSS^{trust}, D_k^{rob}) and a decentralized consensus-based agent framework (DC-ABM). Five theoretical results are established, and simulations show 1.8×-3× faster convergence than baselines with throughput maintained up to f_max≈0.19.
AI.motivation: Cell-free cache-aided MU-MIMO networks improve spectral efficiency but their distributed architecture introduces vulnerabilities to jamming, cache-aware eavesdropping, Byzantine corruption, and pilot-contamination attacks. Existing methods lack a unified framework addressing these diverse attack modes.
AI.method: The paper develops a degeneracy-aware resilient framework with four vulnerability-mode partitions (subfile, edge node, multicast stream, user) and three structural metrics: DWPR^{att} (path robustness), FSS^{trust} (trust-aware substitution), D_k^{rob} (robust degeneracy index). These are incorporated into a decentralized consensus-based agent model (DC-ABM) using trust-weighted trimmed-mean aggregation and adaptive trust evolution.
AI.result: Five theoretical results established: (i) tight top-mass concentration lemma, (ii) matching memory-rate-resilience bounds, (iii) robust-degeneracy bound with outage characterization, (iv) secrecy-cache coupling theorem, (v) Byzantine-robust convergence with breakdown threshold f_max. Simulations show 1.8×-3× faster convergence than ADMM, MARL/GNN-based control, and Su-Vaidya consensus, maintaining throughput up to f_max≈0.19.
AI.conclusion: The proposed framework effectively unifies resilience against multiple attack types in cell-free cache-aided MIMO networks, with strong theoretical guarantees and practical convergence improvements over existing methods.

title: Closing the Loop: Formally Verified Law as a Reward Signal for Self-Improving Legal AI
summary: This article develops an architecture that creates a formally verifiable reward signal to train legal AI, adapting the LLM proposes, verifier disposes paradigm from mathematical AI to the distinctive demands of law. We present an architecture comprising LLM-driven autoformalization into a formal legal calculus extending Catala, a verification kernel, and explanation generation grounded in formal proof traces. For the computational components of law, the architecture provides provable correctness. For open-textured legal analysis, it provides structural guarantees: every required stage of the legal argument is addressed, argumentation is exercised at the correct stages and not omitted, and the deductive links between steps are valid. We demonstrate the architecture on procedural deadline calculations in German law, Commerce Clause analysis in U.S. constitutional law, and cross-jurisdictional sanction proportionality. We further show that the same architecture has a structural advantage for legal AI training: a deterministic external verifier supplies verifiable outcomes for legal problems and thereby closes the traditional reinforcement-learning loop gap in law.

AI.tldr: This paper presents an architecture that creates a formally verifiable reward signal for training legal AI by adapting the "LLM proposes, verifier disposes" paradigm from math to law. It uses LLM-driven autoformalization into a formal legal calculus (extending Catala), a verification kernel, and explanation generation from formal proof traces. The approach provides provable correctness for computational law and structural guarantees for open-textured legal analysis. Demonstrated on German procedural deadlines, U.S. Commerce Clause analysis, and cross-jurisdictional sanction proportionality, the architecture closes the reinforcement-learning loop gap in legal AI by providing a deterministic external verifier.
AI.motivation: Legal AI lacks verifiable reward signals for training, creating a gap in reinforcement learning approaches. Mathematical AI has successfully used the "LLM proposes, verifier disposes" paradigm, but law's unique demands (including open-textured concepts) require an adapted solution.
AI.method: The architecture has three components: (1) LLM-driven autoformalization into a formal legal calculus extending Catala, (2) a verification kernel that checks correctness, and (3) explanation generation from formal proof traces.
AI.result: Demonstrated on three diverse legal domains: German procedural deadline calculations, U.S. Commerce Clause constitutional analysis, and cross-jurisdictional sanction proportionality. The architecture provides provable correctness for computational law and structural guarantees (mandatory stages addressed, correct argumentation placement, valid deductive links) for open-textured analysis. It also closes the RL training loop gap by providing a deterministic external verifier.
AI.conclusion: The proposed architecture successfully adapts formal verification methods from mathematics to law, enabling verifiable reward signals for legal AI training while accommodating both computational and open-textured aspects of legal reasoning.

title: End-to-End Radar and Communication Modulation Recognition with Neuromorphic Computing
summary: Although deep learning-based methods can achieve high accuracy in automatic modulation recognition (AMR) tasks, their high computational cost makes it difficult to strike a balance between accuracy and power consumption, thereby limiting their application on resource-constrained platforms. Neuromorphic architectures that perform spike-driven inference with modest energy budgets have recently been explored for vision and timeseries tasks. Motivated by these works, we propose EMRFormer, a novel end-to-end spiking nerural network (SNN) architecture that applies spike-driven transformer to the constraints of neuromorphic hardware for AMR. The model incorporates an adaptive spike encoder and Integer Leaky Integrate-and-Fire neurons to mitigate the degradation of effective information and enhance SNN representational capacity. By integrating spike-separable Convolution Neural Networks (SSCNN) into Spike-Driven Transformers (SpikeFormer), EMRFormer effectively extracts multi-scale temporal features from the raw IQ waveforms. We validate our approach across various mainstream datasets, the experimental results show that EMRFormer achieves state-of-the-art interms of accuracy, outperforming all the baselines. Furthermore, the model maintains strong performance in low signal-to-noise(SNR) environments and reduces theoretical energy consumption by over 90%. Finally, we evaluate our model on a KA200 neuromorphic chip. The results show that our model achieves up to 5 times reduction in power compared to running on a 3090 GPU or an Orin NX. This work demonstrates a promising pathway for AMR on resource-constrained devices.

AI.tldr: EMRFormer is a spiking neural network (SNN) with spike-driven transformer architecture for automatic modulation recognition (AMR). It achieves state-of-the-art accuracy, reduces theoretical energy consumption by over 90%, and delivers up to 5x power reduction on neuromorphic hardware (KA200 chip) compared to GPU (3090) or Orin NX.
AI.motivation: Deep learning AMR methods suffer from high computational costs and power consumption, making them unsuitable for resource-constrained platforms. The authors aim to balance accuracy and energy efficiency for AMR on such devices.
AI.method: Proposes EMRFormer, an end-to-end SNN with: (1) adaptive spike encoder and Integer Leaky Integrate-and-Fire (LIF) neurons to preserve information and boost representational capacity; (2) spike-separable CNNs (SSCNN) integrated into Spike-Driven Transformers (SpikeFormer) to extract multi-scale temporal features from raw IQ waveforms.
AI.result: Achieves SOTA accuracy across mainstream datasets, maintains strong performance in low-SNR environments, reduces theoretical energy consumption by over 90%, and demonstrates up to 5x power reduction on KA200 neuromorphic chip vs. 3090 GPU or Orin NX.
AI.conclusion: EMRFormer offers a promising pathway for deploying AMR on resource-constrained devices by combining high accuracy with extreme energy efficiency via neuromorphic hardware.

title: Exploring the relationship between human-centric AI and firm idiosyncratic risks
summary: Despite the extensive discussions of human-centric AI (HCAI) in Industry 5.0, its effects on firms' idiosyncratic risks (IR) remains underexplored. This is an imperative issue for firms navigate financial risks during the current technological revolution, as IR reflects investor reactions to corporate heterogeneous AI strategies and implementations by isolating firm-level stock volatility from systematic factors. Integrating situated AI theory with social-technical systems theory, we conceptualise HCAI as a situated AI strategy that reduces AI-related ethical risks and fosters AI-Human synergies in firms' business operations, ultimately reducing IR by aligning with stakeholders' diverse expectations. Moreover, socio-technical factors, namely digitalisation, operational efficiency, executive shareholding, and CEOs with IT background, may moderate the HCAI-IR relationship. Using a multi-source panel dataset of Chinese listed firms from 2015 to 2023, we find that HCAI is associated with lower firm IR. Furthermore, digitalisation and executive shareholding strengthen this risk-reducing effect, whereas operational efficiency and CEOs with IT background surprisingly attenuate it. Our findings offer theoretical contributions and practical insights for both ethical AI governance and firm financial risk management in the AI era.

AI.tldr: This study examines how Human-centric AI (HCAI) reduces firms' idiosyncratic risks (IR) in Industry 5.0, using Chinese listed firm data (2015-2023). HCAI lowers IR by reducing ethical risks and fostering AI-human synergies. Digitalisation and executive shareholding strengthen this effect, while operational efficiency and IT-background CEOs weaken it.
AI.motivation: To address the underexplored relationship between HCAI and firms' idiosyncratic risks, which is critical for firms navigating financial risks during the current technological revolution.
AI.method: Multi-source panel dataset of Chinese listed firms from 2015 to 2023, integrating situated AI theory with social-technical systems theory.
AI.result: HCAI is associated with lower firm IR. Digitalisation and executive shareholding strengthen this risk-reducing effect, while operational efficiency and CEOs with IT background attenuate it.
AI.conclusion: The findings offer theoretical contributions and practical insights for ethical AI governance and firm financial risk management in the AI era.

title: Decentralized Pose Graph Riemannian Optimization for Object-based Multi-Robot SLAM
summary: Pose graph optimization (PGO) is a key back-end component for state estimation in networked multi-robot simultaneous localization and mapping (SLAM). In object-based multi-robot SLAM, the problem becomes more tightly coupled because robots must jointly estimate both their trajectories and the poses of persistent objects observed by multiple agents. Existing decentralized solutions often assume that the communication graph closely matches the physical interaction topology, which is restrictive in realistic deployments where communication is sparse, intermittent, or time-varying. This paper presents a fully decentralized Riemannian optimization framework for object-based multi-robot PGO that decouples the coupled estimation problem via a consensus mechanism, enabling flexible communication topologies. To improve convergence under limited communication budgets, we further develop a distributed approximate-Newton scheme that exploits local second-order information while operating directly on the SE(d) manifold to preserve geometric consistency, and we establish the convergence to Riemannian first-order stationary points and provide a local condition-number analysis explaining the benefit of approximate second-order information over first-order Riemannian descent. The resulting method reduces iteration count and communication overhead without sacrificing estimation accuracy. Extensive evaluations on public benchmarks, large-scale simulations, and real-world multi-robot experiments demonstrate improved accuracy, runtime efficiency, scalability across network topologies, and robustness to communication failures.

AI.tldr: A fully decentralized Riemannian optimization framework for object-based multi-robot PGO using consensus and approximate-Newton methods, enabling flexible communication topologies with reduced iteration count and communication overhead.
AI.motivation: Existing decentralized PGO solutions assume communication graphs closely match physical interaction topologies, which is restrictive in real-world deployments where communication is sparse, intermittent, or time-varying.
AI.method: Proposes a fully decentralized Riemannian optimization framework with consensus mechanism to decouple coupled estimation, plus a distributed approximate-Newton scheme exploiting local second-order information on the SE(d) manifold.
AI.result: Reduced iteration count and communication overhead without sacrificing accuracy; validated on public benchmarks, large-scale simulations, and real-world experiments showing improved accuracy, runtime efficiency, scalability, and robustness to communication failures.
AI.conclusion: The method achieves convergence to Riemannian first-order stationary points with theoretical guarantees, and the approximate second-order approach outperforms first-order Riemannian descent in terms of condition number and efficiency.

title: Sonus Health: Calibrated Heart-Murmur Detection from Smartphone-Based Veterinary Auscultation
summary: Heart disease is among the most common serious conditions in dogs and cats, and a heart murmur heard on auscultation is one of the earliest signs of such disease; such murmurs are often subtle and challenging to detect at early stages. General-practice veterinary examinations catch only a fraction of these murmurs, and definitive cardiac assessment typically requires either a board-certified cardiologist or an in-clinic echocardiogram, which may involve cost and scheduling constraints. We describe Sonus Health, a smartphone-based screening system that analyses an auscultation recording of approximately thirty seconds or longer - captured by a pet owner at home or by a veterinarian or nurse in clinic - and returns a tiered result within moments. The system was evaluated on 322 veterinary-labelled recordings under standard out-of-fold cross-validation. For recordings assigned to the high-confidence tier (30% of cases), accuracy reaches 95.9%, with 94.0% sensitivity and 97.9% specificity. Uncertain cases are prospectively routed to veterinary review rather than assigned an automated classification. Results are stable across standard and group-aware cross-validation, a held-out test split, and multiple random seeds. Beyond murmur detection, the platform also estimates heart rate and heart rate variability, and we position it as a screening, triage, and longitudinal-monitoring layer for companion-animal cardiac care.

AI.tldr: Sonus Health is a smartphone-based screening system that detects heart murmurs in dogs and cats from ~30-second auscultation recordings, achieving 95.9% accuracy in high-confidence cases (30% of recordings) while routing uncertain cases to human review.
AI.motivation: Heart murmurs are early signs of cardiac disease in pets but are often missed in general-practice exams. Definitive diagnosis requires specialists or echocardiograms, which are costly and have scheduling constraints. There's a need for accessible, low-cost screening tools.
AI.method: The system analyzes smartphone-recorded auscultation audio (≥30 seconds) using machine learning, returning tiered results. High-confidence cases get automated classification; uncertain cases are routed to veterinary review. Evaluated on 322 veterinary-labelled recordings using cross-validation.
AI.result: For high-confidence tier (30% of cases): 95.9% accuracy, 94.0% sensitivity, 97.9% specificity. Results stable across cross-validation methods, held-out test split, and multiple random seeds. Also estimates heart rate and heart rate variability.
AI.conclusion: Sonus Health provides a scalable, accessible screening, triage, and monitoring tool for companion-animal cardiac care, bridging the gap between general practice and specialist cardiac assessment.

title: Catastrophic Compositional Generation: Why Vanilla Diffusion Models Fail to Extrapolate
summary: The task of compositional generation involves using a conditional generative model, trained only on a subset of the possible conditions, to produce samples from compositionally-defined target distributions such as a geometric combination of the source distributions. In this work, we argue that this task is often infeasible for vanilla conditional diffusion models: we conjecture that no inference-time technique can efficiently produce samples from the target distribution in certain well-motivated settings. This idea is supported by theory-guided generalization arguments and carefully-designed experiments on both synthetic and realistic data. In particular, while recent methods such as Feynman-Kac correction reduce inference-time approximation error, our results show that score estimation error has a more catastrophic effect on performance when the target distribution is out-of-distribution with respect to the sources, highlighting the need for a different approach to this task.

AI.tldr: This paper argues that vanilla conditional diffusion models cannot efficiently perform compositional generation (producing samples from combined target distributions) when the target is out-of-distribution from training data, and shows that score estimation errors catastrophically degrade performance in such settings.
AI.motivation: To investigate whether conditional diffusion models can perform compositional generation—producing samples from geometrically combined target distributions when only trained on subset conditions—and to understand the fundamental limitations of inference-time techniques for this task.
AI.method: The authors use theory-guided generalization arguments combined with carefully-designed experiments on both synthetic and realistic data to test the feasibility of compositional generation. They compare approaches including Feynman-Kac correction against vanilla conditional diffusion models.
AI.result: Score estimation error has a catastrophic effect on compositional generation performance when the target distribution is out-of-distribution with respect to training sources. Even advanced inference-time methods like Feynman-Kac correction fail to overcome this limitation.
AI.conclusion: Compositional generation is often infeasible for vanilla conditional diffusion models in certain well-motivated settings, suggesting that fundamentally different approaches are needed rather than just improved inference-time corrections.

title: Progressive Pixel-Neighborhood Deformable Cross-Attention for Multispectral Object Detection
summary: Effective cross-modal feature alignment and interaction are central challenges in multispectral object detection. Although global cross-attention provides strong long-range modeling ability, its quadratic complexity with respect to feature size limits deployment on resource-constrained platforms. We therefore propose Progressive Pixel-Neighborhood Deformable Cross-Attention for multispectral feature fusion, termed PNAFusion. The proposed framework is motivated by two observations: weak misalignment between visible and thermal images is usually concentrated around local neighborhoods, and semantic correspondence across modalities often follows non-linear spatial mappings that fixed receptive fields cannot model well. To address these issues, PNAFusion incorporates local spatial priors into its architectural design to concentrate feature interaction and alignment on the most relevant neighborhoods. Specifically, a Pixel-Neighborhood Cross-Attention (PNCA) module is introduced to avoid redundant global feature matching and suppress background noise. Meanwhile, an Adaptive Deformable Alignment (ADA) module captures non-linear spatial correspondences through learned pixel-wise offsets. These components are further integrated through an iterative feedback mechanism to progressively refine cross-modal feature alignment. Experiments on FLIR, M3FD, and DroneVehicle show that PNAFusion achieves 84.2, 90.5, and 85.5 mAP@0.5, respectively, under the YOLOv5 detector, and further reaches 86.8 mAP@0.5 on FLIR and 90.8 mAP@0.5 on M3FD when transferred to Co-DETR. Efficiency analysis indicates that PNAFusion reduces allocated GPU memory by 33.0\% compared with ICAFusion and reduces theoretical FLOPs from 194.8 G to 156.4 G, although the deformable sampling and iterative refinement introduce additional latency. Our code will be available at https://github.com/DanielQiuTian/PNAFusion.

AI.tldr: PNAFusion proposes a Progressive Pixel-Neighborhood Deformable Cross-Attention framework for multispectral object detection that uses local spatial priors (PNCA module) and adaptive deformable alignment (ADA module) with iterative refinement to achieve efficient cross-modal fusion, reducing computational cost while achieving strong mAP results on FLIR, M3FD, and DroneVehicle datasets.
AI.motivation: Global cross-attention has quadratic complexity limiting deployment on resource-constrained platforms. Two key observations motivate the work: (1) misalignment between visible and thermal images is usually local, and (2) semantic correspondences across modalities follow non-linear spatial mappings that fixed receptive fields cannot model well.
AI.method: Proposes PNAFusion with two core modules: (1) Pixel-Neighborhood Cross-Attention (PNCA) that focuses on local neighborhoods rather than global matching to reduce redundancy and suppress background noise, and (2) Adaptive Deformable Alignment (ADA) that learns pixel-wise offsets to capture non-linear spatial correspondences. An iterative feedback mechanism progressively refines cross-modal feature alignment.
AI.result: Achieves 84.2, 90.5, and 85.5 mAP@0.5 on FLIR, M3FD, and DroneVehicle with YOLOv5; reaches 86.8 and 90.8 mAP@0.5 when transferred to Co-DETR. Reduces GPU memory by 33.0% vs ICAFusion and reduces FLOPs from 194.8G to 156.4G, though deformable sampling and iterative refinement add some latency.
AI.conclusion: PNAFusion demonstrates that local spatial priors and deformable alignment can effectively replace costly global attention for multispectral fusion, achieving strong accuracy-efficiency trade-offs suitable for resource-constrained deployment.

title: FlowR2A: Learning Reward-to-Action Distribution for Multimodal Driving Planning
summary: Multimodal driving planning faces a long-standing tension between two paradigms: scoring-based methods benefit from dense reward supervision but are confined to a fixed action vocabulary, while anchor-based methods generate proposals dynamically yet suffer from sparse supervision constrained to a single ground-truth trajectory. In this work, we propose FlowR2A, which resolves this tension by reframing simulation-based rewards from discriminative targets into generative conditions. By learning the reward-conditioned action distribution from dense trajectory-reward pairs with a flow-matching decoder, FlowR2A unifies the dense supervision of scoring-based methods with the proposal generation of anchor-based methods in a single generative model, forcing the model to internalize the correlation between an action and its outcomes in safety, progress, comfort, and rule compliance. To balance hard safety constraints against soft progress objectives, we introduce fine-grained per-timestep reward conditioning and reward noise augmentation. The generative formulation naturally supports controllable test-time sampling via reward guidance and anchored sampling, producing high-quality proposals. FlowR2A achieves state-of-the-art results on the NAVSIM v1 and v2 benchmarks, with multimodal proposals of substantially higher quality than prior methods.

AI.tldr: FlowR2A unifies scoring-based dense reward supervision with anchor-based generative proposal planning via reward-conditioned flow matching, achieving SOTA on NAVSIM benchmarks.
AI.motivation: To resolve the tension between scoring-based methods (dense supervision but fixed action vocabulary) and anchor-based methods (dynamic proposals but sparse supervision) in multimodal driving planning.
AI.method: Reframes simulation-based rewards as generative conditions using a flow-matching decoder to learn reward-conditioned action distributions from dense trajectory-reward pairs, with per-timestep reward conditioning and noise augmentation.
AI.result: State-of-the-art results on NAVSIM v1 and v2 benchmarks with substantially higher quality multimodal proposals than prior methods.
AI.conclusion: FlowR2A effectively bridges the gap between scoring and anchor-based paradigms by forcing the model to internalize action-outcome correlations across safety, progress, comfort, and rule compliance, with controllable test-time sampling via reward guidance.

title: Explaining Failures of Cyber-Physical Systems with Actual Causality
comment: Accepted to the 2026 IEEE International Conference on Robotics and Automation (ICRA 2026)
summary: Modern autonomous Cyber-Physical Systems (CPSs), such as self-driving cars, face increasingly complex demands, and yet are expected to act reliably. The black-box nature often characterizing such systems, especially those relying on neural components, makes it impossible to fully verify the system behavior prior to deployment. Unfortunately, unexpected failures-when the system does not comply with its specification-are inevitable and may have catastrophic implications. To improve trust in the system and facilitate future mitigation after a failure occurs, it is important to try to derive an explanation for the unexpected system behavior. This paper introduces the novel concept of leveraging the framework of actual causality for CPS failure explanation. Up until now, this framework was only used to derive explanations in the context of simple systems, such as image classifiers. This paper addresses the theoretical gaps and provides the guidance needed to allow for correct explanation derivation in the CPS domain. Beyond the theoretical contribution, the paper presents two novel, practical, system-agnostic explanation derivation algorithms, allowing to prioritize either explanation optimality or derivation efficiency. The approach is demonstrated and evaluated in the context of a neural-network-controlled autonomous car, designed to avoid collisions.

AI.tldr: This paper introduces actual causality for explaining failures in autonomous Cyber-Physical Systems (CPS), providing theoretical foundations and two practical algorithms for deriving explanations, demonstrated on a neural-network-controlled autonomous car.
AI.motivation: Unexpected failures in black-box autonomous CPS (e.g., self-driving cars) are inevitable and potentially catastrophic. Deriving explanations for these failures is crucial for building trust and enabling future mitigation, but existing causality frameworks were limited to simple systems like image classifiers.
AI.method: The paper extends the actual causality framework to the CPS domain by addressing theoretical gaps. It proposes two system-agnostic explanation derivation algorithms that prioritize either explanation optimality or efficiency.
AI.result: The approach is demonstrated on a neural-network-controlled autonomous car designed to avoid collisions, showing practical feasibility and trade-offs between optimality and efficiency.
AI.conclusion: Actual causality can be effectively applied to explain failures in complex CPS, offering both theoretical foundations and practical algorithms for failure explanation.

title: KLip-PPO: A per-sample KL perspective on PPO-Clip
summary: Proximal Policy Optimization (PPO) is the standard policy-gradient algorithm for on-policy reinforcement learning. The literature presents it in two forms, a clipped surrogate that bounds the importance ratio between successive policies and a Kullback-Leibler penalty between them. These forms are treated as separate algorithms with their own gradients, their own hyperparameters, and their own reference implementations, and a sizeable body of empirical work compares them. We show that the gradient of the clipped surrogate is reproduced exactly by a Kullback-Leibler surrogate whose coefficient varies per sample, with closed-form dependence on the importance ratio and the advantage. The identity holds at every minibatch step and across the entire inner loop, and on five MuJoCo continuous-control benchmarks the two losses produce indistinguishable training curves. The reformulation exposes a structural feature of the clipped surrogate that the min notation hides. PPO-Clip's implicit per-sample penalty is a step function at the boundary of the trust region, and the shape of this coefficient is the natural design axis for generalising the algorithm. We sketch the resulting follow-up directions in the discussion.

AI.tldr: PPO's clipped surrogate gradient is exactly reproduced by a Kullback-Leibler penalty with per-sample adaptive coefficients, revealing clipped surrogate as a step-function penalty at trust region boundaries.
AI.motivation: To clarify the relationship between PPO's two forms (clipped surrogate and KL penalty) which have been treated as separate algorithms with distinct gradients, hyperparameters, and implementations, despite empirical comparisons being common.
AI.method: Mathematical derivation showing the clipped surrogate gradient equals a KL surrogate with per-sample varying coefficients, validated empirically on five MuJoCo continuous-control benchmarks.
AI.result: The gradient identity holds at every minibatch step across the entire inner loop; both losses produce indistinguishable training curves on MuJoCo benchmarks.
AI.conclusion: PPO-Clip's implicit per-sample penalty is a step function at trust region boundaries, and this coefficient shape is the natural design axis for generalizing the algorithm.

title: Universal Guideline-Driven Image Clustering via a Hybrid LLM Agent
summary: Unifying image clustering across different clustering scenarios remains challenging due to fundamental gaps among tasks. We introduce a Guideline-Driven Image Clustering Agent, the first universal framework that bridges these gaps through textual guidelines. To incorporate complex guidelines without task-specific training, we propose Generative Concept Proxy Modeling, which generates guideline-aware embeddings via concept proxy extraction. For scenarios requiring automatic cluster discovery, we introduce LLM Traversal based on Minimum Spanning Tree that selectively applies LLM reasoning for complex semantic judgments. Our method generalizes across diverse clustering scenarios spanning from general to fine-grained categorization, from global to local criteria, and from balanced to long-tail distributions. Our framework consistently outperforms specialized methods across diverse clustering tasks.

AI.tldr: A universal image clustering framework using textual guidelines and LLM reasoning that works across diverse clustering scenarios without task-specific training.
AI.motivation: Existing image clustering methods are task-specific and fail to generalize across different clustering scenarios (e.g., general vs. fine-grained, global vs. local criteria, balanced vs. long-tail distributions).
AI.method: Proposes a Guideline-Driven Image Clustering Agent with two key components: (1) Generative Concept Proxy Modeling for creating guideline-aware embeddings, and (2) LLM Traversal based on Minimum Spanning Tree for automatic cluster discovery using selective LLM reasoning.
AI.result: The framework consistently outperforms specialized methods across diverse clustering tasks spanning general to fine-grained categorization, global to local criteria, and balanced to long-tail distributions.
AI.conclusion: Textual guidelines can effectively bridge the gaps between different clustering scenarios, enabling a universal clustering framework without task-specific training.

title: SP-Mind: An Autonomous Reasoning Agent for Spatial Proteomics Analysis
comment: 23 pages, 6 figures. Accepted to ICML 2026. Equal contribution by Yucheng Yuan and Yuanfeng Ji
summary: Spatial proteomics enables single-cell-resolution characterization of protein expression within tissue architecture, playing a critical role in understanding tumor microenvironments and guiding precision medicine. However, current analysis workflows remain fragmented, requiring expert manual orchestration of heterogeneous tools and limiting research scalability and reproducibility. We present SP-Mind, the first autonomous AI agent designed to unify the spatial proteomics analysis pipeline, from raw multiplexed tissue imaging to downstream phenotype discovery. Equipped with expert-curated biological analysis skills and specialized computational tools, SP-Mind converts natural-language queries into end-to-end analytical workflows without task-specific fine-tuning. To rigorously evaluate its capabilities, we introduce SP-Bench, a comprehensive benchmark spanning diverse tissue types, comprising 102 tasks across 18 distinct categories. Through extensive evaluation on SP-Bench and established downstream tasks, SP-Mind achieves state-of-the-art performance compared to existing open-source biomedical agent baselines.

AI.tldr: SP-Mind is the first autonomous AI agent that unifies the spatial proteomics analysis pipeline, converting natural-language queries into end-to-end workflows, evaluated on SP-Bench with 102 tasks across 18 categories, achieving state-of-the-art performance.
AI.motivation: Current spatial proteomics analysis workflows are fragmented, requiring manual orchestration of heterogeneous tools, which limits scalability and reproducibility.
AI.method: SP-Mind is an autonomous AI agent equipped with expert-curated biological analysis skills and specialized computational tools that converts natural-language queries into end-to-end analytical workflows without task-specific fine-tuning. SP-Bench benchmark includes 102 tasks across 18 categories spanning diverse tissue types.
AI.result: SP-Mind achieves state-of-the-art performance compared to existing open-source biomedical agent baselines on SP-Bench and established downstream tasks.
AI.conclusion: SP-Mind successfully unifies and automates spatial proteomics analysis, improving scalability and reproducibility in this domain.

title: Enabling Robust Cloth Manipulation via Inference-Time Simulator-in-the-Loop Refinement
summary: Simulator-in-the-loop optimization offers a promising inference-time mechanism for robot manipulation. It uses a physical simulator as a backend rollout engine to evaluate candidate trajectories in parallel and refine nominal actions online, a paradigm proven effective in rigid-body manipulation where state and contact are relatively tractable. We bring this paradigm to real-world cloth manipulation from a single RGB input through three pillars. (i) We design a scalable synthetic-data generation and inference-time rollout pipeline built on FLASH, a deformable-object simulator that provides a practical balance among physical fidelity, numerical stability, and rollout efficiency. (ii) We develop a real-to-sim module, trained purely on synthetic data, that maps a single RGB observation to simulation-compatible cloth state by fusing pretrained visual features with learnable canonical tokens. (iii) We perform online planning by coupling a sparse-mesh rollout backend with prior-guided MPPI, anchored at an offline-distilled policy trajectory, preserving manipulation-relevant deformation and contact while enabling sufficient parallel rollout batches. Real-robot experiments show higher success rates and stronger robustness than baseline methods.

AI.tldr: This paper brings simulator-in-the-loop optimization to real-world cloth manipulation from single RGB input, using a synthetic-data trained real-to-sim module and prior-guided MPPI with a deformable-object simulator (FLASH) as rollout backend, achieving higher success rates and robustness than baselines.
AI.motivation: Simulator-in-the-loop optimization has proven effective for rigid-body manipulation, but it remains unexplored for deformable objects like cloth, where state estimation and contact dynamics are more complex and challenging.
AI.method: Three pillars: (i) synthetic-data generation & rollout pipeline using FLASH simulator; (ii) real-to-sim module trained purely on synthetic data fusing pretrained visual features with learnable canonical tokens for single RGB to cloth state mapping; (iii) online planning via prior-guided MPPI with sparse-mesh rollout backend anchored at offline-distilled policy trajectory.
AI.result: Real-robot experiments demonstrate higher success rates and stronger robustness compared to baseline methods for cloth manipulation tasks.
AI.conclusion: Simulator-in-the-loop optimization can be effectively extended to deformable object manipulation (cloth) by combining synthetic-data training, real-to-sim bridging, and prior-guided online planning, outperforming existing approaches in both success rate and robustness.

title: DREG: A Layer-Wise Jacobian Regularization as a General-Purpose Penalty
summary: We present a large-scale empirical study isolating the contributions of the Derivative Regularization penalty (DREG). Across a fully-crossed factorial sweep of 960 experiments spanning 4 activations, 6 regularizers, 8 datasets, and 5 random seeds, we ask: when, where, and why does DREG work? Our results establish three principal findings. First, DREG achieves the highest overall and clean-regime accuracy among all regularizers evaluated (significantly so against the unregularized baseline, Weight Decay, and IGPen; Wilcoxon $p \leq 0.031$). It ranks second in noise robustness behind Spectral Normalization (SN) - the only two layer-wise regularizers in the study. Second, DREG is globally the best-performing regularizer under GELU, the default activation in modern transformer architectures, particularly on both messy vision and messy NLP benchmarks, suggesting direct applicability to frontier deep learning settings. Third, DREG's advantage over competing regularizers is most pronounced under data scarcity, consistent with its role as a geometric inductive bias that substitutes for the regularizing effect of data volume. Throughout, DREG is applied with a single fixed hyperparameter $λ= 10^{-2.5}$ and no per-dataset tuning, supporting its characterization as a plug-and-play regularizer for neural networks with nontrivial Jacobian structure. These findings are consistent with DREG's design: concentrating regularization pressure on layers where the activation derivative is largest, rather than constraining the network uniformly.

AI.tldr: A large-scale study (960 experiments) shows Derivative Regularization (DREG) outperforms other regularizers overall and under GELU activation, excels in data-scarce settings, and works as a plug-and-play method without hyperparameter tuning.
AI.motivation: To systematically isolate and evaluate when, where, and why Derivative Regularization (DREG) works compared to other regularizers across diverse settings.
AI.method: 960 factorial experiments across 4 activations, 6 regularizers, 8 datasets, and 5 random seeds; DREG applied with fixed λ=10^{-2.5} without per-dataset tuning.
AI.result: DREG achieves highest overall/clean accuracy; ranks 2nd in noise robustness behind SN; best under GELU activation; most effective under data scarcity.
AI.conclusion: DREG is a plug-and-play regularizer that works by concentrating regularization pressure on layers with large activation derivatives, serving as a geometric inductive bias that substitutes for data volume.

title: Beyond Bayer: Task-Optimal Sensor Co-Design for Robust Autonomous-Driving Segmentation
summary: Robust perception underpins autonomous driving, and most recent progress comes from scaling the model-larger backbones, foundation models, and cooperative multi-agent fusion. We pursue a complementary, upstream question: what should the camera itself measure? Using a differentiable RAW-to-task pipeline, we decompose which sensor degrees of freedom benefit dense prediction. Learning the spectral colour-filter-array (CFA) weights is the dominant lever, improving mIoU by +0.017 (KITTI-360) and +0.023 (ACDC) over a fixed camera. In contrast, point-spread-function (optics) co-design is net-negative (-0.020 mIoU on KITTI-360) - a consequence of the data-processing inequality, which also bounds the task information that any downstream model, however large or cooperative, can recover. Noise co-optimisation is marginal, and counter to intuition enlarging the CFA tile beyond 2x2 consistently hurts, as the filters are confined to the rank three sRGB input. Because the intervention is at the sensor, the gains are model-agnostic; we validate robustness on ACDC's fog, night, rain, and snow, and conclude with a simple recipe: learn the 2x2 CFA weights and keep an identity PSF.

AI.tldr: This paper investigates which camera sensor properties most impact autonomous driving perception. Using a differentiable RAW-to-task pipeline, they find that learning spectral color-filter-array (CFA) weights significantly improves performance (+0.017-0.023 mIoU), while point-spread-function co-design actually hurts (-0.020 mIoU) due to data-processing inequality. They recommend a simple recipe: learn 2x2 CFA weights and keep identity PSF.
AI.motivation: While most autonomous driving research focuses on scaling models, backbones, and cooperative multi-agent fusion, this work explores an upstream question: what should the camera sensor itself measure to best benefit perception tasks?
AI.method: Using a differentiable RAW-to-task pipeline, the authors decompose and optimize various sensor degrees of freedom (CFA weights, PSF/optics, noise) to evaluate their impact on dense prediction tasks.
AI.result: Learning CFA weights is the most effective intervention (+0.017 mIoU on KITTI-360, +0.023 on ACDC). PSF co-design is net-negative (-0.020 mIoU). Noise co-optimization is marginal. Enlarging CFA tile beyond 2x2 consistently hurts performance.
AI.conclusion: The authors recommend a simple, model-agnostic recipe: learn the 2x2 CFA weights and keep an identity PSF. Gains are validated across fog, night, rain, and snow conditions.

title: Towards Federated Long-Tailed Graph Learning: An Energy-Guided Dual Decoupling Approach
summary: Federated Graph Learning facilitates collaborative graph modeling across distributed clients while preserving data privacy. However, real-world data categories frequently exhibit long-tailed distributions. Such statistical scarcity severely degrades performance in two ways: it biases the global model toward majority classes, and it structurally isolates minority nodes by submerging them in heterophilic, head-dominated neighborhoods. While existing methods attempt topology-agnostic statistical compensations, they often fail under data scarcity. Instead of recovering tail nodes, they overfit the structural noise from adjacent dominant classes, leading to representation degradation. To address these limitations, we propose FedEPD, a framework built on a dual decoupling paradigm that separates topological purification from semantic recalibration. Specifically, FedEPD utilizes distribution-aware Dirichlet energy pruning to filter spatial heterophilic edges. It then overcomes Non-IID distribution shifts by extracting robust global prototypes from topologically central nodes, which are incorporated into local representations via a spatial low-pass prototype injection. Furthermore, a two stage alternating optimization strategy strictly protects majority decision boundaries while improving minority accuracy. Extensive experiments demonstrate that FedEPD achieves state-of-the-art performance across diverse long-tailed benchmarks, yielding absolute improvements of up to 4.97% in Accuracy and 5.48% in Macro-F1.

AI.tldr: FedEPD is a federated graph learning framework that uses dual decoupling (topological purification + semantic recalibration) to address long-tailed data distributions, achieving SOTA results with up to 4.97% Accuracy and 5.48% Macro-F1 improvements.
AI.motivation: Long-tailed data distributions in federated graph learning cause global model bias toward majority classes and structural isolation of minority nodes in heterophilic neighborhoods, while existing topology-agnostic methods overfit structural noise.
AI.method: FedEPD proposes (1) distribution-aware Dirichlet energy pruning to filter heterophilic edges, (2) robust global prototypes from topologically central nodes via spatial low-pass injection, and (3) two-stage alternating optimization to protect majority boundaries while improving minority accuracy.
AI.result: State-of-the-art performance across diverse long-tailed benchmarks with absolute improvements of up to 4.97% in Accuracy and 5.48% in Macro-F1.
AI.conclusion: The dual decoupling paradigm effectively separates topological purification from semantic recalibration, addressing long-tail degradation in federated graph learning.

title: ArtiTwinSplat: Interactable Digital Twin Reconstruction via Gaussian Splatting from RGB-D videos
comment: Presented at the ICRA 2026 Workshop on Advances and Challenges in AI-Driven Automation and Robotic System Integration with Digital Twins, Vienna, June 2026
summary: Deploying robots in unstructured real-world environments needs accurate, interactive models of the objects. Constructing these models at scale remains a critical bottleneck for robotic system integration. We present ArtiTwinSplat, a framework that automatically constructs articulated, photo-realistic digital twins of objects directly from RGB-D videos, requiring no CAD models, simulation assets, or manual annotations. Our method is built on 3D Gaussian Splatting that preserve geometric fidelity and photometric realism, coupled with an unsupervised articulation discovery pipeline that recovers part structure and joint kinematics from observed motion alone. With tracking and optimization stages our method provides stable, queryable digital twins that support real-time rendering, viewpoint control, and interactive manipulation. Unlike prior methods confined to simulation, ArtiTwinSplat operates directly on real-world observations and produces twins that are immediately usable by downstream robot planning and learning systems. This method offers a practical, scalable pathway toward digital twin construction, lowering the integration barrier for articulated object manipulation in embodied AI and human-robot collaboration contexts.

AI.tldr: ArtiTwinSplat automatically creates articulated, photorealistic 3D digital twins of objects from RGB-D videos using 3D Gaussian Splatting and unsupervised articulation discovery, enabling real-time rendering and interactive manipulation for downstream robotic systems without CAD models or manual annotations.
AI.motivation: Deploying robots in unstructured environments requires accurate, interactive object models, but constructing these models at scale remains a critical bottleneck for robotic system integration, especially without CAD models or manual annotations.
AI.method: ArtiTwinSplat uses 3D Gaussian Splatting for geometric and photometric fidelity, coupled with an unsupervised articulation discovery pipeline that recovers part structure and joint kinematics purely from observed motion in RGB-D videos, followed by tracking and optimization stages.
AI.result: Produces stable, queryable digital twins that support real-time rendering, viewpoint control, and interactive manipulation, operating directly on real-world observations and usable by downstream robot planning and learning systems.
AI.conclusion: Offers a practical, scalable pathway for digital twin construction, lowering the integration barrier for articulated object manipulation in embodied AI and human-robot collaboration contexts.

title: Learning the Koopman Operator using Attention Free Transformers
summary: Learning Koopman operators with autoencoders enables linear prediction in a latent space, but long-horizon rollouts often drift off the learned manifold, leading to phase and amplitude errors on systems with switching, continuous spectra, or strong transients. We introduce two complementary components that make Koopman predictors more robust. First, we add an attention-free latent memory (AFT) block that aggregates a short window of past latents to produce a corrected latent before each Koopman update. Unlike multi-head attention, AFT operates in linear time and adds only $\approx$30k parameters ($3d^2 + T^2$, fewer than matched multi-head attention), yet captures the local temporal context needed to suppress error divergence. Second, we propose dynamic re-encoding: lightweight, online change-point triggers (EWMA, CUSUM, and sequential two-sample tests) that detect latent drift and project predictions back onto the autoencoder manifold. Across three benchmark systems -- Duffing oscillator, Repressilator, IRMA -- our model consistently reduces error accumulation compared to a Koopman autoencoder and matched-capacity multi-head attention. We also compare against GRU and Transformer autoencoders, evaluated both from initial conditions and with a 50-step context, and find that Koopman+AFT (with optional re-encoding) attains markedly lower long-horizon error while maintaining lower inference latency. We report improvements over horizons up to 1000 steps, together with ablations over trigger policies. The result is a fast, compact predictor that stays on the learned manifold over long horizons.

AI.tldr: The paper introduces attention-free latent memory (AFT) blocks and dynamic re-encoding mechanisms to make Koopman autoencoders more robust for long-horizon prediction, reducing drift and error accumulation on systems with switching, continuous spectra, or transients.
AI.motivation: Koopman operators with autoencoders enable linear latent-space prediction, but long-horizon rollouts drift off the learned manifold, causing phase/amplitude errors on systems with switching, continuous spectra, or strong transients.
AI.method: Two complementary components: (1) Attention-free latent memory (AFT) block that aggregates past latents for linear-time correction before each Koopman update; (2) Dynamic re-encoding using online change-point triggers (EWMA, CUSUM, sequential two-sample tests) to detect drift and project predictions back onto the manifold.
AI.result: On Duffing oscillator, Repressilator, and IRMA benchmarks, the model consistently reduces error accumulation vs. standard Koopman autoencoder and matched multi-head attention. Improves long-horizon prediction up to 1000 steps with lower inference latency.
AI.conclusion: A fast, compact predictor that stays on the learned manifold over long horizons, with AFT adding only ~30k parameters and linear-time complexity.

title: DramaDirector: Geometry-Guided Short Drama Generation
comment: 20 pages, 17 figures, 6 tables. Code is available at https://github.com/iLearn-Lab/DramaDirector
summary: Short dramas, with their rapid shot rhythms, dialogue-driven focus shifts, and demanding cinematographic grounding, pose challenges that prompt-level or text-only video generation pipelines struggle to meet. We study plot-to-short-drama generation, where a global plot and local context are transformed into visually grounded multi-shot videos. We propose DramaDirector, a geometry-grounded framework that lets the planner borrow cinematographic geometry from a gallery of real short-drama shots indexed by depth and pose. DramaDirector decouples each shot into static visual and dynamic narrative conditions, trains the planner with schema-constrained SFT and GRPO under a learned text-visual alignment reward, and retrieves depth-pose references to guide first-frame generation and image-to-video synthesis. We also introduce DramaBoard, a benchmark built from 35 live-action dramas, 2.8K episodes, and 81K shots, with structured storyboards and multi-dimensional evaluation protocols. Experiments show that DramaDirector improves over representative multi-agent and video generation baselines on faithfulness, consistency, and controllability. Our code is released at: https://github.com/iLearn-Lab/DramaDirector

AI.tldr: DramaDirector is a geometry-grounded framework for generating multi-shot short dramas from plots, using depth-pose reference retrieval, schema-constrained training, and a new benchmark (DramaBoard) to improve faithfulness, consistency, and controllability over existing baselines.
AI.motivation: Short dramas pose unique challenges (rapid shot rhythms, dialogue-driven focus shifts, demanding cinematographic grounding) that prompt-level or text-only video generation pipelines struggle to handle. The paper aims to enable plot-to-short-drama generation with proper visual grounding.
AI.method: DramaDirector uses a planner that borrows cinematographic geometry from a gallery of real short-drama shots indexed by depth and pose. It decouples each shot into static visual and dynamic narrative conditions, trains the planner with schema-constrained SFT and GRPO under a learned text-visual alignment reward, and retrieves depth-pose references to guide first-frame generation and image-to-video synthesis.
AI.result: Experiments show DramaDirector improves over representative multi-agent and video generation baselines on faithfulness, consistency, and controllability.
AI.conclusion: The proposed geometry-grounded approach effectively addresses the challenges of short drama generation, and the DramaBoard benchmark provides a structured evaluation resource for future research.

title: Probing the Misaligned Thinking Process of Language Models
summary: Large language models exhibit a growing range of misaligned behaviors such as strategic deception, sandbagging, and self-preservation. As they are increasingly deployed in high-stakes settings, it is critical to reliably detect such behaviors to ensure safe and responsible use. In this work, we propose to monitor misalignment by decomposing it into fine-grained cognitive processes -- misalignment indicators -- and detecting their presence in a model's internal activations via linear probes. We develop a taxonomy of 18 indicators spanning different misaligned behaviors, paired with an automated, meta-plan-guided pipeline that generates multi-turn training conversations. To rigorously evaluate generalization, we construct an out-of-distribution suite combining automated behavioral elicitation, established misalignment benchmarks, and natural benign conversations. Across 5 misaligned behaviors, our probes match a strong LLM judge with 0.935 AUROC on out-of-distribution benchmarks while keeping a low false positive rate on benign traffic. We further perform in-depth analysis to understand the probes and the model's internal representations of misalignment indicators.

AI.tldr: The paper proposes detecting misaligned behaviors in LLMs by decomposing them into 18 fine-grained cognitive indicators and training linear probes on internal model activations, achieving 0.935 AUROC on OOD benchmarks with low false positive rates.
AI.motivation: As LLMs exhibit increasingly sophisticated misaligned behaviors (strategic deception, sandbagging, self-preservation) and are deployed in high-stakes settings, there is a critical need for reliable detection methods to ensure safe and responsible use.
AI.method: Decomposes misalignment into 18 fine-grained cognitive indicators (taxonomy), uses an automated meta-plan-guided pipeline to generate multi-turn training conversations, trains linear probes on internal model activations to detect these indicators, and evaluates on an OOD suite combining automated elicitation, established benchmarks, and benign conversations.
AI.result: Linear probes match a strong LLM judge with 0.935 AUROC on out-of-distribution benchmarks while maintaining low false positive rates on benign traffic across 5 misaligned behaviors.
AI.conclusion: Monitoring internal representations via fine-grained cognitive indicators is an effective and reliable approach for detecting LLM misalignment, with strong generalization to unseen distributions.

title: Optimization-based Safe Trajectory Planning for Autonomous Ground Vehicle in Multi-Floor Scenarios
summary: The development of trajectory planning strategies for autonomous ground vehicles (AGVs) represents a prevailing research interest within the domain of intelligent transportation systems. This paper introduces a trajectory planning framework tailored for multi-floor scenarios. The framework consists of two main modules: the task planning module and the trajectory planning module. The task planning module involves a strategic selection phase, where a task planning strategy based on generalized voronoi diagrams (GVD) and multi-objective algorithms is proposed to select the floor exits for each floor. The trajectory planning module utilizes optimization-based methods to generate high-quality trajectories, and a warm-started hierarchical planning framework is designed to ensure rapid convergence. Additionally, for handling complex obstacle constraints, a correlation constraint calculation method is designed for reducing obstacle constraints in trajectory planning. Finally, the feasibility and effectiveness of the proposed framework are verified through simulations.

AI.tldr: This paper proposes a trajectory planning framework for multi-floor autonomous ground vehicles, combining task planning (using GVD and multi-objective algorithms for exit selection) with trajectory planning (optimization-based with warm-started hierarchical planning and correlation constraint reduction), verified through simulations.
AI.motivation: To address the lack of trajectory planning strategies for autonomous ground vehicles operating in multi-floor environments.
AI.method: A two-module framework: (1) Task planning using generalized Voronoi diagrams (GVD) and multi-objective algorithms to select floor exits; (2) Trajectory planning using optimization-based methods with a warm-started hierarchical planning framework and correlation constraint calculation for handling complex obstacles.
AI.result: Simulation results demonstrate the feasibility and effectiveness of the proposed framework.
AI.conclusion: The proposed multi-floor trajectory planning framework is feasible and effective for AGVs in multi-floor intelligent transportation scenarios.

title: Forget Without Compromise: Nexus Sampling for Streaming KV-Cache Eviction Under Fixed Budgets
summary: Long-context and agentic LLM workloads push the KV cache past any fixed memory budget, forcing the inference stack to permanently evict tokens at every step of a continuous-inference stream. Existing methods all share the same template, a per-step direct-attention score followed by deterministic top-$K$ selection, which converts a single below-cutoff step into an irreversible verdict and permanently erases any subtly important token that direct attention cannot single out from noise. To address this challenge, we propose Nexus Sampling, a training-free eviction method that pairs Nexus scoring, an iterative walk over direct attention that surfaces bridge tokens, with weighted reservoir sampling, which retains tokens with inclusion probability in place of deterministic top-$K$. Theoretically, we show that Nexus Sampling dominates deterministic top-$K$ in long-run survival of subtly important tokens. Empirically, at 80% KV cache eviction, Nexus Sampling matches dense attention within 1% on LongBench while outperforming top-$K$ baselines on retrieval-heavy tasks, with up to 10x smaller per-sequence cache memory.

AI.tldr: Nexus Sampling is a training-free KV cache eviction method that uses iterative Nexus scoring (to surface bridge tokens) and weighted reservoir sampling (probabilistic retention instead of deterministic top-K), achieving dense-attention-level performance at 80% eviction and dominating deterministic methods in long-run preservation of subtly important tokens.
AI.motivation: Existing KV cache eviction methods use per-step direct attention scores followed by deterministic top-K selection, which permanently erases subtly important tokens that cannot be distinguished from noise by direct attention alone, especially problematic for long-context and agentic LLM workloads with continuous inference.
AI.method: Nexus Sampling combines (1) Nexus scoring, an iterative walk over direct attention scores to surface "bridge" tokens that are indirectly important, and (2) weighted reservoir sampling, which retains tokens probabilistically based on inclusion probability rather than deterministic top-K selection.
AI.result: At 80% KV cache eviction, Nexus Sampling matches dense attention within 1% on LongBench, outperforms top-K baselines on retrieval-heavy tasks, and achieves up to 10x smaller per-sequence cache memory.
AI.conclusion: Probabilistic eviction via Nexus Sampling theoretically dominates deterministic top-K in long-run survival of subtly important tokens, offering a practical training-free solution for aggressive KV cache compression in continuous inference settings.

title: A Benchmark for Hallucination Detection in VLMs for Gastrointestinal Endoscopy
comment: Accepted at the Medical Image Understanding and Analysis (MIUA) 2026 conference
summary: Vision-language models (VLMs) are prone to hallucination, which remains a major barrier to their safe deployment in clinical practice. To date, most hallucination detection methods have been evaluated on radiology benchmarks such as MIMIC-CXR and VQA-RAD, while gastrointestinal (GI) endoscopy remains largely underexplored. In this paper, we benchmark nine hallucination detection methods on the Gut-VLM dataset, a GI diagnostic Visual Question Answering (VQA) dataset with 4,392 test VQA pairs, across five VLMs (MedGemma-4B, MedGemma-27B, LLaVA-Med-7B, LLaVA-v1.6-7B, and Lingshu-32B). The methods span three categories: black-box methods (RadFlag, SelfCheckGPT-NLI), gray-box methods (AvgProb, AvgEnt, MaxProb, MaxEnt, Semantic Entropy, and VASE), and a white-box method (ReXTrust). Our results show that ReXTrust, a white-box method, achieves the highest AUC across all five models, outperforming the strongest alternative method on each VLM by a statistically significant margin (paired permutation test, p < 0.001 in all cases), reaching a peak AUC of 93.0 on MedGemma-4B. White-box hidden-state access provides a consistent advantage of 19.5 AUC points on average (range: 9.5--33.5), with ReXTrust maintaining strong performance even on LLaVA-v1.6-7B (AUC 79.9), where black-box methods and clustering-based gray-box methods collapse to near-chance performance. Among non-white-box methods, token-level gray-box statistics (MaxEnt, MaxProb) are the strongest alternatives, outperforming both clustering-based gray-box methods (Semantic Entropy, VASE) and black-box approaches on average. We further identify confident confabulation, a failure mode in which models hallucinate with high inter-sample consistency or high token-level probability, as a systemic failure for both consistency and uncertainty-based methods.

AI.tldr: This paper benchmarks nine hallucination detection methods on GI endoscopy VQA (Gut-VLM dataset) across five VLMs, finding that white-box method ReXTrust significantly outperforms all others (up to 93.0 AUC), with white-box access providing a consistent 19.5 AUC point advantage, while also identifying "confident confabulation" as a systemic failure mode for non-white-box methods.
AI.motivation: Hallucination detection in VLMs is critical for safe clinical deployment, but most existing evaluations focus on radiology (MIMIC-CXR, VQA-RAD), leaving gastrointestinal (GI) endoscopy largely unexplored.
AI.method: Nine hallucination detection methods (black-box: RadFlag, SelfCheckGPT-NLI; gray-box: AvgProb, AvgEnt, MaxProb, MaxEnt, Semantic Entropy, VASE; white-box: ReXTrust) are benchmarked on the Gut-VLM dataset (4,392 VQA pairs) across five VLMs of varying sizes (4B-32B).
AI.result: ReXTrust (white-box) achieves highest AUC across all five models (peak 93.0 on MedGemma-4B), outperforming alternatives by statistically significant margins. White-box access yields 19.5 average AUC points advantage. Token-level gray-box methods (MaxEnt, MaxProb) outperform clustering-based gray-box and black-box methods. Identified "confident confabulation" as a systemic failure for consistency/uncertainty-based methods.
AI.conclusion: White-box hidden-state access provides substantial and consistent advantages for hallucination detection in GI endoscopy VQA, while non-white-box methods remain vulnerable to confident confabulation, suggesting future work should prioritize white-box approaches or develop methods robust to this failure mode.

title: Tractable Reasoning and Conjunctive Query Answering for Defeasible DL-Lite under Rational Closure
summary: In Description Logics (DLs), reasoning under Rational Closure (RC) is a well-known and widely accepted non-monotonic formalism to handle defeasible knowledge. In this paper, we study the application of RC to the core and horn variants of the DL-Lite family of lightweight description logics. We analyze both entitlement (instance checking) and Conjunctive Query (CQ) answering under RC. Our main contribution is providing a plug-in architecture that builds upon existing standard classical reasoners, establishing that reasoning and CQ answering under RC for DL-Lite can be done efficiently with minimal computational overhead.

AI.tldr: This paper shows how to efficiently apply Rational Closure (non-monotonic reasoning) to DL-Lite logics for instance checking and conjunctive query answering using a plug-in architecture that works with existing classical reasoners with minimal computational overhead.
AI.motivation: To extend Rational Closure, a well-established non-monotonic formalism for handling defeasible knowledge in Description Logics, to the lightweight DL-Lite family (core and horn variants), and to provide efficient reasoning and query answering methods.
AI.method: The authors propose a plug-in architecture that leverages existing standard classical reasoners to handle reasoning under Rational Closure for DL-Lite, including both instance checking (entailment) and Conjunctive Query (CQ) answering.
AI.result: Reasoning and CQ answering under Rational Closure for DL-Lite can be performed efficiently with minimal computational overhead compared to classical reasoning.
AI.conclusion: The plug-in approach enables practical non-monotonic reasoning in lightweight DLs by building on existing classical reasoners, making Rational Closure feasible for DL-Lite without significant performance penalties.

title: Beyond Monotonic Progress: Retry-Supervised Value Learning for Robot Imitation
summary: Human demonstrations for robot imitation learning often contain mistakes and corrective behaviors, such as imprecise grasps, object misalignment, unstable contact, and repeated attempts. While these segments are commonly treated as noisy or suboptimal data, they provide valuable evidence about when execution deviates from a desirable path and how task feasibility can be restored. However, existing reward and value models often rely on monotonic progress assumptions, which capture coarse task advancement but may overlook local execution errors and corrective behaviors in imperfect demonstrations. In this work, we propose ReTVL (ReTry-Supervised Value Learning), a framework for learning mistake-sensitive value functions from mixed-quality robot demonstrations by leveraging retry events as sparse supervision. ReTVL captures the local degradation-and-recovery structure around mistakes by combining global progress calibration with local pairwise preference learning induced by sparsely annotated retry keypoints. The learned value model is then used to reweight demonstration chunks for downstream behavior cloning, reducing the influence of harmful execution errors while preserving useful corrective behaviors. Experiments on real-robot manipulation tasks show that ReTVL produces more fine-grained value estimates than progress-based baselines and improves imitation learning from imperfect demonstrations.

AI.tldr: ReTVL learns mistake-sensitive value functions from imperfect demonstrations by using retry events as supervision, then reweights demonstrations for better imitation learning.
AI.motivation: Existing reward/value models rely on monotonic progress assumptions, which overlook local execution errors and corrective behaviors in imperfect demonstrations.
AI.method: ReTVL combines global progress calibration with local pairwise preference learning induced by sparsely annotated retry keypoints to capture degradation-and-recovery structures around mistakes.
AI.result: ReTVL produces more fine-grained value estimates than progress-based baselines and improves imitation learning on real-robot manipulation tasks.
AI.conclusion: Retry events in imperfect demonstrations are valuable supervision signals for learning mistake-sensitive value functions that improve downstream imitation learning.

title: 3D Masked Autoencoders are Robust Learners of Volumetric and Multimodal Cellular Representations for Microscopy
comment: Accepted at MICCAI 2026. Code available at: https://github.com/marrlab/mae3d-opencell
summary: Self-supervised learning in fluorescence microscopy often relies on 2D projections, despite the inherently three-dimensional nature of cells. We present a systematic comparison of 2D and 3D masked autoencoders (MAE-2D vs. MAE-3D) on volumetric microscopy data. Under matched architectures and training protocols, MAE-3D consistently outperforms 2D max-projection and slice-based variants on downstream single-cell tasks. We further align visual representations with a pretrained protein language model (ESM2) and show that cross-modal supervision yields larger gains for volumetric models. Channel cross-attention and frequency-domain regularization are critical for leveraging 3D spatial context. On a protein--protein interaction task, MAE-3D achieves a ROC--AUC of 0.865, outperforming prior methods by up to +0.025.
