There is a need for interpolation methods, but it's challenging due to high heterogeneity across different microwave instruments (varying characteristics, irregular intervals, geographic misalignment).
AI.method: A generative model that handles multiple geospatial sources across samples with irregular time intervals, geographic misalignment, and varying instrument characteristics. Trained with a self-supervised task where a random source is masked and reconstructed, combining infrared and microwave data.
AI.result: Self-supervised training significantly reduces Continuous Ranked Probability Score compared to supervised training. Combining infrared and microwave data improves performance over microwave-only. The generative model's ensemble mean matches a deterministic model, while producing a power spectrum much closer to true observations.
AI.conclusion: This is the first generative model capable of interpolating microwave images of cyclones by combining multiple microwave instruments and infrared observations at irregular time intervals.

title: AI Tokenomics: The Economics of Tokens, Computation, and Pricing in Foundation Models
summary: Tokens have become the practical accounting unit for modern foundation model services, linking information processing, computation, memory use, energy expenditure, pricing, and economic value. This paper develops a framework for AI tokenomics: the study of how tokens are generated, consumed, priced, allocated, and optimized across AI systems. We connect token-level technical costs to workflow-level production functions, enterprise resource allocation, measurement and instrumentation methods, and emerging market-design questions. The framework shows that token expenditure and economic value are distinct: value depends on marginal productivity, workflow position, hidden reasoning activity, risk, and downstream propagation effects. The paper concludes by identifying open research directions in hidden-token measurement, empirical calibration, token productivity, dynamic allocation, and token-based markets.

AI.tldr: This paper introduces "AI tokenomics" as a framework for understanding how tokens—the fundamental unit in AI systems—relate to technical costs, economic value, production functions, resource allocation, and market design.
AI.motivation: Tokens have become the practical accounting unit for modern foundation model services, yet there is no unified framework connecting token-level technical costs to economic value, resource allocation, and market design.
AI.method: The paper develops a conceptual framework linking token-level technical costs to workflow-level production functions, enterprise resource allocation, measurement methods, and emerging market-design questions.
AI.result: The framework demonstrates that token expenditure and economic value are distinct—value depends on marginal productivity, workflow position, hidden reasoning activity, risk, and downstream propagation effects.
AI.conclusion: The paper identifies open research directions in hidden-token measurement, empirical calibration, token productivity, dynamic allocation, and token-based markets.

title: Reasoning as Attractor Dynamics: Latent Memory Retrieval via Gibbs-Weighted Energy Minimization
summary: Large Language Models (LLMs) are traditionally viewed as autoregressive generators. However, from the perspective of collective computation, they function as high-dimensional Dense Associative Memories that store complex reasoning patterns as latent attractors. In this work, we investigate the energy landscape of mathematical reasoning. We posit that correct reasoning chains correspond to deep, wide attractor basins ("flat minima") in the model's output distribution, whereas hallucinations manifest as sharp, unstable local minima. To exploit this geometry, we introduce a retrieval mechanism based on a Gibbs measure of the trajectory's spectral entropy. By sampling multiple reasoning paths and weighting them by their inverse energy ($P \propto e^{-βE}$), we approximate the equilibrium distribution of the associative memory, effectively ``relaxing'' the system into a robust solution. Empirically, this physics-inspired mechanism improves Microsoft Phi-3.5 performance on GSM8K by 5.38\% (84.7\% $\to$ 90.1\%), demonstrating that inference is better modeled as a dynamic settling process into an attractor basin rather than greedy next-token prediction.

AI.tldr: The paper reinterprets LLMs as Dense Associative Memories where reasoning is an energy-based settling process. They propose a Gibbs-sampling retrieval mechanism that weights reasoning paths by spectral entropy, improving Phi-3.5 on GSM8K by 5.38%.
AI.motivation: Traditional autoregressive view of LLMs fails to explain hallucinations and reasoning robustness. The authors posit that viewing LLMs through the lens of collective computation and energy landscapes can provide a better theoretical foundation for understanding and improving reasoning.
AI.method: 1) Model reasoning as an energy landscape where correct chains are "flat minima" and hallucinations are "sharp local minima." 2) Introduce a Gibbs measure-based retrieval using spectral entropy of trajectories. 3) Sample multiple reasoning paths and weight by inverse energy (P ∝ e^{-βE}) to approximate equilibrium distribution.
AI.result: Applied to Microsoft Phi-3.5, the method achieves 90.1% on GSM8K, a 5.38% improvement over the baseline 84.7%.
AI.conclusion: Inference in LLMs is better understood as a dynamic settling process into attractor basins rather than greedy autoregressive prediction, and physics-inspired sampling mechanisms can significantly improve reasoning reliability.

title: UniRED: Unified RGB-D Video Frame Interpolation with Event Guidance
summary: High frame-rate RGB-D videos are crucial for a variety of downstream tasks, including motion analysis, dynamic scene understanding, and 3D reconstruction. However, due to hardware and sensing constraints, practical RGB-D cameras are typically limited to low frame rates, making it difficult to capture rapid scene dynamics. Existing video interpolation methods have achieved strong performance on RGB data, but they are not readily applicable to RGB-D scenarios, where they often yield blurry boundaries, visible artifacts, and degraded geometric consistency. Furthermore, motion estimation from only two boundary frames is inherently under-constrained in complex dynamic scenes. Event cameras, by contrast, provide asynchronous measurements with ultra-high temporal resolution, offering dense motion cues. In this paper, we propose a unified multimodal framework for RGB-D video interpolation that jointly exploits RGB appearance, depth geometry, and event-based temporal cues. Specifically, it first extracts and fuses RGB, depth and event cues, then estimates bidirectional flow with motion basis refinement for RGB and Z-axial refinement for depth, and finally synthesizes the target RGB-D frame via bidirectional warping and soft blending. In addition, we construct a new RGB-D-Event dataset to alleviate the scarcity of tri-modal training data. Extensive experiments on a public benchmark and the proposed dataset demonstrate that our method achieves superior photometric fidelity for RGB interpolation and stronger geometric accuracy for depth interpolation than existing approaches.

AI.tldr: This paper proposes a unified multimodal framework for RGB-D video interpolation that jointly exploits RGB, depth, and event camera cues to achieve high frame-rate RGB-D videos with better photometric and geometric quality.
AI.motivation: RGB-D cameras are limited to low frame rates, making it hard to capture rapid scene dynamics. Existing RGB interpolation methods fail on RGB-D data, producing blurry boundaries and geometric artifacts. Motion estimation from only two frames is under-constrained in complex scenes.
AI.method: A unified multimodal framework that: (1) extracts and fuses RGB, depth, and event cues; (2) estimates bidirectional flow with motion basis refinement for RGB and Z-axial refinement for depth; (3) synthesizes target frames via bidirectional warping and soft blending. A new RGB-D-Event dataset is also constructed.
AI.result: Superior photometric fidelity for RGB interpolation and stronger geometric accuracy for depth interpolation compared to existing methods, validated on a public benchmark and the proposed dataset.
AI.conclusion: Leveraging event cameras' ultra-high temporal resolution significantly improves RGB-D video interpolation quality, addressing both appearance and geometry challenges.

title: Abstractions of Queries in Ontology-Based Data Access
comment: Extended version of a paper published in the proceedings of KR 2025
summary:
In ontology-based data access (OBDA), multiple data sources are integrated via mappings to an ontology. We consider an OBDA setting based on existential rules and the certain answer semantics. We address the recent issue of query abstraction, which consists of abstracting data queries by translating them to the ontology layer. Since a perfect abstraction may not exist, the notions of minimally complete and maximally sound abstractions have been introduced.
  We study abstractions within an extension of UCQs with a limited form of inequality and a special predicate marking database constants. While this extension does not lead to an increased complexity of the problems of interest, it is able to express minimally complete abstractions, hence perfect abstractions when they exist. We also characterize maximally sound abstractions by making a new connection with the notion of maximum recovery stemming from data exchange.

AI.tldr: This paper studies query abstraction in ontology-based data access, focusing on minimally complete and maximally sound abstractions using an extension of UCQs with limited inequality and a special constant-marking predicate.
AI.motivation: To address the challenge of query abstraction in OBDA settings based on existential rules, particularly when perfect abstractions may not exist.
AI.method: Extends UCQs with a limited form of inequality and a special predicate for marking database constants, and draws a connection between maximally sound abstractions and the concept of maximum recovery from data exchange.
AI.result: The extension can express minimally complete abstractions (and thus perfect abstractions when they exist) without increasing complexity, and maximally sound abstractions are characterized via maximum recovery.
AI.conclusion: The proposed extension is expressive enough for meaningful query abstraction while maintaining tractable complexity.

title: QC-SMOTE: Quality-Controlled SMOTE for Imbalanced Classification
summary: Class imbalance poses a significant challenge in classification, where existing methods such as SMOTE often generate low-quality synthetic samples in regions with noise or class overlap. We propose QC-SMOTE, a quality-controlled oversampling framework that estimates minority sample reliability using a composite neighbourhood trustworthiness score combining local density, safe-level, and isolation from the majority class. Synthetic candidates are generated using an IPQ-guided best-of-K strategy that evaluates midpoint purity and, when required, majority clearance, with allocation guided by sample reliability and boundary informativeness. Generation behaviour adapts across overlap--imbalance regimes, adjusting interpolation range and selection criteria to match local data geometry. Low-quality synthetic samples are replaced with original minority duplicates when neighbourhood purity falls below an adaptive threshold, providing graceful degradation by reverting to duplication in severely noisy regions. Experiments on 30 imbalanced datasets using repeated stratified cross-validation show that QC-SMOTE achieves the strongest average AUC-ROC and Macro F1 among the compared oversampling methods, with particularly clear gains under moderate and severe imbalance. These results demonstrate the importance of quality-aware, geometry-adaptive synthetic sampling for robust imbalanced classification.

AI.tldr: QC-SMOTE is a quality-controlled oversampling method that generates synthetic minority samples using neighborhood trustworthiness scores, adaptive interpolation, and graceful degradation to duplicates in noisy regions, outperforming existing methods on 30 imbalanced datasets.
AI.motivation: Class imbalance causes poor classification performance, and existing oversampling methods like SMOTE often produce low-quality synthetic samples in noisy or overlapping regions.
AI.method: QC-SMOTE uses a composite neighborhood trustworthiness score (density, safe-level, majority isolation) to evaluate minority sample reliability, generates candidates via IPQ-guided best-of-K strategy with midpoint purity checks, adapts interpolation to local geometry, and replaces low-quality synthetics with original duplicates under an adaptive threshold.
AI.result: On 30 imbalanced datasets with repeated stratified cross-validation, QC-SMOTE achieves the strongest average AUC-ROC and Macro F1 among compared oversampling methods, especially under moderate and severe imbalance.
AI.conclusion: Quality-aware, geometry-adaptive synthetic sampling is crucial for robust imbalanced classification, as demonstrated by QC-SMOTE's consistent superiority.

title: ActiveScope: Actively Seeking and Correcting Perception for MLLMs
summary: Multimodal Large Language Models (MLLMs) have demonstrated impressive vision-language understanding, yet still struggle with fine-grained perception in high-resolution images. While existing training-free methods typically rely on attention-based localization or coarse-to-fine search, they are often misled by distractors and fail to locate multiple targets. Our investigation attributes these failures to Contextual Dominance, where salient distractors overwhelm target attention and cause inaccurate localization, and Semantic Bias, where global semantics cause the model to fixate on the most salient concept, resulting in incomplete localization in multi-object scenarios. Built on these insights, we propose ActiveScope, a training-free framework that enhances MLLMs by actively seeking and correcting perception. ActiveScope features two modules. The Semantic Anchor Localization (SAL) utilizes fine-grained semantic anchors to independently localize key targets, thereby mitigating semantic bias. The Interference-Suppressed Refinement (ISR) refines localization by suppressing attention on salient distractions to overcome contextual dominance. Extensive experiments on high-resolution image understanding benchmarks demonstrate that ActiveScope outperforms existing training-free methods (e.g., 96.34 percent accuracy on $V^{*}$ Bench), validating the superiority of the active search and self-correction paradigm. Our code is available at https://github.com/jasmine-ww/ActiveScope.

AI.tldr: ActiveScope is a training-free framework that enhances Multimodal Large Language Models (MLLMs) for fine-grained high-resolution image perception by addressing two key issues: Contextual Dominance (distractors overwhelming target attention) and Semantic Bias (model fixating on salient concepts). It introduces Semantic Anchor Localization (SAL) and Interference-Suppressed Refinement (ISR) modules, achieving 96.34% accuracy on V* Bench and outperforming existing training-free methods.
AI.motivation: Existing MLLMs struggle with fine-grained perception in high-resolution images due to two issues: Contextual Dominance (salient distractors overwhelm target attention) and Semantic Bias (global semantics cause fixation on the most salient concept, leading to incomplete multi-object localization). Current training-free methods fail to handle these problems effectively.
AI.method: ActiveScope is a training-free framework with two modules: 1) Semantic Anchor Localization (SAL) uses fine-grained semantic anchors to independently localize key targets, mitigating semantic bias. 2) Interference-Suppressed Refinement (ISR) refines localization by suppressing attention on salient distractions to overcome contextual dominance.
AI.result: ActiveScope achieves 96.34% accuracy on V* Bench and outperforms existing training-free methods on high-resolution image understanding benchmarks, demonstrating the effectiveness of active search and self-correction.
AI.conclusion: The proposed active search and self-correction paradigm (ActiveScope) effectively addresses fine-grained perception limitations in MLLMs without requiring additional training, validating its superiority over existing methods.

title: When CQs Go Wrong: Challenges in CQ Verification with OE-Assist
comment: Acceted poster at https://2026.eswc-conferences.org/program/accepted-papers/poster-demo/ 23rd European Semantic Web Conference (Satellite Event)
summary: Competency Questions (CQs) are the central component of CQ-verification, an established process in which an ontology is evaluated against a set of natural language questions to determine whether the intended purpose of the ontology has been properly modelled. However, CQ-verification is often time-consuming and error-prone, as it requires careful interpretation of linguistic nuances and precise alignment with formal ontology constructs. Ambiguities and complexity in CQs can further complicate this process, leading to inconsistent modelling decisions and verification outcomes. In this paper, we investigate what makes a CQ challenging and possible solutions to enhance the users' performance in the CQ-verification process. We experimented with the data of 19 participants who performed CQ-verification on 20 tasks using an LLM assistant to support ontology evaluation. The results show the necessity of a tool to refine CQs before publishing them to avoid ambiguity or excessive complexity in later phases of the ontology engineering process.

AI.tldr: This paper investigates how to improve Competency Question (CQ) verification in ontology engineering by identifying challenging CQ characteristics and testing LLM-assisted support with 19 participants, finding that tools for refining CQs before publication are needed to reduce ambiguity and complexity.
AI.motivation: CQ-verification is often time-consuming and error-prone due to linguistic ambiguities and complexity, leading to inconsistent modelling decisions and verification outcomes.
AI.method: The authors conducted an experiment with 19 participants performing CQ-verification on 20 tasks using an LLM assistant to support ontology evaluation.
AI.result: The results demonstrate the necessity of a tool to refine CQs before publishing them to avoid ambiguity or excessive complexity in later ontology engineering phases.
AI.conclusion: There is a need for tools that can refine CQs early to prevent ambiguity and complexity issues, thereby enhancing user performance in the CQ-verification process.

title: FlowPipe: LLM-Enhanced Conditional Generative Flow Networks for Data Preparation Pipeline Construction
summary: Data preparation pipelines improve data quality in machine learning by transforming raw tables into learning-ready data through sequential cleaning and feature transformation operators. However, automatically constructing such pipelines is computationally difficult because operator sequences are combinatorial and end-to-end evaluation is expensive. Existing state-of-the-art (SOTA) Multi-DQN methods still face three key limitations: decoupled value estimators weaken long-horizon credit assignment, dataset context is only weakly injected into the policy, and exploration is inefficient in a sparse search space with many invalid states. To address these issues, we propose FlowPipe, a unified framework that formulates pipeline synthesis as conditional probabilistic flow generation over a directed acyclic graph. FlowPipe uses Conditional Generative Flow Networks (C-GFlowNets) with a Trajectory Balance objective to connect terminal validation rewards with early pipeline decisions. It further introduces Deep Semantic Modulation through Feature-wise Linear Modulation (FiLM), allowing LLM-derived logical priors to condition the policy's internal activations according to dataset semantics. In addition, FlowPipe incorporates failure awareness into the flow objective to avoid invalid states and concentrate search on high-potential regions. Experiments on two benchmark suites with 74 real-world datasets show that FlowPipe outperforms SOTA baselines, improving accuracy by 11.96% on average and achieving 12.5x faster training convergence. Source code is available at https://github.com/KunyuNi/FlowPipe.

AI.tldr: FlowPipe is a unified framework for automatic ML data pipeline synthesis that uses Conditional Generative Flow Networks with Trajectory Balance, LLM-based semantic modulation, and failure awareness, achieving 11.96% accuracy improvement and 12.5x faster convergence over SOTA baselines on 74 datasets.
AI.motivation: Automatically constructing data preparation pipelines is computationally difficult due to combinatorial operator sequences and expensive end-to-end evaluation. Existing SOTA Multi-DQN methods suffer from decoupled value estimators weakening long-horizon credit assignment, weak injection of dataset context into policy, and inefficient exploration in sparse search spaces with many invalid states.
AI.method: FlowPipe formulates pipeline synthesis as conditional probabilistic flow generation over a DAG using C-GFlowNets with Trajectory Balance objective to connect terminal validation rewards with early decisions. It introduces Deep Semantic Modulation via FiLM to inject LLM-derived logical priors conditioned on dataset semantics, and incorporates failure awareness into the flow objective to avoid invalid states and focus search.
AI.result: On two benchmark suites with 74 real-world datasets, FlowPipe outperforms SOTA baselines with 11.96% average accuracy improvement and 12.5x faster training convergence.
AI.conclusion: FlowPipe effectively addresses key limitations of prior pipeline synthesis methods and demonstrates superior performance and efficiency. The source code is publicly available.

title: Hierarchical Spatial and Channel Aggregation for Cross-domain Few-shot Segmentation
summary: Cross-domain Few-shot Segmentation (CD-FSS) aims to learn generalizable segmentation capability from abundant annotated samples in the source domain, enabling accurate segmentation of novel classes in the target domain with only a few annotated samples. Existing CD-FSS methods mainly focus on mitigating feature distribution shifts caused by style gaps while ignoring significant differences in class semantic granularity and discriminative attributes across domains, leading to two key degradations in support-query matching: semantic over-alignment and attribute over-alignment. To this end, we propose the Dual Hierarchical Aggregation Network (DHANet), which comprises three key modules. First, the Hierarchical Spatial Aggregation (HSA) module performs multi-scale region aggregation of pixel features along the spatial dimension, generating hierarchical semantic-enhanced features to alleviate semantic over-alignment. Additionally, the HCA module conducts multi-scale attribute aggregation along the channel dimension, generating hierarchical attribute-enhanced features to mitigate attribute over-alignment. Finally, we propose the Online Probabilistic Semantic Bank (OPSB), which progressively constructs and updates class probability distributions from query predictions during inference, and samples multiple pseudo-prototypes as additional support information to mitigate insufficient support. Extensive experiments on four target-domain datasets demonstrate that our method achieves state-of-the-art performance.

AI.tldr: DHANet is a Dual Hierarchical Aggregation Network that addresses semantic over-alignment and attribute over-alignment in cross-domain few-shot segmentation via hierarchical spatial and channel aggregation, plus an Online Probabilistic Semantic Bank for pseudo-prototype sampling.
AI.motivation: Existing CD-FSS methods only address style-based feature distribution shifts but ignore semantic granularity and discriminative attribute differences across domains, causing semantic over-alignment and attribute over-alignment in support-query matching.
AI.method: Three modules: (1) HSA - multi-scale region aggregation along spatial dim for semantic-enhanced features; (2) HCA - multi-scale attribute aggregation along channel dim for attribute-enhanced features; (3) OPSB - online probabilistic semantic bank that constructs/updates class probability distributions from query predictions and samples pseudo-prototypes as extra support.
AI.result: State-of-the-art results on four target-domain datasets.
AI.conclusion: Hierarchical dual aggregation (spatial + channel) and online pseudo-prototype generation effectively mitigate two types of over-alignment in CD-FSS.

title: Themis: An explainable AI-enabled framework for Reinforcement Learning with Human Feedback
comment: The extended version of a paper published at the 2026 IEEE Conference on Artificial Intelligence (CAI). Includes an additional appendix with extended derivations and supplementary results. The main paper has 8 pages, 6 figures, 1 table
summary: Training safe Reinforcement Learning (RL) systems is inherently challenging, with no guarantee of avoiding unwanted behaviors. The most effective defenses against this are (i) transparency through explainability and (ii) alignment via human feedback. While both show promising results, no publicly available framework currently combines them. To address this, we introduce Themis, an XAI-enabled testing and evaluation framework for Reinforcement Learning from Human Feedback. Themis supports over 200 widely used environments and is easily configurable for experiments in RL, transparency, and alignment. Our results show that Themis can train reward models that match or outperform the environment's true reward signal using human preferences. We also provide a cloud-based platform for collecting human feedback and managing experiments. It is user-friendly, auto-scalable, and supports large participant groups across multiple experiments without extra development overhead. Tests show Themis can support one thousand users in back-to-back experiments on a modest commercial machine.

AI.tldr: Themis is a new XAI-enabled framework that combines explainability and Reinforcement Learning from Human Feedback (RLHF) to train safer RL systems, supporting 200+ environments and scalable human feedback collection.
AI.motivation: Current RL systems lack combined transparency (explainability) and alignment (human feedback) tools, making it difficult to avoid unwanted behaviors during training.
AI.method: Themis integrates XAI techniques with RLHF, offering a configurable framework for 200+ environments, along with a cloud-based platform for scalable human feedback collection and experiment management.
AI.result: Reward models trained with Themis match or outperform true reward signals; the platform supports up to 1000 concurrent users on a modest machine.
AI.conclusion: Themis successfully bridges explainability and alignment in RL, providing a practical, scalable open framework for safer RL training.

title: Grad Detect: Gradient-Based Hallucination Detection in LLMs
comment: Accepted to the 2nd Workshop on Compositional Learning at ICML 2026, Seoul, South Korea. Copyright 2026 by the author(s)
summary: Large Language Models (LLMs) have demonstrated remarkable capabilities across diverse tasks, yet they remain prone to generating hallucinations. Detecting these hallucinations is critical for deploying LLMs reliably in high-stakes applications. We present Grad Detect, a gradient-based approach for predicting hallucinations by analyzing layer-wise gradient patterns from a single forward-backward pass during inference. Our method shows that the internal gradient structure of a model carries rich information about the correctness of its output. This information is not accessible through output-level signals alone. We evaluate Grad Detect on several Q&A benchmarks across both hallucination detection and model abstention prediction, where it consistently outperforms confidence-based and sampling-based baselines. Through comprehensive layer ablation studies across all eleven models from four architectural families, we find that the final five layers concentrate over 97% of the discriminative gradient signal, enabling efficient deployment with minimal performance loss. Grad Detect provides a unified framework for predicting multiple dimensions of LLM reliability, offering strong predictive performance alongside interpretable insights into where and how model failures originate.

AI.tldr: Grad Detect is a gradient-based method that detects LLM hallucinations by analyzing layer-wise gradient patterns from a single forward-backward pass, outperforming existing methods and revealing that over 97% of discriminative signal concentrates in the final five layers.
AI.motivation: LLMs are prone to hallucinations, and detecting these errors is critical for reliable deployment in high-stakes applications. Existing methods relying on output-level signals are insufficient.
AI.method: Grad Detect predicts hallucinations by analyzing layer-wise gradient patterns obtained from a single forward-backward pass during inference, leveraging internal gradient structure rather than output-level signals alone.
AI.result: Outperforms confidence-based and sampling-based baselines across Q&A benchmarks for both hallucination detection and model abstention prediction. Layer ablation across 11 models shows the final 5 layers contain over 97% of discriminative gradient signal.
AI.conclusion: Grad Detect offers a unified framework for predicting multiple dimensions of LLM reliability with strong performance and interpretable insights into model failure origins, and can be deployed efficiently by focusing on final layers.

title: Training-free Cross-domain Few-shot Segmentation via Robust Semantic Representation and Matching
summary: Cross-domain Few-shot Segmentation (CD-FSS) aims to transfer knowledge learned from source domain to distinct target domains, segmenting unseen target classes with only a few annotated samples. Although existing methods have made significant progress, they still rely on training or fine-tuning processes, which incur high computational costs and risk overfitting. We observe that when powerful and general-purpose vision foundation models are incorporated into these methods, their performance shows only marginal improvement or even degrades due to overfitting. To address this, we eliminate trainable parameters and propose a training-free framework to avoid both training overhead and overfitting. Built upon the self-supervised vision encoder DINOv3, our framework addresses cross-domain challenges through three core modules. First, the Semantic-aware Feature Re-fusion (SAFR) module identifies and re-fuses features that emphasize semantic patterns, generating representations with enhanced semantic discriminability. Additionally, the Adaptive Support Enhancement (ASE) module narrows semantic gaps between support and query through robust query information aggregation. Finally, the Hybrid Prototype Matching (HPM) module integrates matching results from diverse prototypes to adapt to varying semantic complexity across domains. Extensive experiments on four target domain datasets demonstrate that our method achieves state-of-the-art performance in CD-FSS without any training.

AI.tldr: A training-free framework for Cross-domain Few-shot Segmentation using DINOv2, achieving SOTA performance with SAFR, ASE, and HPM modules.
AI.motivation: Existing CD-FSS methods rely on training/fine-tuning which incurs high computational costs and risks overfitting; even powerful foundation models show marginal gains or degrade when fine-tuned.
AI.method: A training-free framework built on DINOv2 with three modules: SAFR (semantic feature re-fusion), ASE (adaptive support enhancement via query aggregation), and HPM (hybrid prototype matching for varying semantic complexity).
AI.result: Achieves state-of-the-art performance on four target domain datasets without any training.
AI.conclusion: Eliminating trainable parameters effectively avoids overfitting and training overhead while leveraging vision foundation models for superior CD-FSS performance.

title: SAFARI: Scaling Long Horizon Agentic Fault Attribution via Active Investigation
comment: Published at the Second Workshop on Agents in the Wild: Safety, Security, and Beyond (AIWILD) at ICML 2026
summary: As autonomous agents tackle increasingly complex multi-step, multi-agent tasks, their execution trajectories have scaled beyond the constraints of even the largest context windows. Current methods for effectively diagnosing agent failures load the full trajectory into an LLM's context window, which suffers from attention dilution and fails when agentic traces inevitably exceed context limits. To address this, we introduce SAFARI (Scaling long-horizon Agentic Fault AttRibution via active Investigation), a framework that replaces linear context loading with a tool-augmented diagnostic loop. By equipping LLMs with a specialized toolbox to read and search trajectory segments alongside a persistent Short-Term Memory (STM) for cross-turn reasoning, SAFARI effectively decouples diagnostic accuracy from architectural context limits. Our experiments demonstrate that SAFARI outperforms state-of-the-art results by 20% on the Who&When dataset within a 1M token budget, and by 19% on TRAIL GAIA subset on a 25K token budget. Most significantly, SAFARI maintains a 0.58 precision even when the target fault resides 5x beyond the model's native context window, a scenario where traditional evaluators fail entirely.

AI.tldr: SAFARI introduces a tool-augmented diagnostic loop with Short-Term Memory to replace linear context loading for diagnosing agent failures in long-horizon, multi-agent tasks, achieving 20% improvements over SOTA and maintaining high precision even beyond the model's native context window.
AI.motivation: Current methods for diagnosing agent failures load full trajectories into LLM context windows, which suffers from attention dilution and fails when agentic traces exceed context limits. As tasks grow more complex, trajectories scale beyond even the largest context windows.
AI.method: SAFARI replaces linear context loading with a tool-augmented diagnostic loop, equipping LLMs with a specialized toolbox to read and search trajectory segments alongside a persistent Short-Term Memory (STM) for cross-turn reasoning, decoupling diagnostic accuracy from architectural context limits.
AI.result: SAFARI outperforms SOTA by 20% on Who&When (1M token budget) and 19% on TRAIL GAIA subset (25K token budget). It maintains 0.58 precision even when the target fault resides 5x beyond the model's native context window, where traditional evaluators fail entirely.
AI.conclusion: SAFARI effectively decouples diagnostic accuracy from architectural context limits, enabling reliable agentic fault attribution in scenarios that exceed traditional context window constraints.

title: Real vs. Complex Spectral Bases for Neural Operators: The Role of Green's Function Alignment
comment: Submitted to/in consideration for the 62nd Allerton Conference on Communication, Control, and Computing
summary: Fourier Neural Operators (FNO) learn solution operators of partial differential equations by parameterizing global convolutions in the complex Fourier domain. For real-valued PDE solutions, the complex FFT carries representational redundancy through conjugate symmetry. We introduce the Hartley Neural Operator (HNO), the exact real-valued mirror of FNO: it replaces the FFT with the purely real Discrete Hartley Transform and learns a single real multiplier per retained spectral mode, with no complex arithmetic. Because the real Hartley spectrum is not halved by conjugate symmetry, HNO retains twice as many frequency corners as FNO but one real weight where FNO carries a complex pair, so the two operators are iso-parametric at equal width and differ only in spectral basis. Our central thesis is that the best basis is a property of the operator. Self-adjoint elliptic operators (Poisson, biharmonic) have real, symmetric Green's functions that the real Hartley multiplier diagonalizes exactly, and HNO is favored there. Time-dependent operators carry phase, from oscillation in the wave equation to transport in advection, Burgers, and Navier-Stokes, which a real diagonal multiplier cannot represent, so FNO is favored there, and increasingly so with the operator's phase content, leaving the phaseless heat equation as the borderline case. Training both operators identically and benchmarking across PDE classes, initial-condition families, and boundary conditions, we find an elliptic-versus-time-dependent split that is monotone in operator phase content and matches the Green's-function theory we develop. Rather than a universal winner, our findings give a predictive rule: match the spectral basis to the symmetry of the solution operator.

AI.tldr: This paper proposes the Hartley Neural Operator (HNO), a real-valued alternative to Fourier Neural Operators (FNO), showing that FNO is better for time-dependent PDEs (with phase) while HNO excels for self-adjoint elliptic PDEs, providing a predictive rule to choose the spectral basis based on operator symmetry.
AI.motivation: FNO uses complex FFT which carries representational redundancy (conjugate symmetry) for real-valued PDE solutions. This motivates a purely real alternative that may be more efficient or better suited for certain PDE classes.
AI.method: Replace the FFT with the Discrete Hartley Transform (purely real), learning one real multiplier per retained spectral mode instead of complex pairs. This makes HNO iso-parametric with FNO but differing in spectral basis.
AI.result: Self-adjoint elliptic operators (Poisson, biharmonic) favor HNO; time-dependent operators with phase (wave, advection, Burgers, Navier-Stokes) favor FNO; heat equation is the borderline case. The split is monotone in operator phase content.
AI.conclusion: There is no universal winner; the best spectral basis depends on operator symmetry—match the basis to the solution operator's properties for best performance.

title: MM-TRELLIS: Point-Cloud Guided Multi-Modal 3D Vehicle Generation in Autonomous Driving
summary: Recovering realistic 3D vehicle models from autonomous driving scenes is crucial for synthesizing training data and building simulation environment. However, most existing vehicle generation methods fail to fully exploit multimodal sensors i.e. multi-view images and LiDAR point clouds) and rely on neural rendering based reconstruction, leading to low-quality mesh. Recently, native 3D generative models have made significant progress, yet they are not built for arbitrary multi-view inputs and often struggle with in-the-wild driving images. In this work, we present MM-TRELLIS, a multi-modal version of TRELLIS for in-the-wild 3D vehicle generation that integrates LiDAR and image sensors from autonomous driving datasets into native 3D generative models. Specifically, multi-view images are cycled as conditioning inputs, while LiDAR point clouds provide test-time guidance to ensure geometric accuracy and cross-view consistency. During denoising, we first align the guidance point cloud with the model priors, then enforce consistency between the generated geometry and the guidance point cloud. Finally, we introduce a voxel filtering strategy based on the opacity of 3D Gaussian Splatting to suppress floaters and produce clean meshes. Comprehensive experiments on Waymo dataset demonstrate our method outperforms existing methods in high-fidelity 3D vehicle generation. Code is available at https://github.com/HongliXiao/MM-TRELLIS.

AI.tldr: MM-TRELLIS is a multimodal 3D vehicle generation method that integrates multi-view images and LiDAR point clouds from autonomous driving data into a native 3D generative model, producing high-quality meshes with geometric accuracy and cross-view consistency, outperforming existing methods on Waymo dataset.
AI.motivation: Existing vehicle generation methods fail to fully exploit multimodal sensors (multi-view images & LiDAR), relying on neural rendering that yields low-quality meshes. Meanwhile, native 3D generative models cannot handle arbitrary multi-view inputs from in-the-wild driving scenes.
AI.method: (1) Multi-view images serve as conditioning inputs for the 3D generative model. (2) LiDAR point clouds provide test-time guidance for geometric accuracy and cross-view consistency. (3) During denoising, guidance point cloud is first aligned with model priors, then consistency is enforced between generated geometry and guidance. (4) A voxel filtering strategy based on 3D Gaussian opacity suppresses floaters and produces clean meshes.
AI.result: Comprehensive experiments on Waymo dataset demonstrate the method outperforms existing methods in high-fidelity 3D vehicle generation.
AI.conclusion: MM-TRELLIS successfully integrates multimodal sensor data (LiDAR + images) into native 3D generative models for in-the-wild vehicle generation, achieving superior quality and clean mesh outputs.

title: CineCap: Structured Reasoning with Spatio-Temporal Anchors for Cinematographic Video Captioning
summary: Cinematographic captioning aims to describe how a video is filmed using professional film-language concepts such as camera movement, shot size, depth of field, composition, and shooting angle. This capability is important for fine-grained video understanding and controllable movie-quality video generation, yet remains underexplored in existing multimodal large language models. Unlike question-answering-based evaluation of cinematic understanding, cinematographic captioning requires a unified open-form description over multiple cinematographic dimensions. This task is challenging for two main reasons: the model must infer professional cinematographic concepts from subtle visual evidence, and it must generate captions that are both comprehensive and accurate. Accordingly, we propose CineCap, a framework that combines structured reasoning with spatio-temporal anchors and reinforcement learning with comprehensiveness, accuracy, and gated coverage rewards. The former grounds professional cinematographic descriptions in explicit visual evidence and organizes them into compact atomic reasoning for supervised fine-tuning, while the latter improves the balance between descriptive completeness and factual correctness. In addition, we construct CineCap Bench, a benchmark of 472 manually annotated video-caption pairs for systematic evaluation. Extensive experiments show that CineCap consistently outperforms strong proprietary and open-source baselines, establishing a new state of the art for cinematographic captioning. The code, model checkpoint, and benchmark are publicly available in https://github.com/Hectormxy/CineCap.git.

AI.tldr: CineCap is a framework for cinematographic captioning that uses structured reasoning with spatio-temporal anchors and reinforcement learning to generate comprehensive and accurate descriptions of film-language concepts (camera movement, shot size, etc.), outperforming state-of-the-art baselines on a new benchmark (CineCap Bench).
AI.motivation: Cinematographic captioning is important for fine-grained video understanding and controllable video generation, but existing multimodal large language models fail to generate unified open-form descriptions over multiple cinematographic dimensions due to challenges in inferring professional concepts from subtle visual evidence and ensuring both comprehensiveness and accuracy.
AI.method: CineCap combines: (1) structured reasoning with spatio-temporal anchors to ground cinematographic descriptions in explicit visual evidence, organized into compact atomic reasoning for supervised fine-tuning; (2) reinforcement learning with comprehensiveness, accuracy, and gated coverage rewards to balance descriptive completeness and factual correctness. A benchmark (CineCap Bench) of 472 manually annotated video-caption pairs was also constructed.
AI.result: CineCap consistently outperforms strong proprietary and open-source baselines, establishing a new state of the art for cinematographic captioning on the CineCap Bench benchmark.
AI.conclusion: The proposed CineCap framework effectively addresses the underexplored task of cinematographic captioning, and its code, model checkpoint, and benchmark are publicly available for future research.

title: TrOCR for Medieval HTR: A Systematic Ablation Study with Cross-Dataset Validation
comment: Accepted at Document Analysis Systems Workshop 2026 (ICDAR Satellite event)
summary: Fine-tuning transformer-based handwritten text recognition (HTR) models on medieval manuscripts is challenging because these models are pre-trained on modern text and must adapt to a very different visual domain. This paper studies how three controllable fine-tuning choices (contrast normalization, data augmentation, and layer freezing) affect recognition accuracy when adapting TrOCR to small historical datasets. We run controlled experiments on a 13th-century Italian manuscript (I-CT 91 "Cortonese") and replicate the same experimental grid on the public READ-16 benchmark as robustness evidence. On Cortonese, our best configuration achieves 8.03% character error rate (CER). Statistical comparisons across 13 configurations show that freezing up to three encoder layers or six decoder layers does not significantly harm accuracy, while deeper freezing becomes progressively detrimental. Removing contrast normalization (CLAHE) yields 7.84% CER, comparable to a domain-specialized baseline, suggesting strong optimization can reduce reliance on image preprocessing. Cross-dataset validation on READ-16 shows that decoder freezing thresholds transfer more robustly than encoder thresholds, and combined freezing strategies require dataset-specific re-validation. Finally, we use Grad-CAM gradient attributions and decoder cross-attention maps to diagnose error patterns and failure modes revealed by the ablations. Source code is available at https://github.com/LaudareProject/TrOCR-analysis

AI.tldr: This paper investigates how contrast normalization (CLAHE), data augmentation, and layer freezing affect TrOCR-based HTR on medieval manuscripts, achieving 8.03% CER on the Cortonese dataset and 7.84% CER without CLAHE, with analysis of freezing thresholds and cross-dataset transferability.
AI.motivation: Fine-tuning transformer-based HTR models on medieval manuscripts is challenging due to domain shift from modern pre-training data; the authors aim to systematically understand how controllable fine-tuning choices affect recognition accuracy on small historical datasets.
AI.method: Controlled experiments varying contrast normalization (CLAHE), data augmentation, and layer freezing strategies, tested on a 13th-century Italian manuscript (Cortonese) and validated on the READ-16 benchmark; error analysis uses Grad-CAM and decoder cross-attention maps.
AI.result: Best configuration achieved 8.03% CER on Cortonese; removing CLAHE yielded 7.84% CER; freezing up to 3 encoder or 6 decoder layers caused no significant harm; decoder freezing thresholds transfer better across datasets than encoder thresholds.
AI.conclusion: Optimization can reduce reliance on image preprocessing; freezing strategies must be validated per dataset; combined freezing is not robustly transferable; attention-based diagnostics reveal failure modes.

title: LaGO: Latent Action Guidance for Online Reinforcement Learning
comment: 9 pages, 2 figures. Accepted at the ICML 2026 Workshop on Large Language Models for Planning (LM4Plan)
summary: Large language models (LLMs) have shown strong potential for planning and sequential decision-making, but prior work often relies on using them as direct controllers, which requires precise action generation and can be unreliable in practice. This paper proposes Latent Action Guidance for Online Reinforcement Learning (LaGO), a framework that uses a pretrained LLM as a latent action prior to softly guide online policy optimization, rather than treating the LLM as an explicit planner or controller. Experiments on both a discrete-control benchmark, CLEVR-Robot, and a continuous-control benchmark, Meta-World, demonstrate that LaGO consistently improves both reward and success rate over Vanilla PPO. In particular, LaGO increases the average success rate from 15.1% to 27.2% on CLEVR-Robot and from 2.7% to 15.2% on Meta-World. Our analysis further shows that stronger pretrained LLMs provide more effective guidance, suggesting that LLM knowledge can improve planning and online decision-making.

AI.tldr: LaGO uses a pretrained LLM as a latent action prior to softly guide online RL (PPO), improving success rates from 15.1%→27.2% on CLEVR-Robot and 2.7%→15.2% on Meta-World, without using the LLM as a direct controller.
AI.motivation: LLMs have potential for planning/decision-making, but using them as direct controllers is unreliable due to precise action generation requirements. The authors propose soft guidance instead of explicit control.
AI.method: LaGO leverages a pretrained LLM as a latent action prior to softly guide online policy optimization (PPO), avoiding direct LLM action generation.
AI.result: LaGO consistently improves reward and success rate over Vanilla PPO on both CLEVR-Robot (15.1%→27.2%) and Meta-World (2.7%→15.2%). Stronger LLMs provide more effective guidance.
AI.conclusion: LLM knowledge can improve planning and online decision-making when used as a soft prior rather than a direct controller.

title: REDI-Match: Rotation-Equivariant Distillation for Efficient and Robust Dense Matching
summary: Vision Foundation Models (VFMs) have significantly advanced dense feature matching, yet severe in-plane rotation remains a critical challenge. Existing solutions face a fundamental dilemma: data-driven methods require inefficient parameter scaling to implicitly learn rotations, whereas strictly equivariant networks lack the semantic capacity of modern VFMs. Consequently, current frameworks typically freeze VFMs and shift the entire burden of rotation generalization to the downstream decoder. To break this architectural bottleneck, we propose REDI-Match, an efficient framework driven by a novel Rotation-Equivariant Distillation (REDI) paradigm. Instead of relying on rotation data augmentation to establish rotational correspondences, REDI distills the non-equivariant semantic representations of a VFM into a lightweight, strictly rotation-equivariant encoder, leveraging an equivariant geometric architecture to constrain robust high-dimensional semantics. To fully exploit these features, we equip the decoder with an entropy-driven spatial alignment module. By evaluating discrete rotation hypotheses, this mechanism explicitly locks onto the canonical coordinate system, eliminating global ambiguity before continuous refinement. Extensive experiments demonstrate that REDI-Match establishes a new state-of-the-art (SOTA) across multiple benchmarks. Notably, it achieves a 13.89% absolute pose accuracy improvement on the highly challenging SatAst dataset while operating 1.9x faster than the current SOTA (RoMa v2), enabling real-time inference (~41 FPS) on a single RTX 4090 GPU. Code: https://github.com/YinjiGe/REDI-Match.

AI.tldr: REDI-Match introduces a Rotation-Equivariant Distillation (REDI) paradigm that distills semantic knowledge from Vision Foundation Models into a strictly rotation-equivariant encoder, paired with an entropy-driven spatial alignment module, achieving SOTA pose accuracy (13.89% improvement on SatAst) with 1.9x faster inference than RoMa v2 (~41 FPS on RTX 4090).
AI.motivation: Existing VFM-based dense matching methods struggle with severe in-plane rotation: data-driven approaches need costly parameter scaling to learn rotations implicitly, while strictly equivariant networks lack the rich semantic capacity of modern VFMs. Current practice freezes VFMs and pushes all rotation generalization burden onto the decoder, creating an architectural bottleneck.
AI.method: (1) Rotation-Equivariant Distillation (REDI): distills non-equivariant semantic representations from a frozen VFM into a lightweight, strictly rotation-equivariant encoder using an equivariant geometric architecture. (2) Entropy-driven spatial alignment module in the decoder that evaluates discrete rotation hypotheses to explicitly lock onto the canonical coordinate system, eliminating global ambiguity before continuous refinement.
AI.result: Achieves new SOTA across multiple benchmarks, with 13.89% absolute pose accuracy improvement on SatAst dataset, operates 1.9x faster than RoMa v2, enabling real-time inference at ~41 FPS on a single RTX 4090 GPU.
AI.conclusion: REDI-Match effectively breaks the rotation generalization bottleneck in VFM-based matching by combining equivariant distillation with explicit rotation hypothesis evaluation, delivering both superior accuracy and real-time efficiency.

title: Cost-Optimal Decision Diagrams for Stochastic Boolean Function Evaluation
summary: In many decision-making scenarios, acquiring information incurs different costs. We consider the problem of constructing a deterministic evaluation strategy that minimizes the expected cost of evaluating a propositional formula under variable costs and a probability distribution over truth assignments. We present a branch-and-bound algorithm with variable-selection heuristics, pruning, and caching. To the best of our knowledge, it is the first practical exact algorithm for this level of generality. Experiments on random instances demonstrate scalability and quantify the efficiency-quality trade-off of a greedy beam-search variant. We additionally evaluate a structured heart-disease diagnosis instance. Finally, we prove that the problem is $\#P$-hard and contained in $\mathrm{PSPACE}$.

AI.tldr: Proposes the first practical exact algorithm for minimizing expected evaluation cost of propositional formulas under variable-specific costs and probabilistic truth assignments, combining branch-and-bound with heuristics, pruning, and caching.
AI.motivation: In many real-world decision-making scenarios, acquiring information incurs different costs (e.g., medical tests). Existing methods lacked practical exact algorithms for minimizing expected evaluation cost under variable costs and probability distributions.
AI.method: A branch-and-bound algorithm with variable-selection heuristics, pruning, and caching. Also a greedy beam-search variant for efficiency-quality trade-offs.
AI.result: Experiments on random instances show scalability; structured heart-disease diagnosis case study demonstrates real-world applicability; theoretical results show the problem is #P-hard and in PSPACE.
AI.conclusion: This is the first practical exact algorithm for evaluation strategy optimization under general variable costs and probabilities, with both theoretical hardness results and empirical validation.

title: UniTranslator: A Unified Multi-modal Framework for End-to-end In-Image Machine Translation
summary: In-Image Machine Translation (IIMT) aims to translate scene text in an image and render the translated text back into the original regions while preserving the overall visual appearance. Recent unified multimodal models provide a promising solution by combining visual-text understanding and image generation within a single framework. However, directly adapting such models to IIMT remains challenging. In particular, they often suffer from understanding-generation conflicts, where the translation inferred during understanding is inconsistent with the text supervision used in generation, and spatial position misalignment, where the rendered text does not accurately match the target text regions. To address these issues, we present UniTranslator, a unified multimodal framework for IIMT that tightly couples translation understanding and text editing. Specifically, we introduce an Understand-Generation Alignment Module (UGAM) to bridge the representation gap between understanding and generation, encouraging semantic consistency between translated content prediction and text rendering. We further propose a Spatial Mask Decoder (SMD) with pixel-level supervision over text regions to improve spatial grounding, geometric alignment, and layout controllability during generation. Extensive experiments on multiple benchmarks demonstrate that UniTranslator achieves state-of-the-art performance across diverse language directions and complex real-world layouts. Moreover, our results reveal a strong mutual reinforcement effect between translation understanding and image generation, highlighting the advantage of unified translation multimodal learning. Code is available at https://github.com/SeerRay-Lab/Unitranslator.

AI.tldr: UniTranslator is a unified multimodal framework for In-Image Machine Translation that addresses understanding-generation conflicts and spatial misalignment via an Understand-Generation Alignment Module (UGAM) and a Spatial Mask Decoder (SMD), achieving state-of-the-art performance on multiple benchmarks.
AI.motivation: Existing unified multimodal models suffer from (1) understanding-generation conflicts where translated text during understanding mismatches text supervision in generation, and (2) spatial position misalignment where rendered text fails to accurately match target text regions.
AI.method: UniTranslator introduces (1) Understand-Generation Alignment Module (UGAM) to bridge representation gaps between understanding and generation for semantic consistency, and (2) Spatial Mask Decoder (SMD) with pixel-level supervision over text regions for improved spatial grounding and layout controllability.
AI.result: Achieves state-of-the-art performance across diverse language directions and complex real-world layouts. Results demonstrate strong mutual reinforcement between translation understanding and image generation.
AI.conclusion: UniTranslator effectively resolves key IIMT challenges through unified multimodal learning, highlighting the advantage of tightly coupling translation understanding and text editing.

title: Decentralised AI Training and Inference with BlockTrain
summary: Frontier AI training is increasingly shaped by access to dense, centrally controlled accelerator clusters. This creates a structural advantage for hyperscalers and large centralized laboratories, and makes open or independent AI efforts depend on scarce capital, privileged infrastructure, and data-center geography. We present Spheroid BlockTrain, a decentralized training protocol in which a model is partitioned into independently trainable blocks, each optimized on a local objective derived from the same global target and composed at inference into one model. On byte-level WikiText, BlockTrain reaches cross entropy 1.359 (perplexity 3.89), within about 0.04 CE of a same-setup end-to-end Transformer reference, while each active worker trains only one block and avoids full-model optimizer state. A shared six-worker block training run reaches CE 1.385 by averaging same-block updates into one assembled model. HTTP/TCP transport experiments move real serialized checkpoints and updates, including a public-IP three-host run that improves CE from 5.580 to 1.811 while moving 15.22 GB. For inference, the current BlockTrain path uses one block-stack traversal per full output and serves over direct TCP across three public-network GPU hosts up to a 75.80B-parameter logical fp16 shape, outperforming a matched plain-autoregressive TCP pipeline baseline because it emits a full sequence per WAN pipeline traversal rather than one token per traversal.

AI.tldr: Spheroid BlockTrain is a decentralized training protocol that partitions models into independently trainable blocks, achieving near-Transformer quality (cross-entropy 1.359 vs 1.32 baseline) while each worker trains only one block. It demonstrates real distributed training over public networks with 15.22 GB data transfer, and inference on up to 75.8B parameter models outperforming autoregressive pipelines by emitting full sequences per traversal.
AI.motivation: To address the structural advantage of hyperscalers in AI training by enabling decentralized, open training without dependence on dense centralized accelerator clusters, scarce capital, or privileged data-center geography.
AI.method: Spheroid BlockTrain partitions a model into independently trainable blocks, each optimized on a local objective derived from the same global target, composed at inference. Uses HTTP/TCP transport for serialized checkpoints and updates with same-block averaging across workers.
AI.result: CE 1.359 (perplexity 3.89) on byte-level WikiText, within 0.04 CE of end-to-end Transformer baseline. Six-worker run achieves CE 1.385. Public-IP three-host run improves CE from 5.580 to 1.811 moving 15.22 GB. Inference supports up to 75.80B-parameter logical fp16 shape, outperforming plain-autoregressive TCP pipeline.
AI.conclusion: BlockTrain demonstrates that decentralized model training is viable and can approach centralized quality while avoiding full-model optimizer state per worker, with efficient inference over WAN.

title: Ill-Posed by Design: Probing Evidence Use in VLMs
summary: Counterfactual analysis is widely used to study evidence use in vision-language models, but its diagnostic value is limited on well-posed tasks: when several cues independently support the same answer, removing one may not change the prediction. We propose monocular metric object-size estimation as an ill-posed diagnostic setting for evidence selection: because physical size cannot be determined from a single uncalibrated image, models must rely on imperfect cues category priors, target appearance, local context, apparent image size, and scene geometry. We assemble Metric VQA ($10{,}813$ dimension queries from Objectron and $331$ tape-measured in-the-wild scenes) and evaluate $12$ open-weight VLMs ($3$--$397$\,B parameters) with counterfactual analysis decomposing six visual and language evidence channels. Even the largest VLMs tested (Qwen3-VL-235B, Qwen3.5-397B, InternVL3.5-241B) trail a text-only frontier LLM on the in-the-wild split. The diagnostic analysis shows: target identity is the most load-bearing cue, target pixels and local context help only some models, apparent size shifts predictions without a directional readout, and global scene geometry is largely unused. We analyze LoRA fine-tuning as an actionable intervention specific to metric estimation: while the task is learnable, the models do not learn to leverage scene geometry.

AI.tldr: This paper proposes metric object-size estimation as an ill-posed diagnostic task to study evidence selection in VLMs, finding that even the largest models (up to 397B parameters) underperform text-only LLMs and fail to leverage scene geometry cues.
AI.motivation: Counterfactual analysis has limited diagnostic value on well-posed tasks because multiple cues independently support the same answer. The authors propose metric object-size estimation as an ill-posed setting where no single cue is sufficient, allowing more informative analysis of evidence selection in VLMs.
AI.method: The authors assemble Metric VQA dataset (10,813 queries from Objectron + 331 real-world scenes). They evaluate 12 open-weight VLMs (3B-397B parameters) using counterfactual analysis that decomposes six visual and language evidence channels, and analyze LoRA fine-tuning as an intervention.
AI.result: The largest VLMs (Qwen3-VL-235B, Qwen3.5-397B, InternVL3.5-241B) still trail a text-only frontier LLM on in-the-wild splits. Target identity is the most load-bearing cue; target pixels and local context help only some models; apparent size shifts predictions without directional readout; global scene geometry is largely unused.
AI.conclusion: While metric estimation is learnable via LoRA fine-tuning, models do not learn to effectively leverage scene geometry cues, indicating a fundamental gap in how VLMs process spatial/geometric information for ill-posed tasks.

title: Scaling Laws for Task-Specific LLM Distillation
summary: Large Language Models (LLMs) achieve strong performance across a growing range of domains, yet their scale poses deployment challenges in applications where latency and cost constraints are critical. This paper derives empirical scaling laws for domain-specific LLM compression, quantifying how in-domain and general knowledge performance scale with dataset size, compression ratio, supervision format, and iterative pruning schedule. Using quantitative finance as our application domain, we compare logit-based and LoRA-based distillation under iterative structural pruning, introducing a blended chain-of-thought supervision loss that stabilizes KL-divergence distillation over reasoning traces. In-domain task quality degrades predictably under compression while general-knowledge benchmarks collapse well before the same point; supervision format is the key driver of this tradeoff, with chain-of-thought supervision actively recovering general knowledge that pruning erases. We release the headline dataset FinHeadlineMix, scaling law results, and practical recommendations to provide a reusable framework for domain-specific compression decisions.

AI.tldr: This paper derives empirical scaling laws for domain-specific LLM compression in quantitative finance, showing that while in-domain performance degrades predictably under compression, general knowledge collapses earlier, and chain-of-thought supervision can recover erased general knowledge.
AI.motivation: LLMs are too large for deployment in latency/cost-constrained applications; there is a need to understand how compression affects domain-specific vs. general knowledge performance systematically.
AI.method: The authors compare logit-based and LoRA-based distillation under iterative structural pruning, introduce a blended chain-of-thought supervision loss that stabilizes KL-divergence distillation over reasoning traces, and derive scaling laws across dataset size, compression ratio, supervision format, and pruning schedule.
AI.result: In-domain task quality degrades predictably under compression; general-knowledge benchmarks collapse well before the same point; supervision format is the key driver of this tradeoff; chain-of-thought supervision actively recovers general knowledge erased by pruning.
AI.conclusion: The paper provides a reusable framework (including the FinHeadlineMix dataset and scaling laws) for making domain-specific compression decisions.

title: TIGER: Taming Identity, Geometry, and Generative Priors for High-Quality Face Video Restoration
summary: Face Video Restoration (FVR) aims to recover high-fidelity facial videos from degraded input while preserving identity and semantic consistency across frames. Existing methods often struggle to simultaneously address three key challenges: identity shift, viewpoint-entangled guidance, and perceptual realism. To tackle these issues, we propose TIGER, a structured tri-prior fusion framework that Tames Identity, Geometry, and gEnerative pRiors for high-quality FVR. Specifically, an Identity Prior is first established by injecting subject-discriminative embeddings into the latent space, effectively anchoring the subject's identity against severe degradations. Then, to provide temporally consistent structural guidance for dynamic videos, TIGER constructs a Geometry Prior by lifting 2D reference cues into a disentangled 3D parameter space, creating a geometric anchor through cross-source parameter fusion. Moreover, to achieve maximum efficiency without compromising realism, we harness the video generation model's Generative Prior through a one-step rectified flow. We further design a progressive three-stage training optimization strategy that refines structural fidelity, textural reconstruction, and distribution-level realism to ensure robust optimization. We also construct a large-scale FVR dataset to facilitate robust training and standardized evaluation. Extensive experiments demonstrate that TIGER achieves state-of-the-art performance in both identity fidelity and temporal stability, delivering a high-quality, efficient and identity-consistent FVR. Project page: https://yzhoulv.github.io/Tiger/.

AI.tldr: TIGER is a tri-prior fusion framework (Identity, Geometry, Generative) for face video restoration that addresses identity shift, viewpoint entanglement, and perceptual realism, achieving state-of-the-art performance.
AI.motivation: To overcome three key challenges in face video restoration: identity shift across frames, viewpoint-entangled guidance, and maintaining perceptual realism while being efficient.
AI.method: Proposes TIGER framework with: (1) Identity Prior via subject-discriminative embeddings, (2) Geometry Prior by lifting 2D cues to disentangled 3D parameter space, (3) Generative Prior via one-step rectified flow; plus a progressive three-stage training optimization strategy and a large-scale FVR dataset.
AI.result: State-of-the-art performance in identity fidelity and temporal stability, delivering high-quality, efficient, and identity-consistent face video restoration.
AI.conclusion: TIGER effectively solves identity shift, viewpoint entanglement, and realism trade-offs through structured tri-prior fusion, setting a new SOTA for FVR.

title: Can Scale Save Us From Plasticity Loss in Large Language Models?
summary: The loss of plasticity - the ability of a network to learn new information after having already learned older information - is a fundamental challenge in creating artificial neural networks capable of continual learning. Although this phenomenon has been known for decades, it has mostly been studied in older, relatively small architectures and rarely in natural-language domains. To determine whether loss of plasticity remains a problem in the modern transformer-based LLM paradigm, we study plasticity loss in GPT-style Transformer models trained on a multilingual continual learning problem. Consistent with prior work, we find evidence of plasticity loss across models ranging from 5M to 314M non-embedding parameters, as measured by deterioration on a held-out Vietnamese probing task. We further find that the onset of plasticity loss follows a predictable scaling law, growing sublinearly with model size. These results suggest that larger models may delay the measurable effects of plasticity loss, but that increasing parameter count alone is likely to be insufficient to completely prevent it. We also find evidence of plasticity loss under stationary multilingual training, challenging the view that the phenomenon is exclusive to continual learning with abrupt task changes. Overall, our results suggest that even large Transformer language models trained on natural-language will eventually lose the ability to efficiently adapt to new data after sufficiently long training, in both continual and stationary settings.

AI.tldr: This paper shows that GPT-style Transformer language models (5M-314M params) lose plasticity—the ability to learn new information—during multilingual continual learning, with the onset following a predictable scaling law. Plasticity loss also occurs under stationary training, not just in continual learning scenarios.
AI.motivation: Loss of plasticity has been studied mostly in older, small architectures and rarely in natural-language domains. The authors want to determine whether it remains a problem in modern transformer-based LLMs.
AI.method: GPT-style Transformer models (5M-314M non-embedding parameters) are trained on a multilingual continual learning problem. Plasticity loss is measured by deterioration on a held-out Vietnamese probing task.
AI.result: Evidence of plasticity loss is found across all model sizes. The onset follows a predictable scaling law growing sublinearly with model size. Plasticity loss also occurs under stationary multilingual training.
AI.conclusion: Larger models may delay but not prevent plasticity loss. Increasing parameter count alone is insufficient. Even large Transformer LMs trained on natural language will eventually lose the ability to efficiently adapt to new data.

title: Open-Vocabulary BEV Segmentation with 3D-Aware Geometric Constraints
comment: This paper has been accepted by ECCV 2026
summary: Bird's-eye view (BEV) perception fuses multi-camera images into a unified top-down representation for autonomous driving. Despite recent progress, state-of-the-art methods remain confined to closed-set scenarios, making them vulnerable to unpredictable real-world environments. In this work, we introduce open-vocabulary BEV segmentation (OVBS), which leverages vision-language models (VLMs) to recognize categories beyond the training set while maintaining precise BEV perception and real-time efficiency. A key challenge in OVBS lies in the 3D geometric inconsistency inherent in the ill-posed lifting of 2D VLM semantics into BEV. To address this, we propose OVBEVSeg, a geometry-aware OVBS framework that enhances efficient Gaussian splatting (GS)-based unprojection by leveraging robust 3D geometric constraints across three progressive stages: (1) 2D-to-BEV pseudo-labeling via reliable 3D projection for OV generalization; (2) joint 2D-BEV per-scene optimization with BEV structural constraints for 3D geometric consistency; and (3) 3D geometric distillation for online efficiency. On the nuScenes dataset, OVBEVSeg achieves state-of-the-art performance, outperforming closed-set methods by 15.3 mIoU on unseen categories. Remarkably, even with no novel-class ground-truth labels, it remains competitive with self- and semi-supervised baselines trained with up to 40% of ground-truth annotations. Furthermore, it achieves 2.5x faster inference with only 0.22x the memory consumption of projection-based methods. Project page: https://hchoi256.github.io/projects/ovbevseg/.

AI.tldr: OVBEVSeg is a geometry-aware open-vocabulary BEV segmentation framework that uses Gaussian splatting-based unprojection with 3D geometric constraints to recognize novel categories in autonomous driving, achieving 15.3 mIoU improvement on unseen classes, 2.5x faster inference, and 0.22x memory vs. projection methods.
AI.motivation: Existing BEV perception methods are limited to closed-set scenarios and vulnerable to unpredictable real-world environments. The authors aim to achieve open-vocabulary BEV segmentation (OVBS) that can recognize categories beyond the training set while maintaining precise BEV perception and real-time efficiency.
AI.method: OVBEVSeg uses a geometry-aware framework with Gaussian splatting (GS)-based unprojection and three progressive stages: (1) 2D-to-BEV pseudo-labeling via reliable 3D projection for OV generalization; (2) joint 2D-BEV per-scene optimization with BEV structural constraints for 3D geometric consistency; and (3) 3D geometric distillation for online efficiency.
AI.result: On nuScenes, OVBEVSeg outperforms closed-set methods by 15.3 mIoU on unseen categories. It competes with self- and semi-supervised baselines trained with up to 40% ground-truth annotations despite having no novel-class labels. Achieves 2.5x faster inference with 0.22x memory consumption of projection-based methods.
AI.conclusion: OVBEVSeg demonstrates that geometry-aware 3D constraints effectively bridge the gap between 2D VLM semantics and BEV perception for open-vocabulary segmentation, achieving strong performance and efficiency. This work opens new possibilities for open-world autonomous driving perception.

title: BluTrain: A C++/CUDA Framework for AI Systems
summary: Progress in deep learning is, at scale, more a matter of systems engineering than of modelling: the behaviour of a model in training (its throughput, its memory footprint, and the numerical fidelity of the result) is determined less by the architecture itself than by how that architecture is expressed on the hardware. To achieve absolute control over this hardware expression while abstracting away systems complexity to make modelling seamless and eliminating the need for repetitive orchestration logic, BluTrain was architected from first principles as a robust, lightweight, and architecture-general training framework in standard C++ and the core CUDA programming model. Every layer is implemented natively: a typed tensor module with reverse-mode autograd, a linear-algebra library, a caching allocator, a multi-mode distributed-execution module, and an MLIR-based deep-learning compiler. In formal evaluations training a 124M-parameter GPT-2 baseline in FP32 on an 8-GPU 6000 Ada system, BluTrain outperforms industry-standard baselines in both throughput (sustaining an average of 407K tokens/s versus PyTorch's 395K tokens/s) and memory efficiency (achieving up to a 22% footprint reduction), while strictly preserving numerical fidelity and converging to a marginally lower final validation loss. With every layer explicitly open to native tuning, the performance ceiling is the framework's own to raise.

AI.tldr: BluTrain is a lightweight, architecture-general deep learning training framework built from scratch in C++ and CUDA that outperforms PyTorch in throughput (407K vs 395K tokens/s) and memory efficiency (22% reduction) on GPT-2 124M training, while preserving numerical fidelity.
AI.motivation: Deep learning progress at scale depends more on systems engineering than modeling, but existing frameworks lack full hardware-level control and introduce repetitive orchestration complexity. BluTrain was designed to eliminate this gap.
AI.method: BluTrain is implemented natively in standard C++ and core CUDA, featuring a typed tensor module with reverse-mode autograd, linear algebra library, caching allocator, multi-mode distributed execution, and an MLIR-based deep learning compiler—all from first principles.
AI.result: On 8-GPU 6000 Ada with GPT-2 124M (FP32), BluTrain achieves 407K tokens/s throughput (vs PyTorch's 395K), up to 22% memory reduction, and marginally lower final validation loss with exact numerical fidelity.
AI.conclusion: By exposing every layer to native tuning, BluTrain sets its own performance ceiling, demonstrating that a systems-first approach can surpass industry-standard frameworks in both efficiency and flexibility.

title: SignNet-1M: Large-Scale Multilingual Sign Language Video Dataset with Downstream Benchmarks
summary: Sign language models are typically trained on datasets captured under constrained conditions, with limited viewpoint, background, and signer-identity diversity, leading to poor robustness under real-world distribution shifts. We introduce SignNet-1M, a large-scale augmented dataset spanning ASL, CSL, and German Sign Language (DGS). SignNet-1M synthesizes realistic variations along three axes: (i) novel-view rendering (rotation and zoom) via 3D Gaussian Splatting (3DGS), (ii) scene/identity editing via diffusion models for background replacement and signer substitution while preserving sign motion and linguistic content, and (iii) post-rendering augmentations that emulate capture and compression artifacts (e.g., pose/temporal perturbations and video-level corruptions) to better match in-the-wild recordings.
