Beyond data release, we provide a unified benchmark suite across downstream tasks (e.g., translation and recognition) and ablations that isolate each augmentation component. Experiments across backbones show that training with SignNet-1M consistently improves generalization under cross-view, cross-background, cross-identity, and post-rendering shifts, while maintaining strong in-distribution performance. The dataset, full augmentation pipeline, and benchmark are available at https://signnet.chatsign.ai/.

AI.tldr: SignNet-1M is a large-scale augmented sign language dataset spanning ASL, CSL, and DGS, created via 3D Gaussian Splatting, diffusion models, and post-rendering augmentations to improve model robustness under real-world distribution shifts across viewpoint, background, and signer identity.
AI.motivation: Existing sign language models trained on datasets captured under constrained conditions (limited viewpoint, background, and signer diversity) lack robustness to real-world distribution shifts.
AI.method: SignNet-1M synthesizes variations via (i) novel-view rendering (rotation/zoom) using 3DGS, (ii) scene/identity editing via diffusion models, and (iii) post-rendering augmentations emulating capture/compression artifacts.
AI.result: Training with SignNet-1M consistently improves generalization under cross-view, cross-background, cross-identity, and post-rendering shifts while maintaining strong in-distribution performance.
AI.conclusion: SignNet-1M enhances sign language model robustness through systematic augmentation, providing a benchmark for translation and recognition tasks.

title: Assessing Distribution Shift in Human Activity Recognition for Domain Generalization
summary: While the field of Human Activity Recognition (HAR) continues to draw interest from researchers and advance in important ways, some key challenges remain. One of the most difficult aspects of building HAR models that show good performance in real-world settings is dealing with data diversity from device and sensor heterogeneity, and contextual changes that are intrinsic to real-world applications. While data diversity in HAR has been well-acknowledged in the literature, there remains a gap in understanding the effect of various types of distribution shifts on HAR models and the domain generalization problem that arises. Towards that end, this paper systematically evaluates 4 different types of distribution shifts, including variations in device type, sensor placement, sampling rate, and user behavior. Quantifying their effects, we illustrate that diversity shifts predominantly define all types of shifts, indicating the existence of unique features that are not shared across different domains. We then introduce a uniform HAR-based distribution shift benchmarks and conduct a comprehensive evaluation of up to 28 domain generalization methods. Our analysis exposes the limitations of current domain generalization algorithms in achieving model generalizability, marginally outperforming the empirical risk minimization baseline. This work represents the first systematic exploration of domain generalization and adaptation concerning specific distribution shifts in sensor-based HAR, offering an open-source benchmark platform and datasets to spur further research.

AI.tldr: This paper systematically evaluates four types of distribution shifts (device type, sensor placement, sampling rate, user behavior) in Human Activity Recognition (HAR), finding that diversity shifts dominate and current domain generalization methods barely outperform simple baselines. It introduces a uniform HAR benchmark with 28 domain generalization methods and open-source resources.
AI.motivation: While data diversity in HAR is well-acknowledged, there is a gap in understanding the effect of various distribution shifts on HAR models and the domain generalization problem that arises. The authors aim to fill this gap.
AI.method: The paper systematically evaluates 4 types of distribution shifts (device type, sensor placement, sampling rate, user behavior), quantifies their effects, introduces a uniform HAR-based distribution shift benchmark, and conducts a comprehensive evaluation of up to 28 domain generalization methods.
AI.result: Diversity shifts predominantly define all types of shifts, indicating unique features not shared across domains. Current domain generalization algorithms marginally outperform the empirical risk minimization baseline, exposing limitations in achieving model generalizability.
AI.conclusion: This is the first systematic exploration of domain generalization and adaptation concerning specific distribution shifts in sensor-based HAR, offering an open-source benchmark platform and datasets to spur further research.

title: Structural Kolmogorov-Arnold Convolutions: Learnable Function on the Values or the Filter Shape as Parameter-Efficient Alternative to Per-Edge Convolutional KANs
summary: Convolutional Kolmogorov--Arnold Networks (KANs) replace the fixed weights of a convolutional kernel with learnable univariate functions. The dominant formulation attaches one such function to every kernel entry and lets it act on pixel values, expressive but parameter-heavy and prone to overfitting. We argue that the learnable functions are better placed in the \emph{structure} of the convolution than on each edge, and we organise the design space along a single axis: whether the function acts on the pixel \emph{values} or on the filter \emph{shape}. We study three realisations. SV-KAN applies one shared univariate function to the values and leaves the spatial filter free and static, aa classical convolution with a single learnable shared activation. AG-KAN keeps the shared value function but supplies the spatial structure through a content-adaptive Gaussian gate. RF-KAN instead moves the learnable functions onto the filter shape, building each filter from oriented ridge profiles expanded in a localised oscillatory (Morlet) wavelet basis with content-adaptive amplitudes. Under a matched four-layer protocol with in-run references and three seeds, RF-KAN and SV-KAN reach $88.47\pm0.10\%$ and $88.20\pm0.31\%$ on CIFAR-10 and $64.40\pm0.19\%$ and $64.57\pm0.30\%$ on CIFAR-100, at about $0.4$M parameters. At this matched scale the shape model and the simplest value model meet at the top, both above a plain convolution and every per-edge KAN we tested, including the official Gram variant, at roughly a fifth of the parameters. A controlled study attributes the RF-KAN gain to an intrinsically localised oscillatory basis and to content adaptivity, and an ablation that removes the learned shape entirely, leaving only the shared value function, collapses accuracy by over forty points, identifying the learned shape as the load-bearing ingredient at this scale.

AI.tldr: This paper proposes that learnable functions in convolutional KANs should be placed in the convolution's structure rather than on each edge, introducing three variants: SV-KAN (shared value function), AG-KAN (content-adaptive Gaussian gate), and RF-KAN (learnable filter shape using Morlet wavelet basis). RF-KAN and SV-KAN achieve top results on CIFAR-10/100 with ~0.4M parameters, outperforming plain convolutions and per-edge KANs at ~1/5 the parameters, with learned shape being the critical component.
AI.motivation: Existing convolutional KANs attach learnable functions to every kernel entry, which is parameter-heavy and prone to overfitting. The authors argue that learnable functions are better placed in the convolution's structure rather than on each edge.
AI.method: The authors organize the design space along whether the function acts on pixel values or filter shape. They propose three realizations: SV-KAN (shared univariate function on values, static spatial filter), AG-KAN (shared value function with content-adaptive Gaussian gate for spatial structure), and RF-KAN (learnable functions on filter shape using oriented ridge profiles expanded in localized Morlet wavelet basis with content-adaptive amplitudes).
AI.result: Under a matched four-layer protocol, RF-KAN and SV-KAN achieve 88.47±0.10% and 88.20±0.31% on CIFAR-10, and 64.40±0.19% and 64.57±0.30% on CIFAR-100, with ~0.4M parameters. They outperform plain convolution and per-edge KAN variants at roughly 1/5 the parameters. Ablation removing learned shape collapses accuracy by over 40 points.
AI.conclusion: At the matched scale, the shape model (RF-KAN) and simplest value model (SV-KAN) perform comparably at the top. The learned shape is identified as the load-bearing ingredient, with gains attributed to the intrinsically localized oscillatory basis and content adaptivity.

title: Solving Inverse Problems of Chaotic Systems with Bidirectional Conditional Flow Matching
summary: Modeling chaotic systems is crucial yet challenging. Inverse problems in chaotic dynamics, namely inferring initial conditions from final states, remain largely unsolved because of ill-posedness, non-uniqueness, instability, and potentially chaotic time-reverse dynamics. We address this open problem with Bidirectional Conditional Flow Matching (Bi-CFM), which learns bidirectional mappings between distributions of initial and final states to capture the stochasticity of chaotic evolution and mitigate exponential error accumulation over time. Furthermore, for systems with conservation laws, we extend it to Conservation-constrained Bi-CFM (CBi-CFM). Across the classic Lorenz, Circuit, and high-dimensional Lorenz 96 systems, Bi-CFM improves five distribution-level metrics over baselines while achieving a speedup of more than two orders of magnitude. In the three-body planet-planet scattering problem in planetary dynamics, CBi-CFM better respects conservation laws, with conservation errors comparable to those of the ground truth. Finally, on real observations of globular clusters, collisional million-body systems shaped by $\sim 10^{10}$ years (10 Gyr) of evolution, our method represents an advance in accuracy, establishing a scalable route to solving inverse problems of long-timescale real-world chaotic dynamics.

AI.tldr: Bi-CFM (and its conservation-constrained variant CBi-CFM) solves the inverse problem of inferring initial states from final states in chaotic systems using bidirectional conditional flow matching, achieving superior accuracy, speed (>100x faster), and conservation law adherence across Lorenz, Circuit, Lorenz 96, three-body, and real globular cluster systems.
AI.motivation: Inverse problems in chaotic dynamics (inferring initial conditions from final states) are largely unsolved due to ill-posedness, non-uniqueness, instability, and chaotic time-reverse dynamics, despite the importance of modeling chaotic systems.
AI.method: Bidirectional Conditional Flow Matching (Bi-CFM) learns bidirectional mappings between distributions of initial and final states to capture chaotic evolution stochasticity and mitigate exponential error accumulation. For systems with conservation laws, Conservation-constrained Bi-CFM (CBi-CFM) is introduced.
AI.result: Bi-CFM outperforms baselines on five distribution-level metrics across Lorenz, Circuit, and Lorenz 96 systems, with >100x speedup. CBi-CFM achieves conservation errors comparable to ground truth in three-body planet-planet scattering. On real globular cluster observations (10 Gyr evolution), the method advances accuracy.
AI.conclusion: The method establishes a scalable route to solving inverse problems of long-timescale real-world chaotic dynamics, representing a significant advance for chaotic systems modeling.

title: MATCH: Flow Matching for Multi-View Anomaly Detection
summary: Detecting anomalies in industrial objects is an important topic for increasing production efficiency. More complex objects often require the analysis of several view points, which has led to the field of multi-view anomaly detection. We present MATCH, the first multi-view anomaly detection method based on Flow Matching (FM). With the ODE formulation of Flow Matching, we can estimate likelihoods and thereby derive an anomaly score to detect anomalies in multi-view image data at object, image, and pixel-level. The architectural flexibility of FM models allows us to efficiently transform features of different spatial sizes to the normal distribution. We evaluate thoroughly on the already established Real-IAD data set and are also the first to provide a comprehensive evaluation of popular anomaly detection methods for the MANTA-Tiny data set. MATCH achieves state-of-the-art performance in both anomaly detection and segmentation, all while running on consumer-level hardware. By omitting the costly divergence term needed for likelihood estimation, we ensure that MATCH is usable in real-time production scenarios. Lastly, several ablation studies are conducted to validate the methodological choices.

AI.tldr: MATCH is a new multi-view anomaly detection method using Flow Matching that achieves state-of-the-art performance on anomaly detection and segmentation across multiple datasets while running on consumer hardware in real-time.
AI.motivation: Complex industrial objects require analysis from multiple viewpoints for effective anomaly detection, motivating the need for a multi-view approach that is both accurate and computationally efficient for real-time production scenarios.
AI.method: MATCH uses Flow Matching with an ODE formulation to estimate likelihoods for anomaly scoring. It transforms features of varying spatial sizes to a normal distribution through FM's architectural flexibility, and omits the costly divergence term for real-time usability.
AI.result: Achieves state-of-the-art performance on Real-IAD dataset and provides the first comprehensive evaluation on MANTA-Tiny dataset. Runs on consumer-level hardware in real-time production scenarios.
AI.conclusion: Flow Matching is an effective and efficient foundation for multi-view anomaly detection, enabling real-time deployment on consumer hardware without sacrificing accuracy.

title: Difference-Making without Making a Difference
summary: Over a series of seven papers, Andreas & Günther have introduced seven definitions of actual causation and have classified them as belonging to three different, competing, types of accounts: factual difference-making, counterfactual difference-making, and regularity-based. I show that their most recent - factual difference-making - definition instantiates all three types, thereby proving that these are distinctions without a difference. I further compare their novel account to the other six accounts on several crucial examples, revealing that this undermines all seven of their accounts.

AI.tldr: This paper demonstrates that Andreas & Günther's seven definitions of actual causation, which they classified into three distinct types, actually collapse into each other, with their newest factual difference-making definition instantiating all three types. This undermines all seven accounts when tested on crucial examples.
AI.motivation: To show that the three types of causal accounts (factual difference-making, counterfactual difference-making, and regularity-based) that Andreas & Günther claim are competing and distinct are actually "distinctions without a difference."
AI.method: The author demonstrates that Andreas & Günther's most recent factual difference-making definition instantiates all three types of accounts, then compares this novel account to the other six accounts on several crucial examples to reveal inconsistencies.
AI.result: The factual difference-making definition contains elements of all three types, proving they are not genuinely distinct. When tested on crucial examples, this undermines all seven of Andreas & Günther's accounts.
AI.conclusion: The claimed distinctions between the three types of causal accounts are illusory, and the entire framework of seven definitions proposed by Andreas & Günther is fundamentally flawed.

title: Modality-Aware Out-of-Distribution Detection for Multi-Modal Action Recognition
summary: The incorporation of additional modalities into action recognition models increases their performance across a wide range of settings. However, how this additional information can contribute to making the models more robust remains underexplored, particularly for the case of multi-modal out-of-distribution (OOD) detection. While methods exist that regularize the multi-modal training process with OOD detection in mind, they still apply off-the-shelf OOD detectors designed for the uni-modal case during inference, discarding important information. Based on an interesting relationship we find between the multi-modal and uni-modal predictions, we propose to use this signal to build a post-hoc detector explicitly designed for the multi-modal scenario. We combine this new source of information with a feature-space score, which detects off-manifold samples in the multi-modal space, and normalize them by the multi-modal logits. In doing so, the proposed hybrid detector is compatible with existing training-time approaches and consistently improves performance. Experiments on a wide range of established datasets from the MultiOOD benchmark show that, on average, our approach outperforms the state of the art. Our results show the importance of explicitly considering the different modalities at inference time for multi-modal OOD detection.

AI.tldr: The paper proposes a post-hoc hybrid detector for multi-modal out-of-distribution detection that leverages relationships between multi-modal and uni-modal predictions, combined with feature-space scores normalized by multi-modal logits, consistently outperforming state-of-the-art methods on MultiOOD benchmark datasets.
AI.motivation: While multi-modal action recognition models benefit from additional modalities, their robustness for out-of-distribution detection remains underexplored. Existing methods apply off-the-shelf uni-modal OOD detectors during inference, discarding important multi-modal information.
AI.method: The authors discover a relationship between multi-modal and uni-modal predictions, then build a post-hoc detector that combines this signal with a feature-space score (detecting off-manifold samples in multi-modal space) normalized by multi-modal logits.
AI.result: Experiments on the MultiOOD benchmark show the proposed hybrid detector consistently outperforms state-of-the-art methods across a wide range of established datasets.
AI.conclusion: Explicitly considering different modalities at inference time is crucial for effective multi-modal OOD detection, and the proposed approach is compatible with existing training-time methods.

title: Accuracy and Satisfaction in Multi-Turn LLM Dialogues for NFR Assessment
comment: 9 pages, 5 figures. Accepted to SIGDIAL 2026 (27th Annual Meeting of the Special Interest Group on Discourse and Dialogue)
summary: LLM-based dialogue assistants have become mainstream tools for software developers, yet current evaluation benchmarks focus exclusively on functional correctness. This leaves a critical gap in assessing the quality and accuracy of these conversations when handling Non-Functional Requirements (NFRs), which are inherently vague, context-dependent, and involve many parts of a program. Evaluating how well these systems support collaborative reasoning about NFRs requires methods that go beyond single-turn accuracy to capture both the correctness of the system's outputs and the quality of the multi-turn interaction. In this paper, we investigate the accuracy and quality of multi-turn conversations between developers and an LLM-based agent in the domain of Health Insurance Portability and Accountability Act (HIPAA) regulatory compliance. We hired 49 programmers to interact with GitHub Copilot to assess 148 HIPAA-derived NFRs against the iTrust codebase, a system designed to comply with HIPAA regulations, across three dimensions: requirement satisfaction level, reasoning, and code localization. We find that developers tend to agree with LLM assessments, but accuracy against expert ground truth is low. We model user satisfaction and find that longer system responses and more information-providing turns negatively affect user satisfaction, whereas proactive interactions positively affect it. Our findings provide insights for designing LLM-based dialogue systems that support NFR assessment.

AI.tldr: This study evaluates multi-turn conversations between developers and GitHub Copilot for assessing HIPAA Non-Functional Requirements, finding that developers tend to agree with LLM assessments despite low accuracy against expert ground truth, and that longer responses reduce user satisfaction while proactive interactions improve it.
AI.motivation: Current evaluation benchmarks for LLM-based dialogue assistants focus solely on functional correctness, ignoring the critical gap in assessing quality and accuracy when handling Non-Functional Requirements (NFRs), which are vague, context-dependent, and involve multiple program parts.
AI.method: 49 programmers interacted with GitHub Copilot to assess 148 HIPAA-derived NFRs against the iTrust codebase across three dimensions: requirement satisfaction level, reasoning, and code localization.
AI.result: Developers tend to agree with LLM assessments, but accuracy against expert ground truth is low. Longer system responses and more information-providing turns negatively affect satisfaction, while proactive interactions positively affect it.
AI.conclusion: The findings provide insights for designing LLM-based dialogue systems that better support NFR assessment, highlighting the need to balance response length and proactive engagement.

title: EgoSAT: A Comprehensive Benchmark of Egocentric Streaming Interaction Understanding
comment: Accepted to ECCV 2026. Project page: https://leiyj23.github.io/EgoSAT/
summary: We introduce EgoSAT, the first comprehensive benchmark for egocentric video reasoning in streaming settings, designed to evaluate the capabilities of modern vision-language models (VLMs). The benchmark targets streaming interaction understanding, where video frames arrive sequentially and models must continuously interpret evolving visual context. EgoSAT unifies several previously distinct tasks within a single streaming framework. In this formulation, queries about completed events correspond to retrospective reasoning, queries about ongoing activities require online understanding, and queries about future actions involve prospective anticipation. This unified setting requires models to reason about the past, present, and future while operating under the constraint that only previously observed frames are available. EgoSAT contains 1,997 unique videos spanning 165 hours of egocentric footage and around 4,800 high-quality question-answer pairs, carefully designed to probe reasoning across varying temporal contexts. Using this benchmark, we evaluate a diverse set of both open-weight and closed-weight VLMs, providing a systematic assessment of their ability for streaming interaction understanding. By distinguishing answerability and conducting diagnostics on confidence of models, we find existing models not only struggle with prospective and retrospective modeling, but also exhibit severe mis-calibration: confidence often fails to track inherent answerability, leading to dangerous "confidently wrong" behaviors. Project page: https://leiyj23.github.io/EgoSAT/

AI.tldr: EgoSAT is a comprehensive benchmark for egocentric video reasoning in streaming settings, evaluating VLMs on retrospective, online, and prospective reasoning tasks across 1,997 videos and 4,800 QA pairs, revealing that existing models struggle with temporal reasoning and exhibit severe confidence miscalibration.
AI.motivation: Existing benchmarks fail to evaluate VLMs on streaming egocentric video understanding where frames arrive sequentially and models must reason about past, present, and future under the constraint of only previously observed frames, while also lacking assessment of model confidence calibration.
AI.method: EgoSAT unifies retrospective (past events), online (ongoing activities), and prospective (future actions) reasoning tasks into a single streaming framework with 1,997 unique egocentric videos (165 hours) and ~4,800 QA pairs. Evaluation distinguishes answerability and performs diagnostic analysis on model confidence.
AI.result: Existing open-weight and closed-weight VLMs struggle with both prospective and retrospective modeling, and exhibit severe miscalibration where confidence fails to track inherent answerability, leading to "confidently wrong" behaviors.
AI.conclusion: VLMs are insufficient for streaming egocentric video reasoning, particularly in temporal understanding and confidence calibration, highlighting the need for better models that can reliably track answerability over time.

title: Grading the Grader: Lessons from Evaluating an Agentic Data Analysis System
summary: Agentic data analysis systems produce rich outputs, including code, numerical results, and verbal diagnostics. This makes them more challenging to evaluate than single-turn LLM responses. It is therefore necessary to distinguish genuine disagreement between an agent's output and a ground-truth answer from grading artifacts. We investigate how reliably automated graders assess such a system and what strategies improve grading quality by applying LAMBDA, a multi-agent data-analysis system, on 153 numerical QRData tasks from DSGym. We develop and evaluate a three-layer human-AI grading cascade: strict regex matching, LLM-based lenient grading, and snippet-based human inspection, which combines non-GenAI and GenAI strategies with different failure profiles. Both automated graders achieve 100% observed precision (0/70 false positives). The lenient grader's recall is 97% against human labels. A keyword-anchored extraction pipeline raises the strict grader's recall by 60 percentage points over a last-number heuristic; the lenient grader is architecturally parser-independent. An iterative nudge mechanism raises grading run success from 36% to 97% and lenient-pass rates from 16% to 46%; comparing nudging with and without original-question re-injection shows that re-injection offers no benefit, confirming the nudge as an answer template cue. We further observe in this case study that variable type is the task metadata field most consistently associated with grading pipeline dynamics and observed outcome grades.

AI.tldr: This paper develops and evaluates a three-layer human-AI grading cascade for agentic data analysis systems, achieving 100% precision and 97% recall on 153 QRData tasks.
AI.motivation: Agentic data analysis systems produce complex, multi-modal outputs (code, numbers, text), making evaluation challenging. There's a need to distinguish genuine errors from grading artifacts.
AI.method: A three-layer grading cascade: strict regex matching, LLM-based lenient grading, and snippet-based human inspection. Uses LAMBDA system on 153 QRData tasks from DSGym. Includes keyword-anchored extraction and iterative nudge mechanism.
AI.result: Both automated graders achieve 100% observed precision. Lenient grader achieves 97% recall. Keyword-anchored extraction improves strict grader's recall by 60 percentage points. Nudge raises grading run success from 36% to 97%.
AI.conclusion: The three-layer cascade effectively evaluates agentic outputs; nudge mechanisms work as answer template cues (not requiring question re-injection); variable type is the most influential task metadata for grading dynamics.

title: Transformation Behavior of Images in Latent Space
summary:
Training of neural networks for histopathology classification tasks typically relies on data encoding into latent space, which reduces complexity and improves performance. There are several encoder networks available, either pretrained on general image datasets such as ImageNET, or specifically on histopathological images. Training of encoder networks should be adapted to downstream tasks, allowing encoding of biologic/diagnostic content while rendering networks invariant to label-irrelevant transformations.
  This paper investigates the effect of classical image transformation on the latent space, using networks provided by Lunit Inc. and Bioptimus, both focusing on pathological images, and by Meta Research Team. We assess variance of embeddings resulting from standard data transformations by comparing original and transformed image embeddings and by contrasting them with random, unrelated embeddings, using image tiles from hematoxylin/eosin-stained sections available in a colorectal tissue dataset and the publicly accessible TCGA dataset.
  Our findings show that embeddings of original and transformed images are closer to each other than to random embeddings, indicating robustness to transformations. However, they are not fully invariant, revealing that the encoder networks do not completely neutralize transformation effects in latent space, explaining why transformation-mediated augmentation of datasets can improve performance. Significant differences were observed between general and histopathology-specific encoder networks.

AI.tldr: This paper evaluates how image transformations affect latent space embeddings from different encoder networks (Lunit, Bioptimus, Meta) in histopathology. It finds transformed embeddings are closer to originals than random embeddings, indicating partial robustness but not full invariance, explaining why data augmentation improves performance. Significant differences exist between general and histopathology-specific encoders.
AI.motivation: To understand whether encoder networks (general vs. histopathology-specific) achieve invariance to label-irrelevant image transformations, and to explain why transformation-based data augmentation remains beneficial for downstream histopathology classification tasks.
AI.method: The authors used encoder networks from Lunit Inc., Bioptimus, and Meta Research Team. They applied standard image transformations to hematoxylin/eosin-stained tiles from a colorectal tissue dataset and TCGA. They compared embedding distances between original and transformed images, and contrasted them with random unrelated embeddings to quantify invariance.
AI.result: Original and transformed image embeddings are closer to each other than to random embeddings, showing robustness but not full invariance. Significant differences were observed between general-purpose (Meta) and histopathology-specific (Lunit, Bioptimus) encoder networks.
AI.conclusion: Encoder networks partially neutralize transformation effects but do not achieve full invariance, which explains why transformation-based augmentation continues to improve model performance. Histopathology-specific encoders behave differently from general ones, highlighting the need to consider encoder choice in downstream tasks.

title: Matching Tasks to Objectives: Fine-Tuning and Prompt-Tuning Strategies for Encoder-Decoder Pre-trained Language Models
summary: Prompt-based learning has emerged as a dominant paradigm in natural language processing. This study explores the impact of diverse pre-training objectives on the performance of encoder-decoder pre-trained language models across generation and question answering tasks, with a focus on commonsense knowledge retrieval and completion. We highlight the benefits of incorporating multiple objectives during both pre-training and fine-tuning stages. We introduce the Match Task to Objective (MTO) framework and methods for determining the appropriate objective for a given task. This framework offers automated methods to prepare task-related data for adaptation through unsupervised training, based on the identified objective. In the fine-tuning stage, we design novel templates that align with the objectives of the pre-training and adaptation stages. When aligned with task requirements, these strategies can achieve a performance gain of over 120\% compared to conventional methods in few-shot settings. They significantly outperform related works in few-shot settings and exceed the baseline even in full-dataset scenarios. Furthermore, we extend this approach to include prompt-tuning methodologies, providing guidance for more effective soft prompt engineering and optimization. Our strategies significantly enhance prompt-tuning performance as well. These insights hold substantial value, precisely guiding the selection and optimization of models customized for specific tasks. Code is available at https://github.com/puraminy/MTO/

AI.tldr: This paper introduces the MTO (Match Task to Objective) framework that aligns pre-training objectives with downstream tasks to significantly boost encoder-decoder PLM performance, achieving over 120% improvement in few-shot settings and outperforming baselines even in full-dataset scenarios.
AI.motivation: To explore how diverse pre-training objectives affect encoder-decoder PLMs across generation and QA tasks, and to provide systematic guidance for selecting and aligning objectives to maximize task performance.
AI.method: Proposes MTO framework with: (1) automated methods to identify the appropriate objective for a given task, (2) unsupervised task-specific data preparation, (3) novel templates aligned with pre-training/adaptation objectives, and (4) extension to prompt-tuning for soft prompt optimization.
AI.result: Over 120% performance gain in few-shot settings compared to conventional methods; significantly outperforms related works in few-shot and exceeds baselines in full-dataset scenarios; also enhances prompt-tuning performance.
AI.conclusion: Aligning pre-training objectives with downstream tasks through the MTO framework provides substantial improvements, offering precise guidance for model selection and optimization tailored to specific tasks.

title: MedPCFM: Improving Medical Point Cloud Completion by Integrating Point Transformers and Flow Matching
summary: Medical point cloud completion is important for anatomical reconstruction and downstream clinical workflows, yet generative modeling in this setting remains insufficiently studied. We investigate completion through continuous-time generative modeling and introduce PCFM, a PTv3-backed flow matching approach for medical point cloud completion. We evaluate on SkullFix and SkullBreak, and additionally on the more recent Mandibular Defect dataset. We build strong baselines by adapting PTv3 to a deterministic encoder-decoder completion model and by instantiating diffusion completion (PCDiff) with both PVCNN and PTv3 denoisers. PCFM with PTv3 is competitive with the deterministic PTv3 baseline and achieves state-of-the-art generative performance across datasets, while requiring substantially fewer sampling steps than diffusion. At the best operating points, PTv3 also yields clear throughput gains, providing up to a 7$\times$ speed-up for PCFM compared to a PVCNN backbone. Finally, we study empirical scaling trends by varying model size and point cardinality, showing consistent gains with higher point resolution and informative trade-offs across model scales.

AI.tldr: PCFM is a flow matching method for medical point cloud completion using a PTv3 backbone, achieving state-of-the-art generative performance with fewer sampling steps than diffusion models and up to 7× speed-up over PVCNN backbones.
AI.motivation: Generative modeling for medical point cloud completion is insufficiently studied, yet it is important for anatomical reconstruction and downstream clinical workflows.
AI.method: The authors propose PCFM, a continuous-time generative flow matching approach backed by PTv3 (Point Transformer v3), and compare it against deterministic PTv3 baselines and diffusion-based completion (PCDiff) denoised by both PVCNN and PTv3.
AI.result: PCFM with PTv3 is competitive with deterministic PTv3, achieves state-of-the-art generative performance across SkullFix, SkullBreak, and Mandibular Defect datasets, requires fewer sampling steps than diffusion, and delivers up to 7× throughput speed-up over PVCNN backbones.
AI.conclusion: Flow matching is an efficient and effective generative paradigm for medical point cloud completion, with consistent scaling benefits from higher point resolution and informative trade-offs across model sizes.

title: World Models in Pieces: Structural Certification for General Agents
summary: In the big-world regime, agents cannot be universally capable and their ability is inevitably specialized across a world model in pieces. Consequently, standard uniform guarantees fail to distinguish between the understanding of critical bottlenecks and irrelevant failures. We first formalize this limitation by proving that general agents are not universal, rendering standard worst-case analysis uninformative. To overcome this, we introduce structural certification, a transition-local framework that maps bounded goal-conditioned performance to entry-wise guarantees on the agent's internal world model. Our main contribution is constructive. We provide algorithms that filter specific transitions using deep compositional goals and prove that a general agent on these goals has a structural world model with a $\mathcal{O}(1/n) + \mathcal{O}(δ)$ error bound. Conversely, this bound is tight in the small-$δ$ regime, whose existence is explicitly guaranteed by our certification. These results enable the certifiable deployment of general agents by localizing the specific transitions where long-horizon planning is reliable.

AI.tldr: This paper proves that general agents cannot be universally capable, and introduces a structural certification framework that provides localized, transition-level guarantees on an agent's world model, achieving tight error bounds for reliable long-horizon planning.
AI.motivation: Standard worst-case analysis fails to distinguish between critical bottlenecks and irrelevant failures in the big-world regime where agents must specialize across a fragmented world model.
AI.method: The authors first formalize the limitation by proving general agents are not universal. They then introduce structural certification, a transition-local framework with algorithms that filter transitions using deep compositional goals, proving a 𝒪(1/n) + 𝒪(δ) error bound on the agent's structural world model.
AI.result: The proposed framework achieves a tight 𝒪(1/n) + 𝒪(δ) error bound in the small-δ regime, with existence explicitly guaranteed by the certification. This enables certifiable deployment by localizing reliable transitions for long-horizon planning.
AI.conclusion: Structural certification provides a principled way to deploy general agents with certified reliability by focusing on localized transition guarantees rather than uninformative uniform bounds.

title: S1-Omni-Image: A Unified Model for Scientific Image Understanding, Generation, and Editing
summary: We present S1-Omni-Image, an open-weight unified multimodal model for scientific image understanding, generation, and editing. Unlike general-purpose image generation models, scientific image tasks require not only high-fidelity synthesis, but also robust understanding of scientific semantics, structural relations, domain knowledge, and task intent. To this end, S1-Omni-Image builds on the scientific multimodal reasoning backbone S1-VL-32B and couples its understanding capability with an image generation module under a unified think-before-generate paradigm. Given a user instruction, the model first produces a task-oriented reasoning trace, a textual answer, and a task special token; their hidden states are then injected into the generation module to condition image generation or editing. S1-Omni-Image supports scientific image understanding, generation, and editing in a unified framework. For generation, it focuses on scientific illustrations and text rendering, including logical diagrams, relational comparisons, data charts, and realistic scientific visualizations. For editing, it casts segmentation and other domain-specific vision tasks as native image editing problems, enabling multi-turn illustration editing, medical and geographic image segmentation, medical image translation, and scientific image super-resolution. We construct SciGenEdit, a 314K-sample training dataset, and release the model weights, inference code, and SciGenEdit-10K. Experiments show that S1-Omni-Image substantially improves scientific image generation and editing while preserving the scientific image understanding capability inherited from S1-VL-32B. It outperforms open-source models on GenExam and TechImage-Bench, achieves state-of-the-art results on four editing benchmarks including MSD, cigRockSEM, SynthRAD2025, and IXI, and maintains stable performance on scientific image understanding evaluations.

AI.tldr: S1-Omni-Image is an open-weight multimodal model that unifies scientific image understanding, generation, and editing using a think-before-generate paradigm, achieving SOTA results on multiple benchmarks while preserving strong understanding capabilities.
AI.motivation: Scientific image tasks require high-fidelity synthesis plus robust understanding of scientific semantics, structural relations, domain knowledge, and task intent, which general-purpose image generation models fail to address.
AI.method: Builds on S1-VL-32B backbone; uses a think-before-generate paradigm where the model first produces a reasoning trace, textual answer, and task special token, whose hidden states condition the generation module.
AI.result: Outperforms open-source models on GenExam and TechImage-Bench; achieves SOTA on four editing benchmarks (MSD, cigRockSEM, SynthRAD2025, IXI); maintains stable scientific image understanding performance.
AI.conclusion: S1-Omni-Image substantially improves scientific image generation and editing while preserving understanding capabilities, validated by a newly constructed SciGenEdit-314K dataset.

title: OpenThoughts-Agent: Data Recipes for Agentic Models
summary: Agentic language models dramatically expand the applications of AI yet little is publicly known about how to curate training data for broadly capable agents. Existing open efforts such as SWE-Smith, SERA, and Nemotron-Terminal typically target a single benchmark, leaving open the question of how to train models that generalize across diverse agentic tasks. The OpenThoughts-Agent (OT-Agent) project addresses this gap with a fully open data curation pipeline for training agentic models. We conduct more than 100 controlled ablation experiments to systematically investigate each stage of the pipeline, yielding insights on the importance of task sources and diversity. We then assemble a training set of 100K examples from our pipeline and fine-tune Qwen3-32B on this dataset, which yields an average accuracy of 44.8% across seven agentic benchmarks and a 3.9 percentage point improvement over the strongest existing open data agentic model (Nemotron-Terminal-32B, 40.9%). Moreover, our training data exhibits strong scaling properties, outperforming alternative open datasets at every training set size in compute-controlled comparisons. We publicly release our training sets, data pipeline, experimental data, and models at openthoughts.ai to support future open research on agentic model training.

AI.tldr: OpenThoughts-Agent (OT-Agent) introduces a fully open data curation pipeline for training agentic language models, with 100+ ablation experiments showing the importance of task diversity. Their 100K-example training set improves Qwen3-32B to 44.8% average accuracy across 7 benchmarks (+3.9% over prior best), with strong scaling properties. All resources are publicly released.
AI.motivation: Existing open agentic training datasets (SWE-Smith, SERA, Nemotron-Terminal) target single benchmarks, lacking generalization across diverse agentic tasks. No publicly known systematic method exists for curating training data for broadly capable agentic models.
AI.method: (1) A fully open data curation pipeline with systematic investigation via 100+ controlled ablation experiments. (2) Assembly of a 100K-example training set. (3) Fine-tuning Qwen3-32B on this dataset. (4) Compute-controlled comparisons against alternative open datasets.
AI.result: OT-Agent achieves 44.8% average accuracy across seven agentic benchmarks, outperforming Nemotron-Terminal-32B (40.9%) by 3.9 percentage points. The training data shows strong scaling properties, outperforming alternative open datasets at every training set size in compute-controlled comparisons.
AI.conclusion: Open data curation pipelines with diverse task sources are critical for training generalizable agentic models. The OT-Agent pipeline, datasets, experimental data, and models are publicly released to support future open research.

title: P-MTP: Efficient Document Parsing via Multi-Token Prediction with Progressive Depth Scaling
summary: Vision-Language Models (VLMs) have revolutionized document parsing by enabling end-to-end mapping from images to structured text, imposing a significant latency bottleneck, particularly for token-dense documents. While Multi-Token Prediction (MTP) has emerged as a promising approach for accelerating inference, its potential is constrained by optimization instability when scaling to deeper look-ahead depth. In this paper, we propose \textbf{P-MTP}, a framework that leverages \textbf{Progressive Multi-Token Prediction} with a lightweight MTP module to scale the look-ahead depth for high-throughput document parsing. Specifically, we introduce Progressive Curriculum Loss that adaptively re-weights different look-ahead depths using cumulative path reliability and retrospective target consistency. By effectively suppressing gradient noise in long-range predictions, P-MTP, facilitates an automated easy-to-hard optimization transition, enabling the model to master increasingly distant look-ahead depths. Furthermore, we propose Confidence-Gated Dynamic Drafting to maximize the effective look-ahead depth and acceptance rate by adaptively calibrating speculative length during inference, thereby minimizing computational waste and further pushing the boundaries of inference speedup. Experimental results across multiple benchmarks and architectures demonstrate that P-MTP, achieves up to a $5\times$ speedup with negligible loss in accuracy, providing the first successful validation of extensive look-ahead MTP in the document parsing domain.

AI.tldr: P-MTP is a framework that uses Progressive Multi-Token Prediction with adaptive curriculum learning and confidence-gated dynamic drafting to achieve up to 5× inference speedup in VLM-based document parsing while maintaining accuracy.
AI.motivation: VLMs for document parsing suffer from latency bottlenecks due to token-dense documents. Existing MTP approaches face optimization instability when scaling to deeper look-ahead depths, limiting their acceleration potential.
AI.method: Two key components: (1) Progressive Curriculum Loss that adaptively re-weights look-ahead depths using cumulative path reliability and retrospective target consistency, enabling easy-to-hard optimization; (2) Confidence-Gated Dynamic Drafting that adaptively calibrates speculative length during inference to maximize acceptance rate and minimize waste.
AI.result: Achieves up to 5× speedup with negligible accuracy loss across multiple benchmarks and architectures, providing the first successful validation of extensive look-ahead MTP in document parsing.
AI.conclusion: P-MTP successfully scales look-ahead depth for multi-token prediction in document parsing VLMs, offering significant inference acceleration while preserving output quality.

title: EERLoss: A Novel Loss Function for Training Deep Biometric Models. A Case Study in Keystroke Dynamics
summary: Deep learning approaches to biometric verification are commonly trained by optimizing indirect objectives, creating a misalignment between the optimization process and the primary evaluation metric, typically the Equal Error Rate (EER). This paper introduces EERLoss: a subdifferentiable, arbitrarily accurate approximation to EER for training deep biometric models. Furthermore, this framework has the potential to be adapted to optimize any specific operating point on the DET curve, enhancing its generalizability. To validate this approach, EERLoss is evaluated on a particularly demanding behavioral biometric modality: keystroke dynamics verification. This task is characterized by its high intra-class and low inter-class variability. Experiments are conducted on the large-scale KVC-onGoing benchmark, incorporating data from over 185,000 subjects across different scenarios. A comprehensive ablation study initially demonstrates the superiority of EERLoss in comparison to existing state-of-the-art loss functions. It also converges substantially faster compared to other losses, reducing the overall training cost. Additionally, a comparison is made between the proposed loss and the KVC-winning architecture by re-training it with EERLoss, demonstrating that the proposed approach significantly outperforms the original SoTA, achieving a relative EER reduction of up to approx. 30\%. This improvement on a challenging, large-scale benchmark validates the effectiveness of EERLoss as a task-aligned training objective specifically suited for high-variance biometric traits.

AI.tldr: This paper proposes EERLoss, a differentiable approximation of Equal Error Rate (EER) for direct optimization in deep biometric verification, achieving ~30% relative EER reduction on keystroke dynamics verification using the large-scale KVC-onGoing benchmark.
AI.motivation: Existing deep learning biometric verification methods optimize indirect objectives that misalign with the primary evaluation metric (EER), creating a gap between training optimization and actual performance assessment.
AI.method: EERLoss is a subdifferentiable, arbitrarily accurate approximation to EER that can also be adapted to optimize specific operating points on the DET curve, enabling task-aligned training for biometric models.
AI.result: EERLoss outperforms existing state-of-the-art loss functions, converges substantially faster, and achieves ~30% relative EER reduction when applied to the KVC-winning architecture on the large-scale KVC-onGoing benchmark (185K+ subjects).
AI.conclusion: EERLoss is an effective task-aligned training objective particularly suited for high-variance biometric traits, validated on challenging keystroke dynamics verification.

title: SENTRY: SAM2-Enhanced Neighbor-Aware and Temporally Reasoned Memory for Visual Tracking
comment: Accepted for publication at the European Conference on Computer Vision (ECCV 2026)
summary: We revisit the memory update mechanism in SAM2-based visual object tracking and identify confidence-only mask selection as the dominant cause of drift under occlusion, rapid motion, and distractors. We introduce SENTRY, a training-free, plug-and-play, refine-before-write module that validates each memory update for short-horizon temporal consistency before committing it. SENTRY aggregates diverse segmentation hypotheses per frame, backtracks them into short tracklets, and uses neighbor-aware cycle-consistent matching against recent trajectories to favor temporally and geometrically consistent masks. It leaves the base architecture untouched, replacing confidence-driven writes with consistency-validated ones. For fair evaluation, we re-evaluate major open-source SAM2-based trackers across all available scales and datasets, filling gaps in prior reports. Integrated into five strong baselines, SENTRY delivers consistent gains across nine benchmarks, achieving new zero-shot SOTA on LaSOT, LaSOT_ext, GOT-10k, VOT20, VOT22, and DiDi. Despite these checks, the SAM2-L version runs at 32.8 FPS on an A100, and across compatible hosts adds only about 0.4--0.6 GB VRAM. Our results provide the first unified all-scale evaluation of SAM2-based trackers and show that enforcing temporal validity at write time stabilizes memory-augmented tracking without retraining.

AI.tldr: SENTRY is a training-free, plug-and-play module that improves SAM2-based visual object tracking by replacing confidence-only mask selection with consistency-validated memory updates, achieving new SOTA on multiple benchmarks at 32.8 FPS.
AI.motivation: The authors identify that confidence-only mask selection in SAM2-based trackers causes drift under occlusion, rapid motion, and distractors. They aim to fix this without retraining or modifying the base architecture.
AI.method: SENTRY is a refine-before-write module that: (1) aggregates diverse segmentation hypotheses per frame, (2) backtracks them into short tracklets, and (3) uses neighbor-aware cycle-consistent matching against recent trajectories to select temporally and geometrically consistent masks before memory updates.
AI.result: SENTRY achieves new zero-shot SOTA on LaSOT, LaSOT_ext, GOT-10k, VOT20, VOT22, and DiDi, runs at 32.8 FPS on A100, and adds only 0.4-0.6 GB VRAM. It delivers consistent gains across five strong baselines and nine benchmarks.
AI.conclusion: Enforcing temporal validity at write time stabilizes memory-augmented tracking without retraining. The paper also provides the first unified all-scale evaluation of SAM2-based trackers.

title: Lite Any Stereo V2: Faster and Stronger Efficient Zero-Shot Stereo Matching
summary: Recent advances in stereo matching have achieved remarkable accuracy, but often rely on large models, heavy computation, or additional foundation-model priors, making them difficult to deploy on resource-constrained platforms. In contrast, efficient stereo models offer faster inference but are commonly considered less capable of strong zero-shot generalization. In this paper, we challenge this assumption by introducing Lite Any Stereo V2 (LAS2), an ultra-fast model series designed for efficient zero-shot stereo matching. LAS2 is developed from both architecture and training perspectives. Architecturally, we revisit efficient stereo design under practical deployment settings and propose a 2D-only cost aggregation framework, optimized for real inference latency rather than theoretical MACs alone. For training, we develop a three-stage strategy that combines synthetic supervision, self-distillation, and real-world knowledge distillation. To improve the reliability of real-world pseudo supervision, we further introduce pseudo-label filtering and an error-clamping operation, enabling smoother synthetic-to-real transfer. We instantiate LAS2 as a family of models, including feed-forward variants for different efficiency budgets and an iterative variant for higher accuracy. Extensive experiments show that LAS2 achieves state-of-the-art accuracy among efficient stereo methods while maintaining significantly lower latency. Specifically, LAS2-H achieves stronger overall zero-shot performance than the iterative method Fast-FoundationStereo, with 1.8x and 2.7x faster inference on H200 and Orin, respectively. The project page, demos, and code are available at https://tomtomtommi.github.io/LiteAnyStereoV2/.

AI.tldr: LAS2 is an ultra-fast, efficient stereo matching model series that achieves strong zero-shot generalization through a 2D-only cost aggregation framework and a three-stage training strategy (synthetic supervision, self-distillation, real-world knowledge distillation), outperforming prior efficient methods in both accuracy and latency.
AI.motivation: Existing stereo matching models either achieve high accuracy with heavy computation or offer fast inference but poor zero-shot generalization. The authors aim to bridge this gap by designing an efficient model that can generalize well to unseen domains without sacrificing speed.
AI.method: 1) A 2D-only cost aggregation framework optimized for real inference latency. 2) A three-stage training strategy: synthetic supervision → self-distillation → real-world knowledge distillation. 3) Pseudo-label filtering and error-clamping to improve synthetic-to-real transfer.
AI.result: LAS2 achieves state-of-the-art accuracy among efficient stereo methods with significantly lower latency. LAS2-H outperforms Fast-FoundationStereo in zero-shot performance while being 1.8x faster on H200 and 2.7x faster on Orin.
AI.conclusion: Efficient stereo models can achieve strong zero-shot generalization without relying on large models or foundation-model priors, challenging the common assumption that efficiency comes at the cost of generalization ability.

title: Boosting Text-Driven Video Segmentation via Geometry-Aware Distillation
summary: Text-driven Referring Video Object Segmentation (RVOS) aims to locate and segment target objects in videos given natural language. However, existing models are typically trained on 2D image or video datasets with naive segmentation losses, which overlooks the geometric consistency across frames and leads to weak spatial understanding. In this paper, we propose Geometry-enhanced Language-guided Video segmentation (GeoLaV), a two-stage framework that distills 3D geometric knowledge from images to enhance text-driven video segmentation. In the first stage, we perform monocular geometry pretraining with monocular novel-view synthesis, enabling the model to acquire geometry-consistent visual representations via spatial alignment on large-scale single-image datasets. In the second stage, we introduce geometry-aware distillation and fine-tune the model on video segmentation datasets, transferring 3D structural knowledge from a general 3D prior model. This process reinforces 3D awareness and improves both spatiotemporal coherence and language grounding in segmentation. Extensive experiments show that our method using only image segmentation data already provides notable zero-shot generalization in RVOS. When combined with geometry-aware distillation for fine-tuning on videos, our method achieves state-of-the-art performance across multiple RVOS benchmarks. The code is available at https://github.com/Tony1882880/GeoLaV.

AI.tldr: GeoLaV is a two-stage framework that enhances Referring Video Object Segmentation by distilling 3D geometric knowledge from images, achieving state-of-the-art performance through monocular geometry pretraining and geometry-aware distillation.
AI.motivation: Existing RVOS models are trained on 2D data with naive segmentation losses, overlooking geometric consistency across frames and leading to weak spatial understanding.
AI.method: A two-stage framework: (1) monocular geometry pretraining with novel-view synthesis for geometry-consistent visual representations, (2) geometry-aware distillation fine-tuning using a 3D prior model to transfer structural knowledge.
AI.result: Using only image segmentation data provides notable zero-shot generalization in RVOS; with video fine-tuning, achieves state-of-the-art across multiple RVOS benchmarks.
AI.conclusion: Incorporating 3D geometric knowledge into language-guided video segmentation significantly improves spatiotemporal coherence and language grounding, outperforming prior methods.

title: video-SALMONN-R$^3$: Learning to ReWatch, ReAsk, and ReAnswer for Efficient Video Understanding
summary: Video large language models (LLMs) are often constrained by computation and memory budgets, leading them to use reduced frame rates and spatial resolutions, which may cause them to miss critical information for question answering (QA). A practical and efficient solution is a two-stage paradigm: first perform coarse video understanding to localize relevant segments, and then re-watch these segments at higher temporal or spatial fidelity. In this paper, we present video-SALMONN-R$^3$, the first end-to-end video-LLM that enables re-watch through reinforcement learning without relying on chain-of-thought (CoT) cold-start. This design removes the need for costly CoT data annotations and avoids CoT-based supervised fine-tuning (SFT), which can otherwise degrade the pretrained video understanding abilities. To address the mismatch between the reasoning-first behavior induced by re-watch and the answer-first tendency of pretrained video-LLMs, we propose a re-answer strategy, in which the model first produces a direct answer in the first watch and then refines it after re-watching. Finally, to improve question adherence during re-watching, we propose a re-ask mechanism that re-injects the query when revisiting localized segments. Experimental results show that video-SALMONN-R$^3$ consistently outperforms both the base model and the QA-SFT baseline, while surpassing prior re-watch-based approaches with significantly lower computational cost. Code, models, and data will be publicly released upon acceptance.

AI.tldr: video-SALMONN-R³ is an end-to-end video-LLM that uses reinforcement learning to enable re-watching of video segments at higher fidelity, overcoming memory/computation limits while avoiding costly chain-of-thought data annotations.
AI.motivation: Video LLMs are constrained by computation and memory budgets, forcing reduced frame rates and spatial resolutions that cause them to miss critical information for question answering.
AI.method: Proposes a re-watch paradigm via reinforcement learning (no CoT cold-start), a re-answer strategy (direct answer first, refine after re-watch), and a re-ask mechanism (re-inject query when revisiting segments) to improve question adherence.
AI.result: Consistently outperforms both the base model and QA-SFT baseline, surpasses prior re-watch-based approaches with significantly lower computational cost.
AI.conclusion: video-SALMONN-R³ offers an efficient and effective solution for video QA by enabling selective re-watching through RL, without costly CoT annotations or degraded pretrained abilities.

title: MambaRaw: Selective State Space Modeling for Efficient 4K Raw Image Reconstruction
summary: In-camera JPEG previews are ubiquitous in raw image formats and provide an sRGB reference at negligible storage cost. Although existing metadata-based reconstruction frameworks can exploit this side information when recovering raw images, their context models often become computationally expensive especially at high resolution, eg, 4K raw image, given that attention mechanisms scale quadratically with feature maps, hindering its practical application. To address these limitations, we propose MambaRaw, a JPEG-conditioned metadata-based raw image reconstruction framework that uses State Space Models (SSMs) to estimate entropy parameters efficiently. Our key contribution comprises a Spatial-Energy Coupled Context Modeling mechanism with two lightweight modules: (1) TileMambaBlock, which performs Mamba-style selective scanning only on information-dense tiles to improve the efficiency; and (2) Energy-Aware Refinement (EAR), an identity-initialized residual module that enhance feature representation to match the long-tail energy distribution of raw signals. Extensive experiments on three camera datasets (Sony, Olympus, Samsung) show consistent improvements over strong metadata-based baselines and set a new state of the art for JPEG-guided raw reconstruction with great efficiency. Notably, at low metadata bitrates, MambaRaw increases PSNR by 1.2--1.4 dB and reduces end-to-end coding latency by about 9%. Code is released at https://github.com/Peizeli1/MambaRaw.

AI.tldr: MambaRaw is a JPEG-conditioned raw image reconstruction framework using State Space Models (SSMs) to efficiently estimate entropy parameters, with a Spatial-Energy Coupled Context Modeling mechanism (TileMambaBlock + Energy-Aware Refinement) that achieves SOTA results on Sony, Olympus, and Samsung datasets while reducing latency by ~9%.
AI.motivation: Existing metadata-based raw reconstruction frameworks using attention mechanisms scale quadratically with feature maps, becoming computationally expensive for high-resolution (e.g., 4K) raw images, hindering practical deployment.
AI.method: MambaRaw uses State Space Models (SSMs) for efficient entropy parameter estimation, with two key modules: (1) TileMambaBlock - performs Mamba-style selective scanning only on information-dense tiles; (2) Energy-Aware Refinement (EAR) - an identity-initialized residual module that enhances feature representation to match the long-tail energy distribution of raw signals.
AI.result: Outperforms strong metadata-based baselines on Sony, Olympus, Samsung datasets. At low metadata bitrates, achieves 1.2-1.4 dB PSNR improvement and ~9% reduction in end-to-end coding latency. Sets new SOTA for JPEG-guided raw reconstruction.
AI.conclusion: MambaRaw demonstrates that SSM-based context modeling can replace quadratic-complexity attention mechanisms for efficient, high-quality raw image reconstruction from JPEG previews, offering practical benefits for real-world imaging pipelines.

title: Advancing WordArt-Oriented Scene Text Recognition: Datasets and Methods
summary: WordArt (artistic text) features highly customized fonts, textures, and layouts, making WordArt-oriented scene TExt Recognition (WATER) substantially more challenging than general Scene Text Recognition (STR). Existing STR datasets and methods, typically built around regular scene text and fixed-template inputs, struggle to scale to WATER. Thus, we aim to advance this task from both data and model perspectives. On the data side, we construct a 2M synthetic dataset, WATER-S, with the scale improved by hundreds of times compared to existing artistic text data. WATER-S consists of two complementary subsets. One rendered by an upgraded rendering pipeline (SynthWordArt), which provides highly accurate and controllable synthetic WordArt data. The other is generated by combining Qwen3-VL for prompt mining and Z-Image for image synthesis, which improves the coverage of realistic and diverse data. On the model side, we propose WATERec. It adopts an visual encoder supporting arbitrary-shaped inputs and an autoregressive decoder to model complex layouts, structurally breaking the bottleneck of fixed-template STR on WordArt. Experiments show that this architecture outperforms prior STR methods, achieving state-of-the-art performance on irregular texts such as WordArt. Together with WATER-R, carefully reorganized from existing real STR data, our strong baseline with the new synthetic data and model design reaches 90.40% accuracy on WordArt-Bench, surpassing both general-purpose and OCR-specialized vision-language models by a large margin. Code and data are available at https://github.com/YesianRohn/WATER.

AI.tldr: This paper tackles WordArt (artistic text) recognition by constructing a 2M synthetic dataset WATER-S and proposing WATERec, a model with arbitrary-shaped visual encoder and autoregressive decoder, achieving 90.40% accuracy on WordArt-Bench, significantly outperforming existing STR and VLM methods.
AI.motivation: Existing STR datasets and methods are built around regular scene text and struggle with highly customized artistic text (WordArt). There is a lack of large-scale, diverse, and controllable WordArt data, and current fixed-template models cannot handle the complex layouts of WordArt.
AI.method: (1) Data: Constructed WATER-S (2M samples) with two subsets — SynthWordArt (upgraded rendering pipeline for accurate synthetic data) and Qwen3-VL + Z-Image pipeline (for realistic/diverse image synthesis). Also reorganized real STR data into WATER-R. (2) Model: WATERec uses an arbitrary-shaped visual encoder + autoregressive decoder to break fixed-template bottlenecks.
AI.result: WATERec achieves 90.40% accuracy on WordArt-Bench, surpassing both prior STR methods and OCR-specialized/general-purpose vision-language models by a large margin.
AI.conclusion: The combination of large-scale high-quality synthetic data (WATER-S) and a flexible architecture (WATERec) effectively advances the WordArt-oriented scene text recognition task, setting a new strong baseline.

title: RetiSEM: Generalising Causal Models for Fragmented Biomedical Data
summary: Learning causal models from fragmented biomedical data is challenging because clinical, molecular, and imaging variables are often incomplete or not jointly observed. We propose RetiSEM, a domain-constrained structural equation modelling (SEM) framework for causal graph recovery and mediation analysis under limited multimodal resources. This proposed work organises variables into biologically informed blocks, applies forbidden-edge constraints, and decomposes pathway-level effects into TE, NDE, and NIE components. We evaluate RetiSEM across ten synthetic benchmark scenarios that vary in dimensionality, nonlinearity, causal depth, and pathway structure, together with a fragmented real-world setting that combines NHANES clinical variables with externally derived retinal representations. This approach achieves lower structural error and higher causal accuracy than unconstrained baselines across the synthetic benchmarks. In the real-data analysis, retinal variables behave mainly as downstream biomarker-like indicators, with smaller but detectable indirect effects. These findings support our strategy as an interpretable framework for testing structured causal hypotheses in limited-resource biomedical AI. The code and resources for this work are publicly available at: https://github.com/Inamullah-Colab/ReitSEM.

AI.tldr: RetiSEM is a domain-constrained structural equation modeling framework for causal graph recovery and mediation analysis from fragmented multimodal biomedical data, outperforming unconstrained baselines on synthetic and real-world benchmarks.
AI.motivation: Learning causal models from fragmented biomedical data is challenging because clinical, molecular, and imaging variables are often incomplete or not jointly observed, limiting the ability to perform reliable causal inference under limited multimodal resources.
AI.method: RetiSEM organizes variables into biologically informed blocks, applies forbidden-edge constraints to enforce domain knowledge, and decomposes pathway-level effects into total effect (TE), natural direct effect (NDE), and natural indirect effect (NIE) for mediation analysis.
AI.result: Achieves lower structural error and higher causal accuracy than unconstrained baselines across ten synthetic benchmarks. In real-data analysis combining NHANES clinical variables with external retinal representations, retinal variables behave mainly as downstream biomarkers with small but detectable indirect effects.
AI.conclusion: The domain-constrained SEM strategy provides an interpretable framework for testing structured causal hypotheses in limited-resource biomedical AI settings.

title: VistaRef: Boosting Visual Spatial Orientation Awareness for Pointing-to-Object Detection
summary: Grounding deictic gestures in natural images is fundamental to AR and human-robot collaboration, providing a basis for seamless spatial interaction. While Transformer-based visual models have achieved significant progress in general object detection, their global attention mechanisms often neglect micro-geometric relationships, degrading orientation accuracy. In pointing tasks, this deficiency manifests as an inability to accurately capture the pointing ray implied by finger poses, which results in pointing drift and localization ambiguity when dealing with distant or densely packed objects. To address this, we propose VistaRef, a framework designed to explicitly enhance spatial orientation awareness. First, we develop the Local Hand Entity Modeling (LHEM) module, which incorporates hand-pose embeddings to strengthen the model's capability to capture subtle finger deviations. Second, drawing inspiration from multi-view geometry, we construct the Geometric Ray Modeling (GRM) module to transform implicit orientation information into explicit spatial geometric features, guiding feature aggregation and deep fusion via attention mechanisms. Furthermore, we introduce a novel Orientation-Consistent Alignment Loss (OCAL) to synergistically supervise hand presence and pointing consistency, ensuring that all architectural improvements collectively serve the core objective of spatial localization. Experimental results demonstrate that VistaRef significantly outperforms the baseline, achieving a 14-point absolute gain in grounding accuracy. Qualitative analysis further confirms that VistaRef effectively models the geometric correlation from hand to target, bridging the spatial perception gap inherent in traditional Transformers for complex scenarios. Code: https://github.com/lingli1724/VistaRef.

AI.tldr: VistaRef enhances Transformer-based visual models for deictic gesture grounding by introducing Local Hand Entity Modeling, Geometric Ray Modeling, and Orientation-Consistent Alignment Loss, achieving a 14-point absolute gain in grounding accuracy.
AI.motivation: Transformer-based visual models with global attention mechanisms neglect micro-geometric relationships, degrading orientation accuracy in pointing tasks - causing pointing drift and localization ambiguity for distant or densely packed objects.
AI.method: 1) Local Hand Entity Modeling (LHEM) with hand-pose embeddings for capturing subtle finger deviations; 2) Geometric Ray Modeling (GRM) transforming implicit orientation into explicit spatial features via multi-view geometry; 3) Orientation-Consistent Alignment Loss (OCAL) for joint supervision of hand presence and pointing consistency.
AI.result: 14-point absolute gain in grounding accuracy over baseline; qualitative results confirm effective modeling of hand-to-target geometric correlations.
AI.conclusion: VistaRef bridges the spatial perception gap in traditional Transformers for complex grounding scenarios, advancing AR and human-robot collaboration.

title: GeoIMO: Geometry-Driven Independent Motion Classification for Event Cameras
summary: Existing automotive event datasets rely on appearance-based annotations from frame pipelines, making them poorly suited for motion-aware event perception. We present a geometry-driven, annotation-free framework that classifies detected objects as static or independently moving by exploiting ego-motion structure directly from the event stream. A Focus of Expansion model with yaw compensation estimates global background motion, while objects are labeled as moving when local motion deviates from this prediction, as quantified by a scale-invariant residual. Temporal stabilization improves robustness across consecutive event windows. The method requires no learning, no manual motion labels, and works with any input bounding boxes. Experiments on MVSEC and the Prophesee 1 Megapixel Automotive Detection dataset demonstrate consistent performance across diverse driving scenarios, with yaw compensation improving results during turns and a simple translational local model offering a favorable accuracy-efficiency trade-off.

AI.tldr: A geometry-driven, annotation-free framework that classifies objects as static or moving by analyzing ego-motion directly from event streams, using Focus of Expansion with yaw compensation and residual-based motion deviation detection.
AI.motivation: Existing event datasets use appearance-based annotations from frame pipelines, which are poorly suited for motion-aware event perception, creating a need for a method that leverages the temporal nature of event data.
AI.method: A Focus of Expansion model with yaw compensation estimates global background motion; objects are labeled as moving when local motion deviates from this prediction via a scale-invariant residual. Temporal stabilization is applied across consecutive event windows.
AI.result: Consistent performance on MVSEC and Prophesee 1 Megapixel Automotive Detection datasets across diverse driving scenarios; yaw compensation improves results during turns.
AI.conclusion: The method is learning-free, requires no manual motion labels, works with any input bounding boxes, and offers a favorable accuracy-efficiency trade-off with a simple translational local model.

title: What Do Flow-Based Inverse Solvers Approximate? A Posterior-Transport View
summary: A growing family of training-free solvers -- FlowDPS, FLOWER, PnP-Flow and their diffusion ancestors (DPS, DAPS) -- repurpose a pretrained flow-matching prior to solve imaging inverse problems by adding a measurement-guidance term to the deterministic probability-flow ODE. Despite strong empirical results, what these per-step corrections actually approximate -- and how far the resulting samples are from the true posterior $p(x\mid y)$ -- has not been characterized. We give a posterior-transport account of flow-based inverse problem solving. Our starting point is a simple but consequential fact: for a \emph{deterministic} flow prior, Bayesian conditioning is realized entirely by a \emph{reweighting of the source distribution}, not by a drift correction; pushing the reweighted source through the \emph{unmodified} velocity field yields exact posterior samples. From this we show that trajectory-guidance solvers can be read as the minimum-kinetic-energy \emph{correction} field needed to morph the unconditional source into the posterior, and that FlowDPS / FLOWER / PnP-Flow correspond to distinct zeroth-order / Gaussian / proximal approximations of this single object; we bound the resulting posterior bias in Wasserstein distance. A controlled $2$D study with a closed-form posterior confirms the theory decisively: source reweighting matches the true posterior to the Monte-Carlo floor on every metric, whereas trajectory guidance incurs $200$--$800\times$ larger error and collapses posterior modes, \emph{regardless of guidance strength}. Guided by the analysis we propose a cheap, principled velocity-correction solver that is competitive across two in-domain priors (AFHQ, CelebA) and two out-of-distribution settings while, unlike point-estimate source-space optimizers, producing diverse posterior samples with uncertainty that correlates with reconstruction error.

AI.tldr: This paper provides a theoretical framework showing that flow-based inverse problem solvers using trajectory guidance approximate posterior sampling poorly, while a simple source reweighting method yields exact posterior samples; they propose a principled velocity-correction solver that outperforms existing methods.
AI.motivation: Existing training-free solvers repurpose pretrained flow-matching priors for inverse problems by adding measurement-guidance terms, but what these corrections actually approximate and how far they deviate from the true posterior has not been characterized.
AI.method: The authors derive that for deterministic flow priors, Bayesian conditioning equals source distribution reweighting (not drift correction); show existing solvers correspond to approximations of the true correction field; propose a principled velocity-correction solver.
AI.result: In controlled 2D experiments, source reweighting matches true posterior exactly while trajectory guidance incurs 200-800x larger error and collapses posterior modes; proposed solver is competitive across in-domain and out-of-distribution settings with diverse posterior samples.
AI.conclusion: Source reweighting is theoretically and empirically superior to trajectory-guidance solvers; the proposed velocity-correction solver offers a principled alternative that produces uncertainty-aware posterior samples.

title: VisCritic: Visual State Comparison as Process Reward for GUI Agents
comment: 17 pages, 4 figures; ECCV 2026 submission; supplementary material uploaded as ancillary file
