AI.tldr: FLUX3D proposes a scalable image-to-3D Gaussian Splatting framework that addresses two key bottlenecks (representation and cross-modal alignment) via Diffusion-Aligned Structured Latents (DA-SLAT), a decoder-only architecture, and a Sparse-structure Multimodal Diffusion Transformer (SMDiT) with Modal-Aware Rotary Positional Embedding (MARoPE), achieving SOTA results in high-fidelity 3DGS generation.
AI.motivation: Existing sparse voxel representation methods for image-to-3DGS suffer from two structural bottlenecks: (1) using discriminative 2D features designed for semantic abstraction suppresses reconstructive cues (representation bottleneck), and (2) standard diffusion transformers fail to align dense 2D image tokens with sparse 3D voxel latents (cross-modal correspondence bottleneck).
AI.method: FLUX3D introduces: (1) Diffusion-Aligned Structured Latents (DA-SLAT) coupled with a decoder-only architecture to improve 3DGS reconstruction fidelity; (2) Sparse-structure Multimodal Diffusion Transformer (SMDiT) and Modal-Aware Rotary Positional Embedding (MARoPE) for geometry-agnostic 2D-3D cross-modal alignment.
AI.result: FLUX3D yields substantial improvements in appearance fidelity and significantly outperforms all state-of-the-art (SOTA) methods in generating high-quality 3DGS assets on extensive benchmarks.
AI.conclusion: By addressing both the representation bottleneck and the cross-modal correspondence bottleneck, FLUX3D provides a scalable and effective framework for high-fidelity image-to-3DGS generation.

title: FLAT: Feedforward Latent Triangle Splatting for Geometrically Accurate Scene Generation
summary: Generating explorable 3D scenes from a single image requires strong generative priors and accurate geometric representations suitable for downstream use. Current video diffusion models offer high-quality generation and implicitly encode multi-view geometric structure in latent space. However, existing feedforward latent scene decoders typically output volumetric 3D Gaussians that lack a well-defined surface, limiting their use in simulation or standard graphics pipelines. This motivates decoding surface-aligned primitives that are not only renderable but also closer to explicit geometric assets. We ask whether compressed video diffusion latents can be mapped directly to explicit surface primitives in a single pass. To this end, we introduce FLAT and, for the first time, show that triangle splats can be decoded directly from video diffusion latents. Compared with decoding 3D Gaussians, predicting flat primitives is notoriously more challenging due to high sensitivity to primitive orientations, oftentimes leading to poor gradient flow. FLAT solves with two key ingredients: a ray-centered rotation parameterization for triangle regression and a novel product window function that improves gradient flow during differentiable triangle rendering. On standard benchmarks, FLAT achieves significantly better geometric accuracy while maintaining competitive visual quality compared to state-of-the-art feedforward baselines. We further show that a lightweight test-time refinement step converts the predicted triangle soup into a fully opaque, game-engine-ready representation that supports real-time rendering. By evaluating 3DGS, 2DGS, and triangle splatting variants under an identical training setup, we provide the first systematic analysis of representation tradeoffs in feedforward scene generation. The project page is available at https://flat-splat.github.io

AI.tldr: FLAT introduces a method to decode triangle splats directly from video diffusion latents for single-image 3D scene generation, achieving better geometric accuracy than 3D Gaussians while maintaining competitive visual quality, using a ray-centered rotation parameterization and novel product window function for improved gradient flow.
AI.motivation: Existing feedforward latent scene decoders output volumetric 3D Gaussians that lack well-defined surfaces, limiting their use in simulation and standard graphics pipelines. The authors aim to decode surface-aligned primitives (triangle splats) directly from compressed video diffusion latents in a single pass.
AI.method: FLAT uses (1) a ray-centered rotation parameterization for triangle regression and (2) a novel product window function that improves gradient flow during differentiable triangle rendering, enabling direct decoding of triangle splats from video diffusion latents.
AI.result: FLAT achieves significantly better geometric accuracy than state-of-the-art feedforward baselines while maintaining competitive visual quality. A test-time refinement step converts predicted triangle soup into game-engine-ready representation supporting real-time rendering.
AI.conclusion: The paper provides the first systematic analysis of representation tradeoffs (3DGS, 2DGS, triangle splatting) under identical training setup, demonstrating that triangle splats decoded from video diffusion latents are a viable surface-aligned primitive for feedforward scene generation.

title: BenchX: Benchmarking AI Models for Cancer Detection and Localization with Demographic and Protocol Biases
summary: Artificial intelligence (AI) has achieved remarkable success in medical imaging, but it is widely recognized that these models often perform inconsistently across real-world clinical settings. Such inconsistencies occur when patient demographics and imaging protocols vary, for example, in detecting small tumors, analyzing scans from different contrast phases, or evaluating patients of different ages or sexes. To quantify these inconsistencies, we develop a large-scale, open benchmark of 85,355 CT scans that systematically evaluates 12 tumor-detection AI models across tumor size, location, patient subgroup, and imaging protocol. We leverage large language models (LLMs) to extract and organize subgroup information from clinical data, which makes the analysis both scalable and reproducible. Our benchmark reveals that current state-of-the-art AI models, optimized for average accuracy, perform poorly in rare or underrepresented subgroups, such as young, female African Americans. However, collecting sufficient annotated data for these rare cases is often impractical. The benchmark provides a foundation for building more reliable and robust AI models for tumor detection and highlighting the need for rigorous, subgroup-level evaluation in medical imaging and computer vision. Datasets, code

AI.tldr: This paper develops a large-scale open benchmark of 85,355 CT scans to evaluate 12 tumor-detection AI models across different tumor sizes, locations, patient subgroups, and imaging protocols, finding that models optimized for average accuracy perform poorly in rare or underrepresented subgroups (e.g., young, female African Americans).
AI.motivation: AI models for medical imaging often perform inconsistently across real-world clinical settings due to variations in patient demographics and imaging protocols, and these inconsistencies need to be systematically quantified.
AI.method: The authors built a benchmark of 85,355 CT scans evaluating 12 tumor-detection AI models across tumor size, location, patient subgroup, and imaging protocol, using LLMs to extract and organize subgroup information from clinical data.
AI.result: Current state-of-the-art AI models optimized for average accuracy perform poorly in rare or underrepresented subgroups such as young, female African Americans.
AI.conclusion: The benchmark provides a foundation for building more reliable AI models for tumor detection and highlights the need for rigorous subgroup-level evaluation in medical imaging and computer vision.

title: DiffusionBench: On Holistic Evaluation of Diffusion Transformers
summary: Diffusion transformer (DiT) research on image generation has converged to a single evaluation setup: class-conditional generation on ImageNet. While methods improve the FID and related metrics, it is increasingly unclear whether they reflect real progress in generative modeling. The natural alternative, i.e., text-to-image (T2I) generation, is perceived as too costly or inconvenient to train and evaluate and is often skipped. We argue that this perception no longer holds. We introduce NanoGen, a unified DiT training and evaluation framework. NanoGen matches state-of-the-art DiT baselines on ImageNet and, with 12 lines of configuration change, also trains competitive text-to-image models. It currently supports RAE, VAE, pixel-space, and MeanFlow diffusion methods under both ImageNet and T2I setups. Under NanoGen, training T2I requires comparable compute to ImageNet. After training 21 latent diffusion models with NanoGen, we observe that method ranking shows no strong correlation between ImageNet and T2I generation: Pearson correlation is between -0.377 and -0.580 across three metrics. This suggests that a method which improves class-conditional ImageNet FID may show no corresponding improvement on T2I, clearly indicating the necessity of evaluating DiTs on both tasks. To this end, we summarize ImageNet and text-to-image results, which yields DiffusionBench, a holistic benchmark for DiT research. We recommend reporting DiffusionBench in place of ImageNet alone: methods that improve DiffusionBench are more likely to reflect broader progress.

AI.tldr: NanoGen is a unified DiT training/evaluation framework supporting both ImageNet and T2I generation. It shows that method rankings between the two tasks have weak to negative correlation (-0.377 to -0.580), proving ImageNet-only evaluation is insufficient. The authors propose DiffusionBench as a more holistic benchmark.
AI.motivation: Current DiT research relies almost exclusively on ImageNet class-conditional generation for evaluation, but it's unclear if improvements there translate to real progress in text-to-image generation. The authors argue T2I evaluation is now feasible and necessary.
AI.method: NanoGen framework supports RAE, VAE, pixel-space, and MeanFlow diffusion under both ImageNet and T2I setups with minimal configuration changes (12 lines). 21 latent diffusion models were trained under this unified framework.
AI.result: Pearson correlation between ImageNet and T2I method rankings ranges from -0.377 to -0.580 across three metrics, showing weak/negative correlation. A method improving ImageNet FID may not improve (or may even hurt) T2I performance.
AI.conclusion: The paper recommends replacing ImageNet-only evaluation with DiffusionBench (combining ImageNet and T2I results) for more meaningful progress assessment in DiT research.
